8. **Examples** — caramel popcorn, honey-roasted peanuts, jaggery-coated puffs (0.5 mark).

**(b) Short Notes (7 marks):**

**(i) Drum Tumbler Design Parameters (3.5 marks):**
- **Drum diameter and length** — control batch volume and tumbling trajectory.
- **Rotational speed (rpm)** — too low = inadequate mixing; too high = centrifugal sticking (8 – 25 rpm typical).
- **Tilt angle (θ)** — 1 – 5° controls residence time and discharge rate.
- **Internal baffles / flights** — promote cascading and prevent slip-sticking.
- **Spray nozzle placement** — uniform atomization and binder distribution.

**(ii) Quality Control Parameters (3.5 marks):**
- **Coating pickup (%)** — confirms formulation reproducibility.
- **Coating yield efficiency (%)** — confirms process economics.
- **Moisture content and a_w** — must be < 4% and < 0.6.
- **Color and appearance** — visual uniformity check.
- **Texture (crispness)** — instrumental penetrometer or 3-point bend test.
- **Salt / sugar / spice distribution** — chemical assay (e.g., Mohr's titration for NaCl).
- **Microbial load** — TPC, yeast/mold count per FSSAI limits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing units** — $W_s$ must be in the same unit (kg or g) throughout the numerical. Converting carelessly is a 0.5-mark penalty.
> 2. **Forgetting to add oil + spice** in $W_{fed}$ for $\eta_c$ calculation — only spice is coating? NO, the entire adhering mass is coating. Many students miss this.
> 3. **Confusing $W_{adhered}$ with $W_c$** — $W_{adhered} = W_c - W_s$, not just $W_c$.
> 4. **Ignoring the role of binder** in written answers — binder is the **most important** concept for spiced/salted; examiners expect 1 full paragraph.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw a block diagram** in 7-mark theory questions — at least 1.5 – 2 marks are reserved for the diagram.
> 6. **Glass transition** — many students write "high Tg is good" but do not explain *why* (glassy state = crunchiness + non-stickiness). Always link T_g to texture and storage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (last-day-prep):**
> - **Coated grain snack** = Substrate + Binder + Coating layer.
> - **3 categories:** Salted (oil binder), Spiced (oil binder + masala), Sweetened (sugar/honey binder-cum-coating).
> - **Coating Pickup CP** = $(W_c - W_s)/W_s \times 100$ → range 3 – 25 % w/w.
> - **Coating Yield Efficiency $\eta_c$** = $W_{adhered}/W_{fed} \times 100$ → target 85 – 95 %.
> - **Drum residence time** = $60L / (N \pi D \tan\theta)$ seconds.
> - **Critical process parameters:** Oil temp 50 – 70 °C; drum rpm 8 – 25; moisture < 4%; a_w < 0.6.
> - **Glass transition (T_g):** For sucrose ≈ 62 °C; storage T must be **well below** T_g.
> - **Quality tests:** Pickup, yield, moisture, a_w, color, texture, microbial.
> - **Equipment:** Rotary tumbler, spray nozzle, vibratory feeder, polishing drum, enrober.
> - **Examples (memorize):** Salted popcorn, masala peanuts, caramel popcorn.
> - **Packaging:** MET-PET/PE laminate for moisture and oxygen barrier.
> - **Numerical tip:** Always show unit conversion steps explicitly; KTU awards method marks generously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology for Batter-Based and Dough-Based Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Technology for Batter-Based and Dough-Based Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Batter**: A semi-fluid mixture of flour (or starch), liquid (water/milk), and optional leavening/seasoning agents, possessing a pourable or droppable consistency that sets/gels upon thermal processing (frying or baking). It is characterized by a moisture content of **> 50% w.b.** and exhibits non-Newtonian pseudoplastic rheology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dough**: A cohesive, viscoelastic, non-pourable mass formed by hydration of flour, where the moisture content is **< 50% w.b.**, yielding a plastic mass that can be sheeted, molded, or extruded. It exhibits both viscous flow and elastic recovery (viscoelasticity).

### Classification (As per KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus)

| System | Class | Examples | Typical Hydration (% flour basis) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batter** | Pour batter | Crepe, tempura, dosa | 110 – 150 % |
| **Batter** | Drop batter | Pakora, onion bhaji, waffle | 80 – 110 % |
| **Dough** | Hard dough | Bread, pretzel, pasta | 50 – 60 % |
| **Dough** | Soft dough | Biscuit, cookie, cracker | 25 – 40 % |
| **Dough** | Fermented dough | Naan, kulcha, pizza base | 55 – 65 % |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:** Imagine making a *milkshake* versus kneading *chapati* (flatbread) at home.
>
> - The **milkshake-like mixture** (free-flowing, can be poured from a spoon) is a **batter** — that is what you dip a vegetable slice into before deep frying pakoras.
> - The **chapati-like stiff ball** (you can roll it with a rolling pin, it holds its shape) is a **dough** — that is what you shape into a biscuit before baking.
>
> The key difference is **how much water you add relative to flour**. More water = pourable batter. Less water = pliable dough. This single ratio decides the entire processing technology, equipment, and final snack texture.

### Why this distinction matters in industry
- **Batters** coat and adhere to substrates → products are typically **fried** (pakora, tempura, corn dog).
- **Doughs** retain their own shape → products are typically **baked, sheeted, or extruded** (biscuit, bread, pasta, fried pellets like murukku).
- The **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** and **starch gelatinization temperature** dictate the final crispness and oil uptake in fried snacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Reference Values:**
> - Specific gravity of typical pour batter: **1.05 – 1.20**
> - Moisture content of fresh dough: **28 – 45% w.b.**
> - Mixing tolerance index (farinograph): **30 – 80 BU**
> - Optimum dough temperature: **26 – 30 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Role of Each Ingredient in Batter/Dough Systems

### 2.1.1 Role of Flour (The Structural Backbone)

When flour meets water, two proteins — **glutenin** and **gliadin** — hydrate and cross-link to form a **gluten network**.

$$\text{Glutenin (elastic)} + \text{Gliadin (viscous)} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, mixing}} \text{Gluten Network (viscoelastic)}$$

- **In Batters**: Limited gluten development is desired → low mechanical mixing, addition of cake flour (8–9% protein) or chemical gluten inhibitors.
- **In Doughs**: Maximum gluten development is required → strong bread flour (11–13% protein), intense kneading.

### 2.1.2 Role of Other Ingredients

| Ingredient | Function in Batter | Function in Dough |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | Hydrates starch/protein; controls viscosity | Hydrates gluten; controls consistency |
| **Salt** | Flavor, strengthens gluten network | Tightens gluten, controls yeast |
| **Sugar** | Browning (Maillard), flavor, tenderness | Fermentation substrate, tenderizing (denatures gluten) |
| **Fat/Oil** | Tenderizing, crispness, mouthfeel | Shortens gluten strands → "shortening effect" |
| **Leavening** | CO₂ generation for porosity | CO₂ generation for porosity |
| **Egg** | Emulsifier, structure, color (Maillard) | Strengthening + emulsification |

---

## 2.2 Rheology of Batters (Why the Texture Feels the Way It Does)

Batter is a **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** fluid. Its viscosity decreases as shear rate increases — that is why it pours easily but clings thickly to a vegetable when dipped.

The most accurate single-parameter model used in industry:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\tau_0$ = yield stress (Pa)
- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $n$ = flow behaviour index (n < 1 for pseudoplastic)

For a typical pakora batter: $\tau_0 \approx 15\text{–}40$ Pa, $K \approx 5\text{–}20$ Pa·sⁿ, $n \approx 0.4\text{–}0.6$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** By tuning $\tau_0$ and $K$, a food engineer can predict **pick-up weight** (how much batter clings to a substrate) — critical for uniform coating in industrial continuous fryers.

---

## 2.3 Dough Rheology — Viscoelasticity

A dough behaves like the **Maxwell model** under small deformations — it is partly elastic (spring) and partly viscous (dashpot).

$$G(t) = G_0 \cdot e^{-t/\lambda}$$

Where:
- $G(t)$ = relaxation modulus at time $t$
- $G_0$ = instantaneous elastic modulus (Pa)
- $\lambda$ = relaxation time (s)

A high $G_0$ and high $\lambda$ → **strong, elastic dough** (bread).
A low $G_0$ and short $\lambda$ → **weak, extensible dough** (biscuit, cookie).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exam)

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}\times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % |
| 2 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s+m_w}\times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % |
| 3 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{batter}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity of batter | — |
| 4 | $WA = \dfrac{V_{water}}{m_{flour}} \times 100$ | Water absorption (farinograph) | % |
| 5 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity | Pa·s |
| 6 | $V_{CO_2} = n \cdot RT/P$ | CO₂ volume from yeast (ideal gas) | m³ |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2 \cdot v$ | Extruder throughput | m³/s |
| 8 | $\phi = \dfrac{m_{pickup}}{m_{substrate}}$ | Batter pick-up ratio | — |
| 9 | $t_{gel} = \dfrac{T_{gel} - T_{mix}}{R_{heat}}$ | Time to reach starch gelatinization | s |
| 10 | $MTI = BU_{10} - BU_{peak}$ | Mixing Tolerance Index (farinograph) | BU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**
> - Batter: $50\% < M_{wb} < 85\%$, $SG$ between 1.05 – 1.20
> - Dough: $25\% < M_{wb} < 50\%$, $WA$ between 50 – 75%
> - Fry temperature for batter-coated snacks: **170 – 190 °C**
> - Baking temperature for dough-based snacks: **150 – 220 °C**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In industrial frying lines (e.g., McCain, Haldiram's):** batter rheology controls oil uptake (target < 30% w.b. in finished pakora).
- **In biscuit lines (Britannia, Parle):** farinograph water absorption and mixing tolerance are the *first specifications* checked before plant trials.
- **In extrusion-based snack (kurkure, puffed curls):** dough moisture at the extruder die governs expansion ratio and cell structure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture

This is the most frequently asked 3-mark derivation in KTU 2024 food technology papers.

**Given:** A batter sample has wet-basis moisture $M_{wb} = 60\%$.

**To Derive:** The dry-basis equivalent $M_{db}$.

**Step 1 — Define both terms on 100 g sample basis.**

For a 100 g sample with $M_{wb} = 60\%$:
- Mass of water: $m_w = 60$ g
- Mass of solids: $m_s = 100 - 60 = 40$ g

**Step 2 — Substitute into the $M_{db}$ definition.**

$$M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Insert numerical values.**

$$M_{db} = \dfrac{60}{40} \times 100 = 150\%$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to obtain the universal relation.**

$$\boxed{M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most important relation in food moisture calculations. KTU examiners frequently give a wet-basis value and ask for dry-basis (or vice versa).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Batter Formulation to Hit a Target Specific Gravity

**Problem:** A formulator wants 10 kg of pakora batter at $SG = 1.15$, knowing that flour ($SG = 1.30$) is the main solid. Density of water = 1000 kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert SG to actual density.**

$$\rho_{batter} = SG \times \rho_{water} = 1.15 \times 1000 = 1150 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Express mass and volume in terms of flour mass $m_f$ and water mass $m_w$.**

Let the recipe use only flour + water for simplicity (additives < 2%):
- Total mass: $m_{total} = m_f + m_w = 10$ kg
- Total volume: $V_{total} = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{m_w}{\rho_w}$

**Step 3 — Set up the volume equation.**

$$V_{total} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{\rho_{batter}} = \dfrac{10}{1150} = 8.696 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\dfrac{m_f}{1300} + \dfrac{m_w}{1000} = 8.696 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Use the mass balance $m_w = 10 - m_f$ to eliminate one variable.**

$$\dfrac{m_f}{1300} + \dfrac{10 - m_f}{1000} = 8.696 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by 1300 × 1000 = 1,300,000 to clear denominators.**

$$1000 \cdot m_f + 1300(10 - m_f) = 1300000 \times 8.696 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$1000 m_f + 13000 - 1300 m_f = 11304.8$$

$$-300 m_f = 13000 - 11304.8 = 1695.2$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $m_f$.**

$$m_f = -\dfrac{1695.2}{300} = -5.65 \text{ kg}$$

**Result — *negative mass* means our assumption is wrong.** This indicates the recipe must include spices, salt, leavening, and oil which raise effective density. In practice, batter $SG$ is empirically tuned by adding ice, milk powder, or thickeners (guar gum, xanthan).

**Practical recipe (typical Indian pakora batter):**
- Besan (chickpea flour): **5.5 kg** (SG ≈ 1.30)
- Water: **4.2 kg**
- Salt + spices: **0.2 kg**
- Oil: **0.1 kg**
→ Achieves $SG \approx 1.15$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Water Absorption (Farinograph) of a Flour

The farinograph is the gold-standard instrument for characterizing flour. It measures the torque required to mix flour + water until the dough reaches a target consistency of **500 Brabender Units (BU)**.

**Step 1 — Farinograph procedure.** Add water in fixed doses to 300 g flour, plot torque vs time.

**Step 2 — Read peak time (DT), peak value, and stability.**

**Step 3 — Water absorption is reported as:**

$$WA (\%) = \dfrac{(V_{water} \times \rho_{water})}{m_{flour}} \times 100$$

For a typical Indian atta (whole wheat flour), $WA = 62\%$.
For US bread flour, $WA = 63 - 67\%$.
For biscuit flour, $WA = 50 - 55\%$.

**Step 4 — Mixing Tolerance Index (MTI) derivation:**

$$MTI = BU_{10\text{ min after peak}} - BU_{peak}$$

A **high MTI → weak flour** (rapid breakdown of gluten, dough slackens).
A **low MTI → strong flour** (stable over long mixing).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Flow Steps for a Dough-Based Snack (Biscuit)

| Step | Unit Operation | Parameter Controlled | Target Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flour sifting | Particle size | < 200 µm |
| 2 | Creaming (fat + sugar) | Air incorporation | 12 – 15% v/v |
| 3 | Addition of liquid (water/milk) | Hydration | 25 – 35% w.b. final |
| 4 | Dough mixing | Gluten development | Minimal (short dough) |
| 5 | Resting/lamination | Gluten relaxation | 10 – 30 min |
| 6 | Sheeting/forming | Thickness | 2 – 5 mm |
| 7 | Baking | Maillard + starch gel | 180 – 200 °C, 8 – 12 min |
| 8 | Cooling | Moisture equilibration | < 4% w.b. final |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU pitfall: Students often confuse **batter mixing** (gentle, low energy, no gluten) with **dough mixing** (intense, high energy, full gluten). Marks are lost if this distinction is not explicit in the answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Process Flow for a Batter-Based Snack (e.g., Pakora, Onion Bhaji)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Reception: Besan, Rice Flour, Spices] --> B[Sifting and Weighing]
    B --> C[Dry Blending: Flour + Salt + Spice Mix]
    C --> D[Addition of Cold Water with Gentle Stirring]
    D --> E[Resting: 15 to 30 minutes at 4 to 8 degC]
    E --> F[Viscosity Check: Target 1500 to 3000 cP]
    F --> G{Density Adjustment}
    G --> H[Add Ice Slurry to Increase Hydration]
    G --> I[Add Dry Flour to Decrease Hydration]
    H --> J[Batter Holding Tank with Agitation]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Substrate Preparation: Slice Vegetables or Protein Pieces]
    K --> L[Coating Station: Dipping or Spray]
    L --> M[Pre-Fryer Dewatering Belt]
    M --> N[Continuous Deep Fat Fryer at 175 to 185 degC]
    N --> O[Post-Fry De-oiling Conveyor]
    O --> P[Flavoring Drum with Masala Coating]
    P --> Q[Cooling Tunnel to 30 degC]
    Q --> R[Packaging: Nitrogen Flush in Metallized Pouch]
    R --> S[Finished Snack Ready for Distribution]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Generic Process Flow for a Dough-Based Snack (e.g., Biscuit, Cracker)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flour Silos and Bulk Storage] --> B[Pneumatic Conveying to Mixer]
    B --> C[Weighing and Dosing Station]
    C --> D[Horizontal Z-Arm Dough Mixer]
    D --> E[Step 1: Creaming of Fat and Sugar 5 to 8 min]
    E --> F[Step 2: Addition of Liquid Ingredients]
    F --> G[Step 3: Final Flour Incorporation]
    G --> H[Dough Resting Hopper: 15 to 45 min]
    H --> I[Sheeter and Laminator]
    I --> J[Rotary Moulder or Wire Cut Cutter]
    J --> K[Baking Oven Three Zones 180 to 220 degC]
    K --> L[Oven Out Conveyor with Rotary Cooler]
    L --> M[Post-Oven Humidity Equalization 24 to 48 h]
    M --> N[Inline Inspection: Metal Detector and X-Ray]
    N --> O[Packaging Line with Twist Wrap or Flow Wrap]
    O --> P[Cartoning and Case Packing]
    P --> Q[Finished Product Warehouse]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Fermented Dough-Based Snack (Idli/Dosa — A Hybrid Batter-Dough System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stage 1 Cleaning and Soaking]
        A1[Rice: 3 parts] --> A2[Soak 4 to 6 h]
        A3[Black Gram Dal: 1 part] --> A4[Soak 4 to 6 h]
    end
    subgraph S2[Stage 2 Wet Grinding]
        A2 --> B1[Grind to Smooth Batter 250 micron]
        A4 --> B2[Grind to Fluffy Paste 80 micron]
        B1 --> C1[Combine and Mix]
        B2 --> C1
    end
    subgraph S3[Stage 3 Fermentation]
        C1 --> D1[Add Salt 1 percent]
        D1 --> D2[Ferment 8 to 12 h at 25 to 30 degC]
        D2 --> D3{CO2 Evolution by Leuconostoc}
    end
    subgraph S4[Stage 4 Cooking]
        D3 --> E1[Idli Steaming 8 to 10 min]
        D3 --> E2[Dosa Troweling on Hot Plate 200 degC]
    end
    E1 --> F1[Finished Soft Spongy Snack]
    E2 --> F2[Finished Crisp Lacy Snack]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Batter vs Dough Processing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Target Snack Formulation] --> Q1{What is the target water to flour ratio?}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 0.8| PourBat[Pour Batter System: Tempura, Crepe]
    Q1 -->|Between 0.5 and 0.8| DropBat[Drop Batter System: Pakora, Waffle]
    Q1 -->|Between 0.25 and 0.5| SoftDough[Soft Dough System: Biscuit, Cookie]
    Q1 -->|Less than 0.25| HardDough[Hard Dough System: Pasta, Pretzel]
    PourBat --> Cook1[Thermal Process: Frying or Pan Cooking]
    DropBat --> Cook1
    SoftDough --> Cook2[Thermal Process: Baking at 180 to 220 degC]
    HardDough --> Cook3[Thermal Process: Baking or High Temp Frying]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define batter. Differentiate between pour batter and drop batter with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Batter** is a semi-fluid mixture of flour, water, and other ingredients with a pourable or droppable consistency, generally containing **> 50% moisture (w.b.)**.
>
> - **Pour Batter**: Has a thin, free-flowing consistency; water-to-flour ratio is high (**110–150%**). Examples: *tempura batter* and *crêpe batter*. They are usually poured onto a hot surface.
> - **Drop Batter**: Has a thicker, spoonable consistency; water-to-flour ratio is moderate (**80–110%**). Examples: *pakora batter* and *waffle batter*. They are dropped by spoonful onto a hot surface or used for coating.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List the four main functional roles of fat in a biscuit dough.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Tenderizing / Shortening**: Fat coats flour particles and prevents excessive gluten development, yielding a tender, crumbly texture (1 mark).
2. **Air incorporation (creaming)**: During creaming, fat traps air cells which expand during baking, providing leavening and a flaky layered structure (1 mark).
3. **Flavor and mouthfeel**: Fat carries fat-soluble flavor compounds and provides lubricity in the mouth (0.5 mark).
4. **Heat transfer**: Fat contributes to uniform heat distribution during baking, aiding in Maillard browning (0.5 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model – Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** Describe the **raw materials, formulation, and processing steps** for the industrial manufacture of **onion pakora** (a batter-based snack). Highlight the rheological role of chickpea flour (besan) and the importance of cold water in the batter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A formulator wants to produce **500 kg of pakora batter** with a final moisture content of **62% (w.b.)** using **besan** (8% moisture) and **water**. Calculate the quantity of besan and water required. Also compute the equivalent dry-basis moisture of the final batter. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Raw materials** | Besan (chickpea flour), water, sliced onions, green chili, salt, turmeric, red chili powder, ajwain, optional baking soda. | 1 |
| 2. **Formulation (typical industrial recipe on 100 kg finished batter)** | Besan 35 kg, water 60 kg, salt 1.5 kg, spices 1.5 kg, leavening 0.1 kg, oil 1.9 kg. | 1.5 |
| 3. **Role of besan** | Besan has **high protein (~22%)** with no gluten but excellent **water absorption (≈120%)** and emulsifying capacity. Its proteins form a **viscoelastic film** around onion slices upon frying. | 1.5 |
| 4. **Rheological role** | Besan + water gives a **pseudoplastic** batter. Viscosity must be tuned to **1500–3000 cP** for proper pick-up (target pick-up ratio $\phi \approx 0.25\text{–}0.40$). | 1 |
| 5. **Role of cold water (4–8 °C)** | Cold water **delays gluten-like protein hydration** and **prevents enzymatic darkening** of besan pigments; also reduces oil absorption by limiting starch pre-gelatinization. | 1 |
| 6. **Processing flow** | Dry blend → Add cold water with gentle stirring → Rest 15–30 min → Dip onion slices → Fry at 175–185 °C for 90–120 s → De-oil → Cool → Pack. | 1 |

**(b) — 7 marks (Mass Balance Calculation)**

**Given:** Total batter = 500 kg, $M_{wb}^{final} = 62\%$, besan initial $M_{wb} = 8\%$.

**Step 1 — Define unknowns.**
Let $m_b$ = mass of besan (as received), $m_w$ = mass of water added.
Total: $m_b + m_w = 500$ kg ... (i)

**Step 2 — Express final moisture (w.b.).**

$$M_{wb}^{final} = \dfrac{\text{total water}}{\text{total mass}} = \dfrac{0.08 m_b + m_w}{500} = 0.62$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system.**

$$0.08 m_b + m_w = 0.62 \times 500 = 310 \text{ kg} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Subtract (ii) from (i):
$$m_b - 0.08 m_b = 500 - 310$$
$$0.92 m_b = 190$$
$$m_b = \dfrac{190}{0.92} = 206.52 \text{ kg}$$

From (i): $m_w = 500 - 206.52 = 293.48$ kg.

**Step 4 — Compute dry-basis moisture.**

$$M_{db} = \dfrac{62}{100 - 62} \times 100 = \dfrac{62}{38} \times 100 = 163.16\%$$

> **Final Answer:** Besan = **206.5 kg**, Water = **293.5 kg**, $M_{db}$ = **163.16%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model – Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **manufacturing technology of biscuits** with a focus on the role of each ingredient (flour, fat, sugar, leavening, water). Describe the **creaming process** and how it differs from bread-dough mixing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **water absorption** of a given wheat flour on the farinograph is found to be **64%**. Calculate the amount of water required to mix with **250 kg of flour** to make a dough. If **5 kg of salt, 2 kg of baking powder, and 3 kg of milk solids** are added (all dry), what is the **final water-to-flour ratio in the dough**? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

| Sub-topic | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Role of flour** | Soft wheat flour (8–9% protein) preferred to limit gluten → tender, short bite. | 1 |
| 2. **Role of fat** | Shortening of gluten strands; creaming traps air cells for leavening. | 1 |
| 3. **Role of sugar** | Sweetness, browning (Maillard at 140–165 °C), and tenderizing (competes with flour for water, delaying gluten hydration). | 1 |
| 4. **Role of leavening** | Baking powder (sodium bicarbonate + acidulant) generates CO₂ during baking → porous, light texture. | 1 |
| 5. **Role of water** | Hydrates starch and protein; controls dough consistency (target 25–35% moisture). | 1 |
| 6. **Creaming process** | Fat and sugar are whipped together at high speed → air cells (10–15% v/v) become the *nucleation sites* for steam and CO₂ expansion during baking. | 1 |
| 7. **Difference from bread mixing** | Bread: high-speed, full gluten development, long mixing (10–20 min). Biscuit: low-speed, *minimal* gluten, short mixing (3–5 min) to keep dough "short". | 1 |

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Water absorption definition.**

$$WA = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{flour}} \times 100 = 64\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate water needed for 250 kg flour.**

$$m_{water} = 0.64 \times 250 = 160 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Account for water absorbed by other dry ingredients.**
Baking powder, salt, and milk solids also consume water. Assume they absorb an additional **1.5 kg** of process water (typical industry estimate). So total added water = **161.5 kg**.

**Step 4 — Final dough composition.**
- Flour: 250 kg
- Water: 161.5 kg
- Salt: 5 kg
- Baking powder: 2 kg
- Milk solids: 3 kg
- **Total dough: 421.5 kg**

**Step 5 — Final water-to-flour ratio.**

$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{flour}} = \dfrac{161.5}{250} = 0.646 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 64.6\%$$

> **Final Answer:** Water required = **160 kg** (excluding ingredient absorption), final W:F ratio = **0.646** (or **64.6%**).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Compiled from Past KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing batter with dough**: A *batter* is pourable (>50% moisture); a *dough* is kneadable (<50% moisture). Examiners *specifically deduct 1 mark* for failing to state the moisture threshold.
> 2. **Skipping the unit** in numerical answers: Always write kg, %, °C, cP, etc. A correct number without a unit = -0.5 mark.
> 3. **Using absolute value bars `|x|` inside markdown tables**: This breaks the table and confuses the renderer. Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention starch gelatinization temperature (60–80 °C)**: Any fried-batter question requires this. Examiners give 1 mark for stating it explicitly.
> 5. **In mass-balance problems, omitting the "added solids carry their own moisture" condition**: Besan at 8% moisture contributes 8% of its mass as water. Ignoring this = -2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing "creaming" with "mixing"**: Creaming is a *specific unit operation* of whipping fat + sugar to incorporate air. It is *not* the same as combining all ingredients.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Technology for Batter-Based and Dough-Based Products)**

- [x] **Batter** = pourable / droppable, **> 50% moisture (w.b.)**; **Dough** = kneadable, **< 50% moisture (w.b.)**.
- [x] **Gluten** = glutenin (elastic) + gliadin (viscous); formed only upon hydration + mechanical energy.
- [x] **Besan (chickpea flour)** has **no gluten** but high protein → forms a viscoelastic film ideal for batter-fried snacks.
- [x] **Cake flour** (8–9% protein) → low gluten → for biscuits/cookies. **Bread flour** (11–13% protein) → high gluten → for bread.
- [x] **Batter rheology** is pseudoplastic: $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$, with $n < 1$.
- [x] **Dough rheology** is viscoelastic, modeled by **Maxwell** (spring + dashpot) or **Kelvin–Voigt** elements.
- [x] **Key parameters**: Specific gravity of batter (1.05–1.20), Water absorption of dough (50–75%), Mixing tolerance index (MTI, BU).
- [x] **Frying temperature for batters**: 170–190 °C. **Baking temperature for dough snacks**: 150–220 °C.
- [x] **Starch gelatinization range**: 60–80 °C (critical for batter set and crispness).
- [x] **Master conversion**: $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$.
- [x] **Biscuit dough mixing** = *short* (low energy, minimal gluten). **Bread dough mixing** = *developed* (high energy, full gluten).
- [x] **Cold water in batters** (4–8 °C): delays hydration, prevents enzymatic browning, reduces oil uptake.
- [x] **Creaming** in biscuits: traps 10–15% v/v air → nucleation sites for CO₂/steam expansion in the oven.
- [x] **Industrial equipment**: Z-arm dough mixer, rotary moulder, wire-cut cutter, three-zone travelling oven, rotary cooling conveyor.
- [x] **Yeast fermentation** in dough: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2 C_2H_5OH + 2 CO_2$; CO₂ inflates gluten network.
- [x] **Laminates** in doughs (croissant, puff) require fat-layer alternation and rest to prevent fat smearing.
- [x] **Extrusion-based doughs** (pasta, snacks): moisture 25–35% in the extruder, die temperature 120–180 °C, expansion ratio 4–8.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology for Formulated Products - Chips, Wafers, Papads, Instant Pre-Mixes
Horticulture Produce-Based Snacks - Technology for Fruit-Based Snacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Horticulture Produce-Based Snacks - Technology for Fruit-Based Snacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *Fruit-based snacks* are ready-to-eat, shelf-stable food products manufactured predominantly from fresh or processed horticultural fruits, which are preserved primarily through controlled moisture reduction (drying, frying, or concentration) and/or sugar-acid matrices (candying, leathers, bars), yielding a product with low water activity ($a_w \le 0.60$), extended microbiological stability, and characteristic fruit flavor, color, and texture.

According to the **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and the **Codex Alimentarius**, fruit-based snacks occupy the regulatory intersection of *Processed Fruits and Vegetables* (Codex CXS 79-2013) and *Snack Foods*. They are classified as **Horticultural Produce-Based Snacks** because the principal structural matrix (the *biopolymer scaffold* consisting of pectin, cellulose, hemicellulose, and starch) originates from intact or reconstituted fruit tissue.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Fruit-Based Snacks

| Class | Typical Example | Key Process | $a_w$ Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dried fruit chips** | Banana, apple, jackfruit chips | Vacuum drying / hot air drying | 0.20 – 0.45 |
| **Vacuum-fried chips** | Mango, pineapple, apple | Vacuum frying ($< 100^{\circ}\text{C}$) | 0.10 – 0.30 |
| **Fruit leathers / bars** | Mango leather, mixed fruit bar | Dehydrated fruit purée sheet | 0.55 – 0.65 |
| **Candied / osmo-dried fruits** | Tutti frutti, candied papaya | Osmotic dehydration + sugar infusion | 0.65 – 0.75 |
| **Fruit powders** | Spray-dried amla, mango powder | Spray/freeze drying of purée | 0.10 – 0.25 |
| **Extruded fruit puffs** | Fortified corn-fruit blends | Hot-extrusion cooking | 0.10 – 0.30 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a fruit as a *water balloon* — its juicy interior is held inside a delicate skin. A fruit snack is essentially a *carefully dehydrated* version of that balloon, where ~80–95% of the water is gently removed so that the flavor concentrates like a *symphony orchestra tuning into a single melody* — sweet, tangy, and intensely fruity. The water is the "dilution" of the orchestra; remove it and you hear the pure music of natural sugars, acids, and aromatics.

The "Why" behind fruit snacks is the **metabolic clock** of fresh fruit — seasonal, perishable, and vulnerable to enzymatic browning (catalyzed by **polyphenol oxidase, PPO**, EC 1.10.3.1) and microbial spoilage. By controlling moisture, water activity, and packaging atmosphere, manufacturers extend shelf life from **5–10 days (fresh fruit)** to **6–12 months (snack form)**.

## 1.3 Critical Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Values for Commercial Fruit Snacks (FSSAI + Codex):**
> - **Final moisture content:** $\le 6\% \text{ (w.b.)}$ for dried chips and powders.
> - **Water activity ($a_w$):** $\le 0.60$ (microbial safety threshold; below this, *Staphylococcus aureus* and most yeasts are inhibited).
> - **Sugar content (Brix):** $65^{\circ}\text{–}85^{\circ}\text{Bx}$ for leathers and candied fruits.
> - **Color:** Hunter $L^*$ value preserved within **$\pm 5$ units** of fresh sample to denote low browning.
> - **Acidity (titratable):** $0.5\% \text{–} 1.5\%$ as citric acid equivalent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Universal Process Flow

Every fruit-based snack, whether banana chip or amla candy, passes through a **six-stage engineering pipeline**. Understanding *why* each stage exists is the key to scoring on KTU application questions.

### Stage 1 — Raw Material Selection & Grading
- **Why:** Fruits exhibit wide compositional variance (Brix: 8–22°Bx; acidity: 0.3–2.5%). Grading by Brix and firmness ensures batch uniformity.
- **Key parameter:** Flesh-to-peel ratio, **Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** measured with a **refractometer** in **°Brix**.

### Stage 2 — Pre-treatment (Critical Control Point)
Pretreatments are the "knob" that determines color, texture, and drying rate.

- **Blanching** at $80^{\circ}\text{C}–100^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $1\text{–}5$ min → inactivates **PPO enzyme** (polyphenol oxidase) and **POD (peroxidase)**.
- **Sulphiting** (KMS, $\text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_5$) at $500\text{–}2000 \text{ ppm}$ → prevents enzymatic browning and microbial growth.
- **Citric acid dip** (0.5%–2%) → chelates $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ cofactor of PPO, lowers surface pH below 4.0.
- **Osmotic pre-dehydration** in $60^{\circ}\text{–}70^{\circ}\text{Bx}$ sugar syrup → partially removes water, improves texture.

### Stage 3 — Size Reduction / Shaping
- Slicing (typically $2\text{–}5 \text{ mm}$ thickness for chips).
- Pureeing for leather and powder.
- Why thin? Higher **surface-area-to-volume ratio** accelerates moisture transfer.

### Stage 4 — Moisture Reduction (Core Process)
This stage *defines* the product class:
- **Hot air drying** (tray/cabinet): $50^{\circ}\text{C}–80^{\circ}\text{C}$, air velocity $1\text{–}3 \text{ m/s}$.
- **Vacuum drying:** $30^{\circ}\text{C}–60^{\circ}\text{C}$, pressure $\le 4.6 \text{ kPa}$ — preserves heat-labile vitamin C.
- **Freeze drying (lyophilization):** $-30^{\circ}\text{C} \to 30^{\circ}\text{C}$, pressure $\le 100 \text{ Pa}$ — best color/structure, expensive.
- **Vacuum frying:** $80^{\circ}\text{C}–100^{\circ}\text{C}$, absolute pressure $\le 5 \text{ kPa}$ — lowers oil absorption to **$< 30\%$** vs. **$40\text{–}50\%$** in atmospheric frying.
- **Spray drying:** inlet $150^{\circ}\text{C}–200^{\circ}\text{C}$, outlet $60^{\circ}\text{C}–90^{\circ}\text{C}$ for fruit powders.

### Stage 5 — Post-treatment
- **Sugar glazing** (candied fruits): immersion in $70^{\circ}\text{Bx}$ syrup then drying.
- **Seasoning** (savory chips): $1\text{–}3\%$ salt/spice tumble.
- **Cooling/equilibration** to ambient $a_w$.

### Stage 6 — Packaging
- **Moisture-barrier films:** metallized PET, aluminum foil laminates, **WVTR** (Water Vapor Transmission Rate) $\le 1 \text{ g/m}^2$/day.
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** $70\% \text{ N}_2 + 30\% \text{ CO}_2$.
- **Nitrogen flushing** to displace $\text{O}_2$ and prevent lipid oxidation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board-exam-verifiable** and routinely tested in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % |
| 2 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % |
| 3 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | Conversion between wet and dry basis | % |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{m_s \cdot (M_{db,i} - M_{db,f})}{t}$ | Drying rate (water removal) | kg/h |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{X_t - X_{eq}}{X_0 - X_{eq}} = e^{-kt}$ | Thin-layer drying (Page/Lewis model) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $a_w = \dfrac{P_{partial}}{P_{sat}(T)}$ | Water activity | dimensionless |
| 7 | $U_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil,\,abs}}{m_{chip}} \times 100$ | Oil uptake in fried chips | % |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = m_s \cdot (M_{db,i} - M_{db,f}) \cdot \lambda_w$ | Latent heat of evaporation in drying | kJ/h |
| 9 | $X_t = X^* + (X_0 - X^*)\,e^{-kt}$ | Henderson–Pabis drying model | kg water/kg dry solid |
| 10 | $L^*$, $a^*$, $b^*$ | Hunter L\*a\*b\* color coordinates | dimensionless |

**Where:** $m_w$ = mass of water, $m_s$ = mass of dry solid, $X$ = moisture content (dry basis), $X_{eq}$ = equilibrium moisture, $k$ = drying constant $\text{(h}^{-1}\text{)}$, $\lambda_w = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$, and $L^* \in [0, 100]$ denotes lightness.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Indian snack industry** (worth **₹30,000+ crores in 2024**), fruit-based snacks like banana chips (Kerala's signature product) and jackfruit chips represent a booming sub-segment. The **engineering significance** lies in:

1. **Shelf-life extension** of perishable horticulture produce — reduces post-harvest losses (currently $20\text{–}30\%$ in India).
2. **Value addition** — converts low-cost seasonal fruits (₹10/kg mango) into premium products (₹400–800/kg dried chips).
3. **Employment in MSMEs** — cottage-scale vacuum fryers and tray dryers are the backbone of Kerala's snack industry.
4. **Nutrient retention** — when freeze-drying is used, $>90\%$ vitamin C retention vs. $\sim 50\%$ in hot air drying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Conversion between Wet and Dry Basis Moisture

This derivation is a **Part-A favourite** and frequently worth 3 marks.

We start from the definitions:
- Wet basis: $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$
- Dry basis: $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$

**Step 1 — Express the total mass:**
$$m_w + m_s = m_{total}$$

**Step 2 — Take reciprocal of the wet basis equation:**
$$\dfrac{1}{M_{wb}/100} = \dfrac{m_w + m_s}{m_w} = 1 + \dfrac{m_s}{m_w}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $\dfrac{m_s}{m_w} = \dfrac{1}{M_{db}/100}$:**
$$\dfrac{100}{M_{wb}} = 1 + \dfrac{100}{M_{db}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $M_{db}$:**
$$\dfrac{100}{M_{db}} = \dfrac{100}{M_{wb}} - 1 = \dfrac{100 - M_{wb}}{M_{wb}}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the closed-form relation:**
$$M_{db} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}$$

This equation, often called the **"100-rule"** in food engineering, converts moisture contents between the two conventions used by KTU examiners.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Mass-Balance of a Tray Drying Operation

A KTU Board-favourite problem: *"100 kg of fresh mango slices at 85% moisture (w.b.) are dried to a final moisture of 5% (w.b.). Calculate the mass of dried product and the mass of water removed."*

**Step 1 — Express initial and final dry solid mass:**

Initial: $m_{s,i} = m_{i} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{wb,i}}{100}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.85) = 15 \text{ kg}$

Final: $m_{s,f} = m_{f} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{wb,f}}{100}\right)$

**Step 2 — Apply the conservation of dry solids (no solids are lost in drying):**
$$m_{s,i} = m_{s,f} \implies 15 = m_f \times (1 - 0.05) = 0.95 \, m_f$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the final mass:**
$$m_f = \dfrac{15}{0.95} = 15.789 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the water removed:**
$$m_{w,removed} = m_i - m_f = 100 - 15.789 = 84.211 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage of original water:**
$$\% \text{ water removed} = \dfrac{84.211}{85} \times 100 = 99.07\%$$

**Interpretation:** Drying removed $\sim 99\%$ of the available water — explaining why the product is microbiologically shelf-stable.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Energy Required for Drying (Latent Heat Method)

Given: 1000 kg of pineapple cubes dried from $M_{db,i} = 6$ to $M_{db,f} = 0.2$ (kg water/kg dry solid).

**Step 1 — Calculate dry solid mass:**
$$m_s = \dfrac{1000}{1 + M_{db,i}} = \dfrac{1000}{1 + 6} = 142.86 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Water to be removed per kg dry solid:**
$$\Delta X = 6 - 0.2 = 5.8 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3 — Total water removed:**
$$m_w = m_s \times \Delta X = 142.86 \times 5.8 = 828.57 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat at $80^{\circ}\text{C}$:** $\lambda_{w,80^{\circ}C} = 2308 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 5 — Theoretical latent heat energy:**
$$\dot{Q}_{latent} = 828.57 \times 2308 = 1{,}912{,}182 \text{ kJ} = 531.16 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 6 — Add sensible heat for the product ($c_p \approx 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\Delta T = 55 \text{ K}$):**
$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = 1000 \times 3.5 \times 55 = 192{,}500 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 7 — Total theoretical energy:**
$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 1{,}912{,}182 + 192{,}500 = 2{,}104{,}682 \text{ kJ} = 584.6 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 8 — Apply dryer efficiency ($\eta = 60\%$ for cabinet dryers):**
$$\dot{Q}_{actual} = \dfrac{584.6}{0.60} = 974.4 \text{ kWh}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Drying Methods for Fruit Snacks

| Parameter | Hot Air Drying | Vacuum Drying | Freeze Drying | Vacuum Frying |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | $50\text{–}80^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $30\text{–}60^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $-30^{\circ}\text{C} \to 30^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $80\text{–}100^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Pressure (absolute) | 101.3 kPa | $\le 4.6$ kPa | $\le 0.1$ kPa | $\le 5$ kPa |
| Vitamin C Retention | $40\text{–}60\%$ | $70\text{–}85\%$ | $> 90\%$ | $50\text{–}70\%$ |
| Color Retention ($L^*$) | Moderate | High | Very High | High (golden) |
| Texture | Hard, dense | Porous, crisp | Highly porous | Crisp, oily |
| Energy Use | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |
| Capital Cost | Low | Medium-High | Very High | High |
| Shelf Life | $6\text{–}9$ months | $9\text{–}12$ months | $12\text{–}18$ months | $6\text{–}9$ months |
| **KTU Typical Use** | Banana, jackfruit | Mango, apple | Premium berries | Banana, jackfruit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Process Flow for Vacuum-Fried Fruit Chips (e.g., Jackfruit Chips)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Jackfruit Selection] --> B[Grading and Weighing]
    B --> C[Peeling and Slicing 2 to 3 mm]
    C --> D[Pre-treatment: KMS 0.1% + Citric Acid 0.5% dip, 10 min]
    D --> E[Blanching at 90 °C for 3 min]
    E --> F[Surface Moisture Removal by Air Blower]
    F --> G[Vacuum Frying at 95 °C, 3 to 5 kPa, 15 to 25 min]
    G --> H[Centrifugal De-oiling at 600 rpm for 1 min]
    H --> I[Flavor Seasoning: Salt 1.5%, Pepper 0.3%]
    I --> J[Cooling Tunnel at 25 °C, 5 min]
    J --> K[Quality Inspection: Color L-star, Aw, Moisture]
    K --> L[MAP Packaging: N2 90% + CO2 10%]
    L --> M[Metal Detection and Weighing]
    M --> N[Carton Packing 24 x 50 g]
    N --> O[Storage at 25 °C, RH below 60%]
```
