# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

### Question 1. Define *fruit leather*. Mention two quality parameters and the typical Brix range used for its preparation. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fruit leather** is a dehydrated, chewy, sheet-like snack product made by drying a thin layer (typically $3\text{–}5 \text{ mm}$) of concentrated fruit purée containing pectin, sugar, and acid, until the moisture is reduced to **$18\text{–}22\%$ (w.b.)** and water activity falls below **0.70**.
>
> **Two quality parameters:**
> 1. **Color** (Hunter $L^* \ge 35$ for acceptable mango leather).
> 2. **Texture** — measured as **puncture force** of $1.5\text{–}3.0 \text{ N}$ using a texture analyzer.
>
> **Typical Brix range:** Initial purée standardized to **$18^{\circ}\text{–}22^{\circ}\text{Bx}$** before drying.

### Question 2. State **Fick's second law of diffusion** as applicable to moisture migration in a fruit slice during drying and define its key terms. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

$$\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 X}{\partial z^2}$$

where:
- $X$ = local moisture content (kg water/kg dry solid) — *dimensionless*,
- $t$ = drying time (s),
- $z$ = spatial coordinate through slice thickness (m),
- $D_{eff}$ = effective moisture diffusivity (m²/s) — typically $10^{-10}\text{–}10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ for fruits.

The equation states that the **time-rate of change of moisture at a point** is proportional to the **second spatial gradient** of moisture, with the proportionality constant being the effective diffusivity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

### Question A: Choice 1 (14 Marks)

#### (a) Describe, with a flow diagram, the **complete process technology for vacuum-fried banana chips**. Discuss the role of KMS, citric acid, and centrifugal de-oiling. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Raw material selection:** Choose mature, unripe bananas (Kerala *Nendran* variety, $80^{\circ}\text{Bx}$, $1.2\%$ acidity) with high pectin and low sugar to minimize stickiness and oil absorption.

**Step 2 — Peeling and slicing:** Manual peeling, mechanical slicing to $2\text{–}3 \text{ mm}$ thickness to ensure uniform drying and frying. Slice thickness is **critical**: too thick → under-fried, too thin → burnt chips.

**Step 3 — Pre-treatment dip:** The slices are immersed for **10 minutes** in a solution containing:
- **KMS (Potassium Metabisulphite, $\text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_5$) at $1000 \text{ ppm}$** — releases $\text{SO}_2$ which inhibits **PPO** (polyphenol oxidase) and non-enzymatic Maillard browning, and acts as a microbial retardant.
- **Citric acid at $0.5\%$** — chelates the $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ cofactor of PPO, lowering the surface pH to $3.5\text{–}4.0$, and acts synergistically with KMS to prevent browning.

**Step 4 — Vacuum frying:** Frying at $90^{\circ}\text{C}–100^{\circ}\text{C}$ under absolute pressure of **$3\text{–}5 \text{ kPa}$** for $15\text{–}25 \text{ min**.
- **Why vacuum?** At low pressure, water boils below $40^{\circ}\text{C}$. The combination of *low temperature + moisture vapor escape* gives a porous, less oily, golden-yellow chip.
- **Oil uptake** is reduced from $40\text{–}50\%$ (atmospheric frying) to **$< 30\%$**.

**Step 5 — Centrifugal de-oiling:** The chips are spun in a perforated basket at **$600 \text{ rpm}$** for **$60 \text{ s}$** to remove surface-bound oil, reducing the final oil content to **$25\text{–}28\%$** (from $35\%$ without centrifugation).

**Step 6 — Seasoning and packaging:** Salt (1.5%) + pepper (0.3%) tumble, then packed in **nitrogen-flushed metallized PET pouches** (WVTR $\le 1 \text{ g/m}^2$/day) for $6\text{–}9$ months shelf life.

#### (b) A fruit snack manufacturer wants to dry **500 kg of fresh pineapple slices** from **85% moisture (w.b.)** to a safe moisture of **5% (w.b.)** in a cabinet dryer. Calculate: (i) the final mass of dried product, (ii) the mass of water removed, and (iii) the theoretical energy required for drying (use $\lambda_w = 2308 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at $80^{\circ}\text{C}$). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Final mass of dried product:**

Initial dry solid mass:
$$m_s = 500 \times (1 - 0.85) = 500 \times 0.15 = 75 \text{ kg}$$

Final mass at $5\%$ (w.b.):
$$m_f \times (1 - 0.05) = 75 \implies m_f = \dfrac{75}{0.95} = 78.95 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Mass of water removed:**
$$m_w = 500 - 78.95 = 421.05 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Theoretical energy required:**
$$\dot{Q} = m_w \times \lambda_w = 421.05 \times 2308 = 971{,}623 \text{ kJ}$$
$$\dot{Q} = \dfrac{971{,}623}{3600} = 269.9 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students frequently forget that **dry solid mass is conserved** during drying. They attempt to subtract the moisture percentages directly ($85\% - 5\% = 80\%$) and multiply by 500 kg, giving an incorrect answer of **400 kg** instead of **421.05 kg**.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Marks are lost when $\lambda_w$ at the *actual drying temperature* is not used. The default **$2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$** is for $100^{\circ}\text{C}$. At $80^{\circ}\text{C}$, use **$2308 \text{ kJ/kg}$**.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** Always present the **unit conversion** ($3600 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ h}$) when reporting in kWh.

---

### Question B: Choice 2 (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the **principle, process, and applications of freeze-drying (lyophilization)** in fruit-based snack technology. Compare it with hot air drying in terms of nutrient retention and structure. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Freeze Drying:**

Freeze drying (lyophilization) is a **sublimation-based dehydration** process. The product is first **frozen** (typically at $-30^{\circ}\text{C} \text{ to } -40^{\circ}\text{C}$), then subjected to **high vacuum** (pressure $\le 100 \text{ Pa}$, well below the **triple point of water**, $P_{tp} = 611.657 \text{ Pa}$). Under these conditions, ice sublimates directly into water vapor without passing through the liquid phase, preserving the cellular structure of the fruit.

**Three-Stage Process:**

1. **Freezing stage:** Rapid freezing at $-30^{\circ}\text{C}$ forms small ice crystals (small crystals → better structure preservation; slow freezing → large crystals that rupture cells).
2. **Primary drying (sublimation):** At $0.1\text{–}0.5 \text{ mbar}$, $\sim 80\%$ of the free water is removed at $-20^{\circ}\text{C} \text{ to } -10^{\circ}\text{C}$.
3. **Secondary drying (desorption):** Temperature is raised to $30^{\circ}\text{C}–40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to remove bound water, achieving final moisture $\le 3\%$ (w.b.).

**Applications in Fruit Snacks:**
- Freeze-dried strawberry, mango, pineapple, and banana chunks — premium market.
- Used for nutraceutical fruit powders (amla, sea buckthorn).
- Instant fruit ingredients for breakfast cereals and yogurt toppings.

**Comparison with Hot Air Drying:**

| Parameter | Hot Air Drying | Freeze Drying |
|---|---|---|
| Vitamin C retention | $40\text{–}60\%$ | $> 90\%$ |
| Color (Hunter $L^*$) | $35\text{–}50$ (dark) | $70\text{–}85$ (bright) |
| Structure | Shrunk, dense | Highly porous, crisp |
| Rehydration | Poor | Excellent |
| Energy consumption | $5000 \text{ kJ/kg water}$ | $25{,}000\text{–}30{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg water}$ |
| Capital cost | Low | Very high |
| Product cost (INR/kg) | $80\text{–}200$ | $2000\text{–}5000$ |

#### (b) Discuss in detail the **preparation of osmotically dehydrated candied fruits (tutti-frutti)**. List four factors affecting osmotic dehydration rate and the engineering significance of the process. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Osmotic dehydration (OD) is a **partial water removal** process in which fruit pieces are immersed in a **hypertonic** (high-osmotic-pressure) solution — typically $60^{\circ}\text{–}70^{\circ}\text{Bx}$ sugar syrup (sucrose + glucose + fructose). Water flows **out of the fruit** (low solute concentration) **into the syrup** (high solute concentration) across the **semipermeable cell membrane**, while a counter-flow of sugars migrates **into** the fruit. Equilibrium is reached when osmotic pressure differences vanish.

**Process Flow:**
1. **Selection:** Fresh, firm, fully ripe papaya / pineapple / raw mango (Brix 8–12°Bx, pH 3.5–4.0).
2. **Peeling and dicing:** Uniform $1 \text{ cm}^3$ cubes.
3. **Blanching:** $90^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $3 \text{ min}$ to inactivate PPO.
4. **Osmotic dip:** $60^{\circ}\text{Bx}$ syrup, fruit-to-syrup ratio $1:2$, $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, $4 \text{–}6 \text{ hours}$.
5. **Draining and surface-drying** with air knife.
6. **Glazing:** Dip in $70^{\circ}\text{Bx}$ syrup and tumble-dry at $55^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $4 \text{ h}$.
7. **Coloring and flavoring** (optional).
8. **Packaging** in airtight containers.

**Four factors affecting OD rate:**
1. **Syrup concentration** — higher Brix → faster water loss; optimum $60^{\circ}\text{–}70^{\circ}\text{Bx}$.
2. **Temperature** — increased $T$ (up to $60^{\circ}\text{C}$) increases membrane permeability and rate, but above $60^{\circ}\text{C}$ causes softening.
3. **Immersion time** — water loss follows first-order kinetics, plateauing at $4\text{–}6 \text{ h}$.
4. **Agitation** — gentle stirring increases mass-transfer coefficient by $\sim 30\%$.

**Engineering Significance:**
- **Energy efficient** — no phase change; $5\times$ less energy than hot air drying.
- **Pre-treatment** before air/vacuum/freeze drying → reduces subsequent drying load by $30\text{–}50\%$.
- **Retention of flavor and aroma** — mild temperatures.
- **Product versatility** — tutti-frutti, candied fruits, fruit pieces for bakery, ice cream, and confectionery.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Choice 2):** Students often confuse *osmosis* with *reverse osmosis* (a membrane separation process) and lose 2 marks. Reverse osmosis uses **applied pressure** to overcome osmotic pressure; OD uses **natural osmotic gradient** at **atmospheric pressure** with no membrane other than the fruit's own cell walls.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Fruit-Based Snacks):**

- **Six-stage process:** Selection → Pre-treatment → Sizing → Moisture reduction → Post-treatment → Packaging.
- **Water activity threshold:** $a_w \le 0.60$ for microbial safety; 0.30–0.45 for crispy chips.
- **Enzymes to inactivate:** **PPO** (polyphenol oxidase, EC 1.10.3.1) and **POD** (peroxidase, EC 1.11.1.7). KMS + citric acid is the gold-standard dip.
- **Moisture conversion identity:** $M_{db} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}$ — memorize and apply.
- **Dry solid mass is conserved** in any dehydration process — the foundation of every drying mass balance.
- **Vacuum frying** reduces oil uptake to $< 30\%$, preserves color, and operates at $80\text{–}100^{\circ}\text{C}$ under $\le 5 \text{ kPa}$.
- **Freeze drying** preserves vitamin C $> 90\%$ but is the most energy-intensive ($25{,}000\text{–}30{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg water}$).
- **Osmotic dehydration** uses $60^{\circ}\text{–}70^{\circ}\text{Bx}$ syrup, $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, $4\text{–}6 \text{ h}$ — no phase change, hence energy-efficient.
- **Fick's second law:** $\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 X}{\partial z^2}$ governs internal moisture migration in drying.
- **Drying models to remember:** Lewis (Newton) $X_t = X^* + (X_0 - X^*) e^{-kt}$ — frequently tested.
- **Hunter L\*a\*b\* color scale:** $L^* \in [0, 100]$ for lightness; preserve $L^*$ within $\pm 5$ units of fresh sample.
- **Packaging:** Use **metallized PET** (WVTR $\le 1 \text{ g/m}^2$/day) with **nitrogen flushing** for shelf life of $6\text{–}12$ months.
- **FSSAI/Codex compliance:** Final moisture $\le 6\%$ (w.b.) for dried fruit chips; $a_w \le 0.60$ mandatory.
- **Latent heat of vaporization:** $2308 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at $80^{\circ}\text{C}$; $2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ — choose based on dryer temperature.
- **Engineering impact:** Reduces post-harvest loss ($20\text{–}30\% \to < 5\%$), value-adds seasonal fruits, supports Kerala's ₹5000+ crore banana/jackfruit chip industry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology for Vegetable-Based Snacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technology for Vegetable-Based Snacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vegetable-Based Snack Technology** is defined as the systematic application of unit operations (peeling, slicing, blanching, drying, frying, baking, extrusion, and seasoning) on fresh vegetables, tubers, roots, and pulses to produce ready-to-eat (RTE) shelf-stable products characterized by **low moisture content** (typically $a_w < 0.6$), acceptable **crispness/texture**, and microbiological safety suitable for ambient storage.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PEFTT756 – Snack Food Technology*, vegetable-based snacks constitute the largest commercial segment of the global savory snack industry (estimated at **USD 150+ billion globally**), with potato being the dominant raw material, followed by pulses, corn, and specialty vegetables.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take a fresh potato slice, dip it in hot oil, and water inside it turns to steam, escaping violently and leaving behind a crispy, golden lattice of starch and oil — **that is the entire science of vegetable snack technology.**

Think of the operation in three logical steps:

1. **Disruption** – Mechanical slicing breaks cell walls and increases surface area.
2. **Transformation** – Heat (frying/baking/extrusion) drives off moisture and gelatinizes starch.
3. **Preservation** – Final low moisture + low water activity prevents microbial growth, just like pickling or drying mango.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Every vegetable snack is essentially a *dehydrated, flavored, crispy matrix*. The technology simply chooses *how* to remove water (frying, baking, puffing, drying) and *what* to add back (salt, spices, oil, cheese).

### 1.3 Classification of Vegetable-Based Snacks

| Category | Examples | Dominant Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Tuber-based** | Potato chips, french fries, banana chips | Frying / Vacuum frying |
| **Root-based** | Beetroot, carrot, sweet potato chips | Baking / Frying |
| **Pulse/Legume-based** | Dal-based fryums, moong snacks, roasted chana | Extrusion / Roasting |
| **Leafy/Green vegetable** | Spinach, kale chips | Dehydration + Baking |
| **Fruit-vegetable hybrids** | Tomato, okra, bitter gourd crisps | Vacuum frying / Freeze drying |
| **Coated snacks** | Vegetable tempura, batter-coated fries | Breading + Frying |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Industry Benchmarks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following numerical benchmarks are critical for KTU examination purposes and industrial acceptability:
> - **Final Moisture Content:** 1–3 % (fried) ; 4–8 % (baked)
> - **Water Activity ($a_w$):** $\leq 0.6$ for shelf stability
> - **Frying Temperature:** $160 \text{ °C} - 180 \text{ °C}$
> - **Oil Uptake Target:** 30–40 % of finished chip weight
> - **Slice Thickness:** 1.2–1.8 mm (standard chips); 2.5–3.0 mm (crinkle)
> - **Blanching Temperature:** $75 \text{ °C} - 85 \text{ °C}$ for 3–5 minutes

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Unit Operations Sequence (Universal Flow)

Regardless of the vegetable, the manufacturing sequence follows this **canonical 10-step flow**:

1. **Receiving & Grading** – visual inspection, size sorting
2. **Washing** – rotary drum washers with potable water
3. **Peeling** – abrasive, steam, or lye (NaOH) peeling
4. **Slicing/Dicing** – rotary slicers, water-jet cutters
5. **Blanching** – hot water or steam; inactivates enzymes
6. **Dewatering/Drying** – removes surface moisture (pre-frying step)
7. **Frying / Baking / Extrusion** – core transformation
8. **De-oiling** (if fried) – vibrating screens, centrifugal
9. **Seasoning** – tumble coater with flavor powder/oil
10. **Packaging** – nitrogen-flushed, metallized pouches

### 2.2 The Why Behind Each Operation

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding the **cause-effect relationship** is the key to scoring high in KTU descriptive questions.

- **Peeling:** Removes soil, pesticide residues, and inedible skin that carries bitter glycoalkaloids (e.g., solanine in potatoes).
- **Blanching:** Inactivates **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** which causes browning; also leaches reducing sugars.
- **Slicing uniformity:** Determines **uniform moisture removal** and **uniform color** (Maillard reaction depends on surface-to-volume ratio).
- **Frying at $160-180$ °C:** Above the boiling point of water ($100$ °C) but below smoke point of oil; allows rapid crust formation while interior remains soft.
- **Cooling/De-oiling:** Halts cooking; reduces surface oil via centrifugal force.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are examinable and directly applicable to numerical problems in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{drying} = \dfrac{m_{initial} - m_{final}}{m_{initial}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 2 | Water Activity (R-O bound) | $a_w = \dfrac{P_{product}}{P_{pure water}}$ | Dimensionless ; $0 \leq a_w \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Oil Uptake Ratio | $OOR = \dfrac{W_{oil\ absorbed}}{W_{dry\ chip}} \times 100$ | Typically **30–40 %** |
| 4 | Frying Mass Balance | $W_{in} - W_{water\ lost} + W_{oil\ absorbed} = W_{out}$ | Mass conservation |
| 5 | Heat Transfer (frying) | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $c_p$ = specific heat ($\text{kJ/kg·K}$) |
| 6 | Rehydration Ratio | $RR = \dfrac{W_{rehydrated}}{W_{dehydrated}}$ | Dimensionless ; >1 |
| 7 | Maillard Reaction Rate | $k \propto e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Temperature-dependent |
| 8 | Shrinkage Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{V_{initial} - V_{final}}{V_{initial}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 9 | Porosity (post-frying) | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ | $0 < \varepsilon < 1$ |
| 10 | Peroxide Value (oil quality) | $PV = \dfrac{(S - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | **meq O$_2$/kg oil** |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars use `\vert` (LaTeX) rather than the pipe symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Peanut butter factories** use similar blanching logic for skin removal.
- **Breakfast cereal industries** leverage extrusion principles identical to pulse snacks.
- **Pharmaceutical drying** uses the same dehydration kinetics as vegetable chips.
- **Pet food industry** employs twin-screw extrusion identical to that used for legume snacks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Frying Mass and Energy Balance

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU Part B question** (typically 7 marks) when combined with a numerical. Students must reproduce each algebraic transition.

**Problem Statement:** A potato slice weighing $50\text{ g}$ contains **80 % moisture**. After frying, it weighs $22\text{ g}$ with **35 % oil content**. Calculate:
(a) Mass of water evaporated
(b) Oil absorbed
(c) Frying efficiency

---

#### Step 1: Establish Initial Mass Components

The initial mass is composed of water and solids (non-water fraction).

$$
M_{initial} = 50 \text{ g}
$$

Water mass initially present:

$$
M_{water,\,initial} = 50 \times 0.80 = 40 \text{ g}
$$

Solid mass (cell wall + starch, non-volatile):

$$
M_{solid,\,initial} = 50 - 40 = 10 \text{ g}
$$

---

#### Step 2: Apply Mass Conservation at the Output

Final product composition:

$$
M_{final} = 22 \text{ g}
$$

Mass of oil absorbed in the final chip:

$$
M_{oil} = 22 \times 0.35 = 7.7 \text{ g}
$$

Remaining mass is solid + retained water:

$$
M_{solid+water,\,final} = 22 - 7.7 = 14.3 \text{ g}
$$

Since solids are non-volatile (do not evaporate):

$$
M_{solid,\,final} = M_{solid,\,initial} = 10 \text{ g}
$$

Therefore, retained water in the final chip:

$$
M_{water,\,final} = 14.3 - 10 = 4.3 \text{ g}
$$

---

#### Step 3: Compute Water Evaporated (Part a)

$$
M_{water,\,evap} = M_{water,\,initial} - M_{water,\,final}
$$

$$
M_{water,\,evap} = 40 - 4.3 = 35.7 \text{ g}
$$

> **Answer (a): 35.7 g of water was removed during frying.**

---

#### Step 4: Compute Oil Absorbed (Part b)

$$
M_{oil,\,absorbed} = 7.7 \text{ g}
$$

> **Answer (b): 7.7 g of oil was absorbed.**

---

#### Step 5: Compute Frying Efficiency (Part c)

Frying efficiency is defined as the ratio of water removed to total mass lost (assuming all mass loss is water evaporation):

$$
\eta_{frying} = \dfrac{M_{water,\,evap}}{M_{initial} - M_{final}} \times 100
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\eta_{frying} = \dfrac{35.7}{50 - 22} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{frying} = \dfrac{35.7}{28} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{frying} = 127.5 \text{ \%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why efficiency > 100 %?** This reveals that **oil is added back** while water is removed — the net mass change is *less* than water lost. Students often incorrectly interpret this as an error; it is actually the **hallmark signature of frying mass balance**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Moisture Removal During Hot Air Drying

For a vegetable slice undergoing thin-layer drying, the page model is used:

$$
\dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = e^{-kt}
$$

Where:
- $M_t$ = moisture at time $t$ (% dry basis)
- $M_0$ = initial moisture
- $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture
- $k$ = drying constant ($\text{min}^{-1}$)
- $t$ = time (min)

**Numerical Example:**

Given: $M_0 = 5.0$ (dry basis), $M_e = 0.05$, $k = 0.05 \text{ min}^{-1}$. Find $M_t$ at $t = 30$ min.

---

**Step 1:** Substitute into the equation.

$$
\dfrac{M_t - 0.05}{5.0 - 0.05} = e^{-(0.05)(30)}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the exponent.

$$
-(0.05)(30) = -1.5
$$

$$
e^{-1.5} = 0.2231
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply through.

$$
M_t - 0.05 = 4.95 \times 0.2231
$$

$$
M_t - 0.05 = 1.1043
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $M_t$.

$$
M_t = 1.1543 \text{ (dry basis)}
$$

Converting to **wet basis** ($M_{wb} = M_{db} / (1 + M_{db})$):

$$
M_{wb} = \dfrac{1.1543}{1 + 1.1543} = 0.5359 = 53.59 \text{ \%}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Moisture content at $t=30$ min is **53.59 % (w.b.)** or **1.15 (d.b.)**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Processing Methods

| Parameter | Deep Frying | Vacuum Frying | Hot Air Drying | Baking | Extrusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature (°C)** | 160–180 | 80–110 | 60–90 | 150–200 | 120–180 |
| **Final Moisture (%)** | 1–3 | 2–5 | 4–8 | 3–6 | 5–10 |
| **Oil Content (%)** | 30–40 | 15–25 | <2 | <5 | <5 |
| **Color (L value)** | Darker | Brighter | Pale | Golden | Variable |
| **Texture** | Very crispy | Light crispy | Chewy-hard | Crispy | Puffed |
| **Energy Use** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **Acrylamide Risk** | High | Low | Low | Moderate | Low |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Low | Moderate | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Acrylamide** (suspected carcinogen) forms from the **Maillard reaction** between *reducing sugars* (glucose, fructose) and *asparagine* at temperatures above $120$ °C. **Vacuum frying** at $<100$ °C significantly mitigates this risk — a frequent KTU theory question.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Oil Uptake Mechanism (Capillary Theory)

Oil penetrates the chip via the same capillaries through which water escaped. The Lucas-Washburn equation governs capillary rise:

$$
L^2 = \dfrac{r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}
$$

Where:
- $L$ = penetration depth
- $r$ = capillary radius
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of oil
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $t$ = time
- $\eta$ = oil viscosity

**Interpretation:** Lower viscosity oil and smaller pore size → deeper penetration. This is why **cold oiling post-frying** (lower $\eta$) increases oil uptake.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Potato Chip Manufacturing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Potato Receiving] --> nodeB[Rotary Drum Washing]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Abrasive Peeling]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Inspection and Trimming]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Rotary Slicing 1.5 mm]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Hot Water Blanching 80 C 4 min]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Surface Dewatering Vibratory Screen]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Process Selection}
    nodeH -->|Conventional| nodeI[Continuous Fryer 175 C 90 sec]
    nodeH -->|Vacuum| nodeJ[Vacuum Fryer 95 C 25 min]
    nodeI --> nodeK[De-oiling Centrifuge]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Seasoning Tumble Coater]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Quality Check Weight Color Metal Detector]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Nitrogen Flushed Packaging]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Storage and Dispatch]
```

### 4.2 Pulse-Based Extruded Snack — Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Pulses Yellow Peas Moong] --> nodeB[Cleaning and Destoning]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Dry Milling Flour 60 mesh]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Conditioning Moisture 18 to 22 percent]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Twin Screw Extruder 140 C]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Die Cutting and Shaping]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Drying Tunnel 80 C]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Oil Spray Seasoning]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Pneumatic Conveyor Cooling]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Automated Packaging]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Quality Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Vegetable Raw Material] --> nodeB{High Sugar Content}
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[Vacuum Frying or Baking below 120 C]
    nodeB -->|No| nodeD{High Moisture Content}
    nodeD -->|Greater than 80 percent| nodeE[Predrying plus Frying]
    nodeD -->|Less than 80 percent| nodeF[Direct Frying or Extrusion]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Final Quality Control]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Finished Vegetable Snack]
```

### 4.4 Seasoning Application Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Seasoning Powder Blend] --> nodeB[Continuous Tumble Drum]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Oil Spray Nozzle]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Helical Agitator]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Bagging and Metal Detection]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Finished Snack]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define water activity. Why is reducing water activity critical in vegetable-based snack preservation?*

**Model Answer:**
Water activity ($a_w$) is the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a product to the vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature:

$$
a_w = \dfrac{P_{product}}{P_{pure\ water}}
$$

It indicates the *available* water for microbial and chemical reactions. Most spoilage bacteria require $a_w > 0.91$, yeasts $> 0.88$, and molds $> 0.70$. Vegetable chips achieve $a_w < 0.6$ post-frying, making them microbially shelf-stable at ambient temperature without refrigeration or chemical preservatives. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three differences between vacuum frying and conventional frying of vegetables.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Vacuum Frying | Conventional Frying |
|---|---|---|
| Operating pressure | Reduced (3–30 kPa) | Atmospheric (101 kPa) |
| Frying temperature | 80–110 °C | 160–180 °C |
| Oil uptake | Lower (15–25 %) | Higher (30–40 %) |
| Color retention | Better (less browning) | Darker |
| Acrylamide formation | Minimal | Significant |
| Equipment cost | High | Low |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]****

**(a)** Explain in detail the **unit operations involved in the manufacture of potato chips**, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each operation in determining final product quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A potato slice weighing $80\text{ g}$ contains **78 % moisture**. After frying, it weighs $32\text{ g}$ with **38 % oil**. Calculate: (i) Water evaporated, (ii) Oil absorbed, (iii) Comment on the frying efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

The manufacture of potato chips proceeds through the following sequential unit operations:

1. **Receiving and Sorting:** Potatoes are inspected for size, defects, and glycoalkaloid content (solanine should be $< 20\text{ mg}/100\text{ g}$).

2. **Washing:** Rotary drum washers with potable water and brushes remove soil and debris.

3. **Peeling:** Either abrasive rollers (for hard-skinned potatoes) or lye peeling (10–15 % NaOH at $70$–$90$ °C for 2–4 min) — lye is more efficient industrially.

4. **Trimming and Inspection:** Manual or laser-guided removal of eyes and defects.

5. **Slicing:** Rotary slicers at $1.2$–$1.8\text{ mm}$ thickness. Uniformity is critical for uniform drying and color.

6. **Blanching:** Hot water at $75$–$85$ °C for 3–5 min. **Purpose:** Inactivates polyphenol oxidase (prevents enzymatic browning), leaches reducing sugars (reduces acrylamide formation), and improves texture.

7. **Surface Dewatering:** Vibratory screens and air knives remove surface moisture — otherwise oil sputtering occurs during frying.

8. **Frying:** Continuous belt fryer at $175$ °C for 60–120 seconds. The slice loses ~50 % of its mass as water vapor while absorbing ~30–40 % oil.

9. **De-oiling:** Vibratory conveyors or centrifugal de-oilers remove surface oil; final oil content 30–35 %.

10. **Seasoning:** Tumble drum coating with cheese, salt, masala, or vinegar powder (1–3 % by weight).

11. **Packaging:** Nitrogen-flushed metallized polypropylene pouches protect against oxygen, moisture, and light.

**Quality Influence:**
- **Slice thickness** affects crispness.
- **Blanching time** controls color.
- **Frying temperature** governs acrylamide level.
- **Oil quality** determines peroxide value and shelf life.

**(7 marks — model answer)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

Given:
- $M_{initial} = 80\text{ g}$
- Initial moisture = $78$ %
- $M_{final} = 32\text{ g}$
- Oil in final = $38$ %

**Step 1: Initial water and solids**

$$
M_{water,\,initial} = 80 \times 0.78 = 62.4 \text{ g}
$$

$$
M_{solid,\,initial} = 80 - 62.4 = 17.6 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2: Oil absorbed (ii)**

$$
M_{oil} = 32 \times 0.38 = 12.16 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3: Final water**

$$
M_{solid+water,\,final} = 32 - 12.16 = 19.84 \text{ g}
$$

Since solids remain unchanged:

$$
M_{water,\,final} = 19.84 - 17.6 = 2.24 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4: Water evaporated (i)**

$$
M_{water,\,evap} = 62.4 - 2.24 = 60.16 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5: Frying efficiency (iii)**

$$
\eta_{frying} = \dfrac{60.16}{80 - 32} \times 100 = \dfrac{60.16}{48} \times 100 = 125.3 \text{ \%}
$$

> **Comment:** Frying efficiency exceeds 100 % because the **net mass loss is reduced** by oil uptake. This value $\eta > 100$ is a *signature indicator* of proper frying — water loss dominates while oil adds back mass.

**(7 marks — model answer)**

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **technology for pulse-based extruded snacks**. Highlight the role of extrusion parameters (temperature, moisture, screw speed) on product characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Maillard reaction** and its relevance to acrylamide formation in vegetable snacks. Suggest two control strategies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

Pulse-based extruded snacks use **dehulled pulse flours** (yellow pea, chickpea, lentil, moong) as the primary raw material due to their high protein (20–25 %) and fiber content.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Cleaning:** Removal of stones, dust, and foreign matter via destoners and magnetic separators.
2. **Dehulling:** Abrasive or roller dehullers split the seed coat — improves digestibility and color.
3. **Dry Milling:** Hammer or pin mill to $60$–$80$ mesh particle size.
4. **Conditioning:** Moisture raised to $18$–$22$ % by steam/water injection — critical for starch gelatinization inside the extruder.
5. **Extrusion:** Twin-screw extruder at $120$–$180$ °C; SME (Specific Mechanical Energy) input of $100$–$400\text{ Wh/kg}$.
6. **Die Cutting:** Curved or shaped dies produce curls, puffs, balls, or rings.
7. **Drying:** Hot air tunnel at $80$ °C to reduce moisture to $5$–$8$ %.
8. **Seasoning and Packaging:** Oil-spray + seasoning powder in tumble coater.

**Role of Extrusion Parameters:**

- **Temperature:** Higher temperature ($>150$ °C) increases expansion but risks protein denaturation and dark color.
- **Moisture:** Higher feed moisture (>22 %) reduces SME input → softer texture.
- **Screw Speed:** Higher rpm increases SME → more fragmentation, denser product.

**(7 marks — model answer)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

The **Maillard reaction** is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between *reducing carbonyl groups* (aldehydes/ketones of reducing sugars like glucose) and *amino groups* of amino acids/proteins. It proceeds in three stages:

1. **Initial:** Schiff base → Amadori rearrangement
2. **Intermediate:** Reactive dicarbonyls, furfurals
3. **Final:** Melanoidins (brown polymers)

**Relevance to Acrylamide:**
Acrylamide (CH$_2$=CH–CONH$_2$) forms when **asparagine** (an amino acid abundant in potatoes) reacts with reducing sugars at temperatures above $120$ °C, especially in low-moisture environments — typical of frying, baking, and roasting.

**Control Strategies:**

1. **Reduce reducing sugars:** Soak potato slices in water for 30 min before frying (leaches glucose/fructose); or use cultivars with low sugar content (e.g., Lady Rosetta).

2. **Lower frying temperature / Vacuum fry:** Operate below $120$ °C when feasible; vacuum frying at $95$ °C virtually eliminates acrylamide.

3. **Asparaginase treatment:** Enzyme that converts asparagine to aspartic acid, eliminating the precursor.

4. **Use of antioxidants:** Citric acid and rosemary extract reduce intermediate reactive carbonyls.

**(7 marks — model answer)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always specify moisture content in either *dry basis* (d.b.) or *wet basis* (w.b.) — examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.
> 2. **Confusing oil uptake with oil absorption:** Uptake is the *final oil content*; absorption is the *net oil gain* during processing.
> 3. **Skipping the role of blanching:** Students often describe blanching but forget to state its *anti-browning* and *anti-acrylamide* functions.
> 4. **Mermaid diagram errors:** Avoid reserved words like `end` as node IDs — KTU examiners may not grade the diagram if syntax is broken.
> 5. **Mass balance oversight:** Frying efficiency $>100\%$ is correct and must be explained, not deleted.
> 6. **Missing dietary acrylamide reference:** For Part B questions on browning, mentioning only color (not safety) leads to loss of 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examination**

- **Vegetable snacks = low moisture + low $a_w$ + oil + seasoning** — memorize the **4-pillar principle**.
- **Frying temperature window:** **$160$–$180$ °C** (above water boiling point, below oil smoke point).
- **Water activity threshold:** **$a_w < 0.6$** ensures shelf stability.
- **Oil uptake target:** **30–40 %** in conventional frying; **15–25 %** in vacuum frying.
- **Blanching temperature/time:** **75–85 °C** for **3–5 minutes** — inactivates PPO.
- **Acrylamide precursor:** Asparagine + reducing sugars at $>120$ °C.
- **Maillard reaction:** Reducing sugar + amino acid → melanoidins.
- **Pulse snacks:** Extrusion at **120–180 °C** with feed moisture **18–22 %**.
- **Slice thickness standard:** **1.2–1.8 mm** for standard chips.
- **Frying efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{M_{water,\,evap}}{M_{initial} - M_{final}} \times 100$ — can exceed 100 %.
- **Mass conservation:** $W_{in} = W_{water\,lost} + W_{oil\,gained} + W_{final\,product}$.
- **Drying model:** Page equation — $\dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = e^{-kt}$.
- **Capillary mechanism (Lucas-Washburn):** Explains oil penetration into pores.
- **Vacuum frying advantage:** Lower temperature → better color + lower acrylamide.
- **Quality control tests:** Peroxide value, free fatty acids, color (L\*a\*b\*), moisture, $a_w$, crispness (texture analyzer).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Colouring and Flavouring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Colouring and Flavouring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Colouring and Flavouring in Snack Foods

In the realm of snack food technology, **Colouring and Flavouring** represent the twin pillars of **sensory acceptance**. While a snack may be nutritionally perfect and texturally flawless, it is the *appearance* and *taste-aroma* profile that drive consumer purchase decisions at the retail shelf. Module 3 of the KTU 2024 scheme (PEFTT756) is therefore dedicated to the science, selection, and application of these two critical food additives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, the examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between *natural*, *nature-identical*, and *synthetic* colorants/flavouring agents, and to explain the chemistry behind thermally-generated process flavours (e.g., Maillard reaction) in fried/baked snack systems.

### 1.2 What is a Food Colourant?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU / FSSAI Aligned):**
> A **food colourant** (or food colour) is any dye, pigment, or substance that, when added to a food, is capable of (alone or through reaction with another substance) imparting a colour to the food. These are regulated as per the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** in India, replacing the older Prevention of Food Adulteration (PFA) Act, 1954.

#### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a plain white canvas — the same canvas painted with a vibrant sunset evokes warmth and emotion. Similarly, a fried *murukku* without its characteristic golden-amber hue appears "under-cooked" or "raw" to the consumer, even if the internal moisture and texture are perfect. **Colour = First Impression**. Our brain judges taste *before* the food even touches the tongue. This is known as the **"Colour-Taste Expectancy"** phenomenon, scientifically documented in psychophysics studies.

### 1.3 What is a Flavouring Agent?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **flavouring agent** (or flavour) is a substance that, when added to a food, is capable of imparting a particular taste and/or odour to the food. Flavour, in sensory science, is the combined perception of:
> 1. **Taste** (gustation — sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami) detected by *taste buds* on the tongue.
> 2. **Aroma** (olfaction — volatile compounds) detected by the *olfactory epithelium* in the nasal cavity.
> 3. **Chemesthesis** (trigeminal nerve responses — pungency, cooling, astringency).

#### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of flavour as the **"personality"** of a snack. Colour is the *face* you see; flavour is the *voice* you hear when you bite. A cheese ball without its cheesy aroma compounds is just a starchy crunch — the volatile lactones, pyrazines, and short-chain fatty acids are what "speak" to the brain's limbic system and trigger the reward circuit.

### 1.4 Standard Regulatory Metrics (Must Memorise)

| Metric | Standard / Limit |
|---|---|
| **Permitted Synthetic Colours in India (FSSAI Rule 3.1.10)** | **8 colours** (see Section 2) |
| **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** for Sunset Yellow FCF | **0–2.5 mg/kg body weight** |
| **ADI for Tartrazine** | **0–7.5 mg/kg body weight** |
| **Number of basic taste qualities** | **5** (Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, Umami) |
| **Threshold for volatile aroma detection** | Often in **parts per billion (ppb)** range |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote FSSAI 2011 Regulations (and NOT the PFA Act) in 2024 scheme answers. The PFA Act was superseded in 2011.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Food Colourants

Food colourants are classified broadly into **three categories** based on their origin and regulatory status:

### A. Natural Colourants
These are pigments extracted from plant, animal, mineral, or microbiological sources. They are generally considered **safer** and command premium "clean-label" positioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chemistry Refresher:** Most natural pigments are secondary metabolites in plants, evolved to attract pollinators or for UV protection.

| Natural Colourant | Source | Chemical Class | Colour Shade | pH Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Caramel** | Heated sugar | Melanoidins (Maillard product) | Dark brown | Stable (pH 3–9) |
| **Annatto** | Bixa orellana seeds | **Carotenoid** (Bixin, Norbixin) | Yellow-orange | Stable pH 4–8 |
| **Curcumin** | Turmeric rhizome | **Polyphenol** (Diferuloylmethane) | Bright yellow | Unstable above pH 7 |
| **Chlorophyll** | Green leafy plants | **Porphyrin** (Mg-tetrapyrrole) | Green | Unstable (replaced by pheophytin) |
| **Anthocyanins** | Berries, red cabbage | **Flavonoid** (glycosides) | Red-purple-blue | **Highly pH sensitive** |
| **Betalains** | Beetroot | **N-containing pigments** (Betanin) | Red-violet | Stable pH 4–6 |
| **Paprika / Capsanthin** | Red pepper | **Carotenoid** (Xanthophyll) | Red-orange | Stable |
| **Cochineal / Carmine** | Cochineal insect | **Anthraquinone** glycoside | Crimson | Stable pH 5–7 |
| **Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂)** | Mineral | **Inorganic** oxide | White | Inert / stable |
| **Iron Oxides** | Mineral | **Inorganic** | Red, yellow, black | Inert / stable |

### B. Synthetic (Artificial) Colourants
These are **coal-tar derivatives** or **azo dyes** synthesised chemically. They offer:
- High tinctorial strength (intense colour at low dose)
- Excellent batch-to-batch consistency
- Lower cost
- Wider pH and heat stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU MUST-KNOW: The 8 Synthetic Colours Permitted in India (FSSAI)**
> 1. **Tartrazine** (CI 19140) — Yellow
> 2. **Sunset Yellow FCF** (CI 15985) — Orange
> 3. **Erythrosine** (CI 45430) — Pink/Red
> 4. **Ponceau 4R** (CI 16255) — Red
> 5. **Allura Red AC** (CI 16035) — Red
> 6. **Brilliant Blue FCF** (CI 42090) — Blue
> 7. **Indigo Carmine** (CI 73015) — Blue
> 8. **Fast Green FCF** (CI 42053) — Green
>
> Maximum permitted limit in snacks: **100 mg/kg (100 ppm)** for individual or combined colours.

### C. Nature-Identical Colourants
These are chemically synthesised versions of molecules that occur in nature. Example: **β-Carotene** can be extracted from carrots (natural) OR synthesised chemically (nature-identical). They offer consistency of synthetic products with the "natural acceptance" of natural ones.

### 2.2 Functional Roles of Colour in Snacks

1. **Aesthetic / Visual Appeal** — Drives first-purchase impulse.
2. **Colour Loss Compensation** — Heat, light, and oxidation destroy natural pigments during frying/baking. Added colour compensates.
3. **Standardisation** — Ensures uniform appearance across batches.
4. **Identity / Branding** — Orange for cheese, red for tomato, green for pudina (mint).
5. **Masking of Inferiority** — Conceals protein or off-colour particles.
6. **Psychological Taste Cue** — Red = sweet, Green = sour, Yellow = salty (cross-modal perception).

### 2.3 Classification of Flavouring Agents

| Class | Definition | Example in Snacks |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Flavour** | Derived from plant/animal source by physical/enzymatic/microbial means | Vanilla extract, peppermint oil |
| **Nature-Identical Flavour** | Chemically synthesised version of a molecule found in nature | Vanillin (synthetic) |
| **Artificial Flavour** | Not found in nature; synthesised in lab | Ethyl vanillin (3× stronger than vanillin) |
| **Process Flavour (Reaction Flavour)** | Produced by heating precursors (Maillard-type) | Pyrazines in roasted peanuts, meat flavours |
| **Smoke Flavour** | Condensate of wood smoke | Barbecue snack coatings |
| **Encapsulated Flavour** | Flavour trapped in a matrix (maltodextrin, gum arabic) | Chewing gum, flavoured chips |

### 2.4 The Chemistry of Flavour Formation in Snacks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD: Flavour Generation Pathways During Processing**

#### 1. **Maillard Reaction** (Non-enzymatic browning)
Discovered by **Louis-Camille Maillard** in **1912**. A cascade reaction between **reducing sugars** (glucose, fructose) and **amino groups** (from proteins, free amino acids, peptides). Three stages:

$$
\text{Stage 1: Reducing Sugar + Amino Acid} \xrightarrow{\text{condensation}} \text{Glycosylamine} \xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{Amadori Compound}
$$

$$
\text{Stage 2: Amadori Compound} \xrightarrow{\text{dehydration/enolization}} \text{Reductones, Furfurals, Pyrazines}
$$

$$
\text{Stage 3: Polymerization} \rightarrow \text{Melanoidins (brown pigments) + Aroma volatiles}
$$

**Key flavour compounds generated:** Pyrazines (roasted/nutty), Furfurals (caramel/bready), Thiophenes (meaty), Thiazoles (savoury).

#### 2. **Lipid Oxidation** (Autoxidation)
Unsaturated fatty acids (oleic, linoleic, linolenic) undergo **peroxidation** producing:
- **Aldehydes** (hexanal — beany, grassy)
- **Ketones** (rancid notes)
- **Lactones** (peach, coconut — desirable in baked goods)

#### 3. **Caramelisation**
Heating sugars (>160 °C) without amino acids produces:
- **Diacetyl** (buttery)
- **Furfural** (caramel)
- **Maltol** (toasty, cotton-candy)

#### 4. **Strecker Degradation**
Amino acids are degraded by dicarbonyls (from Maillard) to produce:
- **Aldehydes** with one less carbon than parent amino acid
- Example: **Leucine → 3-Methylbutanal** (malty)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule of Thumb | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **CI (Colour Index) Number** | International coding for dyes | Dimensionless |
| **ppm Colour Addition** | Typical snack level: 50–500 | mg/kg of product |
| **Beer-Lambert Law** (for colour analysis) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | A = absorbance, ε = molar absorptivity, c = concentration, l = path length |
| **Tinctorial Strength** | Inversely related to dosage needed | Higher = less dye needed |
| **Maillard Optimum pH** | $pH = 4.5 - 6.5$ | Higher rate at alkaline pH |
| **Maillard Optimum Temperature** | $T = 140 - 165~^\circ\text{C}$ | Accelerates above $a_w = 0.6$ |
| **5 Basic Tastes & their detection threshold** | Sweet (sucrose): ~10 mM; Salty (NaCl): ~30 mM; Sour (HCl): ~0.9 mM; Bitter (quinine): ~0.008 mM; Umami (MSG): ~0.7 mM | Approximate values |
| **Encapsulation Efficiency (EE)** | $\text{EE}\% = \frac{W_{\text{total flavour}} - W_{\text{surface flavour}}}{W_{\text{total flavour}}} \times 100$ | Higher = better retention |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR TABLES:** In the Beer-Lambert equation, use `\vert` or `\mid` for any vertical-bar expressions. Example: $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ (no bars needed here, but for $A = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$ always use `\vert` if writing absolute value forms).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process optimisation** in fryers and extruders to control $a_w$ and residence time, thereby tuning Maillard-derived flavour and colour.
- **Shelf-life prediction** using colour change kinetics (e.g., $\Delta E$ in CIE Lab colour space) and aroma loss (Henry's Law partitioning).
- **Encapsulation engineering** for sustained flavour release in microwave popcorn, where volatile compounds must survive the steam expansion step.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is **qualitative-technological** (Food Technology, not pure Physics/Mathematics), the "derivations" in this section refer to the **step-by-step chemical pathway analysis**, **regulatory logic**, and **calculation frameworks** that KTU examiners value.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Maillard Reaction Pathway (Board Exam Standard Answer)

### **Step 1 — Initial Condensation**

The carbonyl group (C=O) of the reducing sugar reacts with the amino group (–NH₂) of the amino acid to form an unstable **Schiff base**, which cyclises to form an **N-glycosylamine**.

$$
\text{Reducing Sugar} \choose\text{open-chain aldehyde}} + \text{H}_2\text{N} - \text{R} \rightarrow \text{Schiff Base} \rightarrow \text{N-glycosylamine}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the rate-determining condensation step. The Schiff base (imine, R–CH=N–R′) is formed by nucleophilic addition of the amine nitrogen to the carbonyl carbon, followed by loss of water.

### **Step 2 — Amadori Rearrangement**

The N-glycosylamine rearranges to a more stable **Amadori compound** (1-amino-1-deoxy-2-ketose).

$$
\text{N-glycosylamine} \xrightarrow{\text{Amadori rearrangement}} \text{1-Amino-1-deoxy-2-ketose}
$$

**Explanation:** The aldose sugar is converted to its ketose isomer while retaining the amino acid moiety. This rearrangement is acid- or base-catalysed and is the key intermediate of the entire pathway.

### **Step 3 — Dehydration & Fragmentation**

The Amadori compound undergoes dehydration (loss of water) to form **furfurals**, **reductones**, and **dicarbonyls** (e.g., deoxyosones).

$$
\text{Amadori} \xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{3-Deoxyglucosone} + \text{Furfural} + \text{Reductones}
$$

**Explanation:** These dicarbonyls are highly reactive and serve as the precursors for Strecker degradation and final polymerisation. At pH < 7, furfurals dominate; at pH > 7, reductones dominate.

### **Step 4 — Strecker Degradation (Inter-step with Maillard)**

Dicarbonyl compounds react with a *second* amino acid to release CO₂ and form a **Strecker aldehyde** with one carbon less than the parent amino acid.

$$
\text{Amino Acid} + \alpha\text{-Dicarbonyl} \rightarrow \text{Strecker Aldehyde} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Aminoketone}
$$

**Example derivation:**

$$
\text{Leucine} + \text{Methylglyoxal} \rightarrow \text{3-Methylbutanal} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Aminoketone}
$$

**Explanation:** The 3-methylbutanal gives the characteristic "malty" aroma of roasted snacks.

### **Step 5 — Final Polymerisation**

Aldehydes, furfurals, and pyrroles polymerise to form high-molecular-weight nitrogenous brown polymers called **melanoidins**.

$$
n \cdot \text{Intermediate} \xrightarrow{\text{polymerisation}} \text{Melanoidins (brown colour)} + \text{Pyrazines, Pyrroles, Thiophenes (aroma)}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the visual browning step and the source of final aroma profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Answer Mnemonic:** **"S-A-D-S-P"** — Schiff base → Amadori → Dehydration → Strecker → Polymerisation. This 5-step mental model is what KTU examiners expect in a 7-mark question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Colourant for Different Snack Systems

| Snack Type | Processing Condition | Recommended Colourant | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fried Murukku** | Oil, 160–180 °C | Caramel, Annatto | Heat-stable, fat-soluble |
| **Extruded Puffs (Cheese)** | 120–150 °C, low $a_w$ | Annatto + Turmeric | Stable in dry matrix |
| **Tomato-flavoured Chips** | 110–130 °C, post-dusting | Paprika, Carmine | Matches flavour cue |
| **Mint-flavoured Namkeen** | Drying, room temp | Chlorophyll (Cu-complex) | Green is rare in nature → high value |
| **Microwave Popcorn (Butter)** | 200 °C, sudden steam | β-Carotene (encapsulated) | Heat-stable, lipid-soluble |
| **Boiled Candy (Snack segment)** | Acidic, pH 2.5–3.5 | Caramel, Anthocyanin (red cabbage) | Acid-stable, no azo dyes preferred |
| **Baked Khakra** | 180–200 °C, dry heat | Turmeric + Caramel | Both browning + natural origin |

## 3.3 Calculation Example: Colour Dosage in a Snack

> **Problem:** A snack manufacturer needs to produce **500 kg** of cheese-flavored corn puffs. The desired dosage of **Annatto extract** is **50 mg/kg** of product. Calculate the total Annatto required.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Total Annatto} = \text{Batch Size} \times \text{Dosage}
$$

$$
\text{Total Annatto} = 500~\text{kg} \times 50~\text{mg/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Total Annatto} = 25{,}000~\text{mg} = 25~\text{g}
$$

**Answer:** **25 grams of Annatto extract** is required for the 500 kg batch. This is typically diluted in oil and sprayed onto the puffs in a **tumble coater** for uniform distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always express concentration in **mg/kg (ppm)** for trace additives and in **% (w/w)** for bulk ingredients. Never mix units in a calculation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Flavouring Agent for Snack Applications

| Flavour Type | Dosage Form | Application Method | Stability Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spray-Dried Powder** | Free-flowing powder (with maltodextrin carrier) | Dry-blend into seasoning | Hygroscopic — needs low RH packaging |
| **Liquid Oil-Soluble** | Dissolved in refined vegetable oil | Sprayed on hot product | Oxidation; use BHT/BHA antioxidants |
| **Encapsulated** | β-Cyclodextrin complex or gum arabic matrix | Dry-blend or slurry | Costly but excellent retention |
| **Plated (on maltodextrin)** | Powder with 10–20% flavour load | Dry-blend | Moderate stability |
| **Reaction Flavour Paste** | Maillard-reacted slurry | Post-process dusting | pH-sensitive; refrigerated storage |

## 3.5 Critical Pitfalls in Flavour Chemistry

| Mistake | Consequence | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Adding flavours BEFORE frying | Volatilisation loss (50–90%) | **Post-process surface application** |
| Using water-soluble flavour in oil system | Phase separation, poor dispersion | Use **oil-soluble** variant |
| Storing flavoured snacks at high RH | Flavour hydrolysis, caking | Keep RH < 65% |
| Mixing acid + annatto | Fading to pink | Use **acid-stable** colour |
| High-temperature baking of vanillin | Vanillin degrades → smoky off-note | Encapsulate or post-add |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Question]**
**"List any THREE synthetic food colours permitted under FSSAI regulations in India. State the maximum permitted limit in snack foods."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The three permitted synthetic food colours under FSSAI 2011 (formerly PFA Rule 26) are:

1. **Tartrazine** (Colour Index 19140) — Yellow
2. **Sunset Yellow FCF** (Colour Index 15985) — Orange
3. **Ponceau 4R** (Colour Index 16255) — Red

**Maximum permitted limit in snack foods: 100 mg/kg (100 ppm) for individual or combined colours.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Question]**
**"Define Maillard reaction. Name its THREE main stages."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The Maillard reaction is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between the **carbonyl group of a reducing sugar** and the **amino group of an amino acid/protein**, discovered by **Louis-Camille Maillard in 1912**.

The three main stages are:

1. **Initial Stage** — Condensation to form Schiff base and Amadori compound.
2. **Intermediate Stage** — Dehydration, fragmentation, and Strecker degradation (produces aroma volatiles).
3. **Final Stage** — Polymerisation into brown nitrogenous polymers called **melanoidins**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** In the 2024 scheme, each Part-B question carries 14 marks with a typical split of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two alternative question choices (Q-A and Q-B) are offered; answer **either** the first OR the second.

---

### **📝 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam Pattern]**

**(a)** Classify food colours with suitable examples. Explain the functions of colours in snack foods. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the Maillard reaction in detail, highlighting the role of intermediate compounds in flavour and colour development in extruded/fried snacks. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-A (a):**

Food colours are classified into three broad categories:

**1. Natural Colourants** — Obtained from plant, animal, mineral, or microbial sources. Examples: **caramel** (from heated sugar, brown), **curcumin** (from turmeric, yellow), **carotenoids** like β-carotene (from carrots, orange), **anthocyanins** (from berries, red-purple), **chlorophyll** (from green leaves, green), and **betalains** (from beetroot, red).

**2. Synthetic Colourants** — Chemically synthesised dyes, predominantly azo, triphenylmethane, and xanthene classes. FSSAI permits **eight synthetic colours** in India: Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow FCF, Erythrosine, Ponceau 4R, Allura Red AC, Brilliant Blue FCF, Indigo Carmine, and Fast Green FCF.

**3. Nature-Identical Colourants** — Chemically identical to natural molecules but produced by synthesis. Example: synthetic β-carotene and riboflavin.

**Functions of colours in snack foods:**

- **Visual appeal:** Drives impulse purchase at retail shelves.
- **Batch standardisation:** Compensates for natural variation due to raw material and processing.
- **Loss compensation:** Replaces pigments destroyed by heat (frying, baking), light, and oxygen.
- **Flavour expectation:** Sets the cross-modal sensory cue (orange ↔ cheese, red ↔ tomato, green ↔ mint).
- **Branding and identity:** Distinguishes product variants.
- **Masking of defects:** Conceals protein specks, uneven browning, or impurities.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-A (b):**

The **Maillard reaction** is the most important flavour-and-colour-generating pathway in thermally processed snacks (fried murukku, baked biscuits, extruded puffs, roasted nuts). It is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between **reducing sugars** (glucose, fructose, maltose) and **amino groups** (free amino acids, peptides, proteins). The reaction proceeds in three major stages:

**Stage 1 — Initial Condensation:**
The carbonyl group of the open-chain reducing sugar attacks the amino group to form an unstable **Schiff base**, which cyclises to an **N-glycosylamine**, and rearranges to a stable **Amadori compound** (in the case of aldoses) or **Heyns compound** (in the case of ketoses).

**Stage 2 — Intermediate Degradation:**
The Amadori compound dehydrates to form **3-deoxyglucosone, furfurals, reductones, and dicarbonyls**. These reactive intermediates participate in **Strecker degradation** with free amino acids, releasing **Strecker aldehydes** (e.g., 3-methylbutanal from leucine, phenylacetaldehyde from phenylalanine) and CO₂. Pyrazines, oxazoles, and thiazoles — the key "roasted" aroma compounds of snacks — are formed in this stage.

**Stage 3 — Final Polymerisation:**
Aldehydes, furfurals, pyrroles, and thiophenes polymerise to form **melanoidins**, the high-molecular-weight brown nitrogenous pigments responsible for the characteristic golden-brown colour of fried or baked snacks.

**Engineering controls:** Maillard reaction is accelerated by **pH > 7**, **temperature > 140 °C**, and **water activity ($a_w$) between 0.6 and 0.9**. In extruder-cooked snacks, the barrel temperature profile and moisture injection points are tuned to optimise Maillard-derived flavour while avoiding burnt notes.

---

### **📝 Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the various types of flavouring agents used in snack food industries. Differentiate between natural, nature-identical, and artificial flavours with examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the techniques of flavour encapsulation. What are the advantages of using encapsulated flavours in snack formulations? *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-B (a):**

Flavouring agents are classified based on origin and regulatory status:

**1. Natural Flavours** — Derived from plant or animal sources via physical (extraction, distillation), enzymatic, or microbial processes. Examples: vanilla extract from *Vanilla planifolia*, peppermint oil from *Mentha piperita*, citrus oils from orange/lemon peels, and meat hydrolysates.

**2. Nature-Identical Flavours** — Chemically synthesised substances that are **chemically identical** to a flavour compound found in nature. Example: **Vanillin** synthesised from guaiacol is nature-identical, while **ethyl vanillin** (which has no natural counterpart) is artificial.

**3. Artificial Flavours** — Synthesised compounds that have **no counterpart in nature**. Example: **Ethyl vanillin** (3-ethyl analogue of vanillin, ~3× more potent).

**4. Process (Reaction) Flavours** — Produced by **heating precursor mixtures** (amino acids + reducing sugars, or hydrolysed vegetable protein + thiamine) to generate Maillard-type aroma compounds. Examples: roasted peanut flavour, beefy savoury notes.

**5. Smoke Flavours** — Condensates of natural wood smoke, used in barbecue snack coatings.

**6. Encapsulated Flavours** — Flavour compounds trapped within a protective matrix (maltodextrin, gum arabic, modified starch, β-cyclodextrin) for controlled release.

| Type | Regulatory Perception | Cost | Potency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural | Premium / clean-label | High | Variable |
| Nature-Identical | Acceptable | Moderate | Consistent |
| Artificial | Restricted labelling | Low | High |

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-B (b):**

**Flavour encapsulation** is the process of entrapping volatile flavour compounds within a protective wall material to form microcapsules (typically 10–100 µm diameter). The objectives are: protection from oxidation, controlled release, ease of handling, and conversion of liquids to free-flowing powders.

**Common encapsulation techniques:**

1. **Spray Drying** — Most widely used. Flavour emulsion (in maltodextrin/gum arabic solution) is atomised in a hot chamber; water evaporates, leaving a solid matrix entrapping the flavour. ~90% of encapsulated food flavours use this method.
2. **Spray Cooling / Spray Chilling** — For lipid-soluble flavours. Flavour is emulsified in melted fat/wax and sprayed into a cool chamber; solidifies as a fat-coated particle.
3. **Extrusion** — Flavour is mixed with a carbohydrate melt and forced through a die; flashing of vapour creates porous glassy matrices.
4. **Coacervation** — Complex coacervation using gelatin + gum arabic; pH adjustment forms a coacervate around the flavour droplet. Used for "burst-release" chewing gum.
5. **β-Cyclodextrin Inclusion Complexation** — Cyclodextrin's hydrophobic cavity traps hydrophobic flavour molecules (e.g., menthol, vanillin) inside the toroidal ring.
6. **Freeze Drying (Lyophilisation)** — For heat-sensitive flavours; expensive but high quality.

**Advantages in snack formulations:**

- **Protection from heat, light, and oxidation** during storage.
- **Sustained release** in the mouth or upon microwave heating.
- **Conversion of volatile liquids to free-flowing powders** (easier dosing in dry seasoning premixes).
- **Reduced flavour loss** during high-temperature processing (e.g., microwave popcorn).
- **Masking of off-notes** from protein hydrolysates.
- **Improved worker safety** (less inhalation of liquid vapours).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Writing "PFA Rule 26" instead of FSSAI 2011 Regulation 3.1.10** — PFA was repealed in 2011. Use FSSAI 2011. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing Tartrazine with Sunset Yellow FCF** — Tartrazine is yellow (CI 19140); Sunset Yellow is orange (CI 15985). (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Writing "Maillard reaction involves caramelisation"** — They are **DIFFERENT**. Maillard requires amino acids; caramelisation does NOT. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 4. **Omitting the Strecker degradation step** — Examiners expect all 5 steps (S-A-D-S-P) in a 7-mark Maillard question. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 5. **Quoting CI 15985 for Tartrazine** — Wrong index. Memorise the Colour Index numbers. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Using "Ponceau 4R" and "Carmoisine" interchangeably** — They are **different** dyes; Carmoisine is NOT permitted in India. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 7. **Forgetting to mention melanoidins** when discussing Maillard colour generation. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 8. **Saying "vanillin is artificial"** — Vanillin is **nature-identical**; **ethyl vanillin** is artificial. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **FSSAI 2011** replaced PFA 1954 — use the newer regulation in 2024 answers.
- **8 synthetic colours** are permitted in India: Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow FCF, Erythrosine, Ponceau 4R, Allura Red AC, Brilliant Blue FCF, Indigo Carmine, Fast Green FCF.
- **Maximum limit:** **100 ppm** (mg/kg) for individual or combined synthetic colours in snacks.
- **5 basic tastes:** Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, **Umami** (5th, added in 1985 by Japanese researchers — Kikunae Ikeda).
- **Maillard** = reducing sugar + amino acid (needs protein/amino N).
- **Caramelisation** = sugar alone, no amino acid, temperature > 160 °C.
- **Melanoidins** = the brown polymers of Maillard Stage 3.
- **Strecker aldehydes** = one carbon less than the parent amino acid; release CO₂.
- **Process flavours** are produced by **Maillard-type reactions** under controlled conditions.
- **Flavour encapsulation** — most common technique: **spray drying** with maltodextrin/gum arabic.
- **Annatto** = carotenoid, oil-soluble, used in cheese snacks.
- **Anthocyanins** are **pH-sensitive** (red at acidic, blue at alkaline).
- **Chlorophyll** turns into **pheophytin** (olive-brown) on heating — magnesium is replaced by hydrogen.
- **Betalains** = nitrogen-containing, found in beetroot and *Bougainvillea*.
- **Vanillin = nature-identical**; **Ethyl vanillin = artificial**.
- **Tinctorial strength** of synthetic dyes is much higher than natural colourants.
- **Cross-modal perception:** red → sweet; green → sour; yellow → salty; white → bland. Use this in 7-mark answers.
- **Post-process application** of colour and flavour is standard to avoid thermal loss.
- **"Clean label" trend** is driving replacement of synthetic with natural/nature-identical in premium snack segments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 — Colouring and Flavouring Notes**
> Revise the **8 permitted synthetic colours**, the **5-step Maillard pathway (S-A-D-S-P)**, the **4 flavour classes** (Natural / Nature-Identical / Artificial / Process), and the **6 encapsulation techniques** for a guaranteed high score in the KTU 2024 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> *Packaging* in the context of snack food technology is defined as the coordinated system of preparing food products (especially ready-to-eat extruded snacks, fried namkeen, baked crackers, popcorn, and confectionery-based snacks) for transport, distribution, storage, retailing, and end-use, accomplished through the use of suitable barrier materials, protective technologies, and informational labelling that collectively preserve sensory quality, ensure food safety, extend shelf life, and provide consumer convenience while complying with regulatory frameworks such as **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)**, **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, and **USFDA** where applicable.

Packaging is not merely a container — under the **2024 NEP-aligned OBE framework**, it is treated as an integrated *Unit Operation* of food preservation, sitting alongside drying, frying, and extrusion in the snack production workflow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snack Suitcase" Mental Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of packaging as a *Snack Suitcase*** you pack for a long trip. Just as a good suitcase must (1) protect the contents from rain (moisture barrier), (2) survive being tossed around (mechanical strength), (3) keep air out so clothes don't smell musty (oxygen barrier), (4) tell the airport staff what's inside (labelling), and (5) be easy to open at your destination (consumer convenience) — a snack package must do exactly the same five jobs simultaneously.

The five non-negotiable functions of any snack package, remembered by the acronym **PILOT**:

| Letter | Function | Real-World Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **P** | **Preservation** | Stops moisture gain, oil oxidation, microbial growth |
| **I** | **Information** | Lists ingredients, nutrition, batch code, FSSAI logo |
| **L** | **Logistics** | Survives stacking, vibration, transport, palletization |
| **O** | **Obstruction (Barrier)** | Blocks O₂, light, water vapour, aroma loss |
| **T** | **Tamper-evident & Touch** | Shows tampering; feels good in the hand |

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Snack Packaging

- **Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)** — measured in **g/m²·day** at specified T & RH.
- **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** — measured in **cc/m²·day·atm** or **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹**.
- **Migration Limit (ML)** — measured in **mg/dm²** or **mg/kg food simulant** as per **EU 10/2011** / **FSSAI** norms.
- **Shelf life (tₛ)** — measured in **days / weeks / months** at storage temperature.
- Standard test conditions: **23 °C and 65 % RH** (ASTM D3985 / D6701) or **38 °C and 90 % RH** (tropical).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Packaging — Three Functional Tiers

> [!NOTE]
> Snack packaging is engineered as a **3-tier hierarchy**. Examiners frequently test this hierarchy — memorize it.

**Tier 1 — Primary Packaging:** Direct contact with the snack (e.g., the inner metallized film of a chips pouch). Must be food-grade, migration-safe.

**Tier 2 — Secondary Packaging:** Groups multiple primary units (e.g., a printed cardboard carton holding 12 chip bags). Mainly for branding and retail display.

**Tier 3 — Tertiary Packaging:** Outer transport packaging (corrugated fibreboard boxes, shrink-wrapped pallets). Mainly for logistics and warehousing.

### 2.2 Functional Requirements of a Snack Package

A high-quality snack package must satisfy **eight engineering criteria**:

1. **Moisture barrier** — critical for crackers, papad, fried namkeen (target *a*w ≤ 0.6).
2. **Oxygen barrier** — critical for fried snacks prone to rancidity.
3. **Light barrier** — UV + visible light accelerates lipid oxidation; metallized films help.
4. **Aroma retention** — flavour volatiles must not escape.
5. **Mechanical strength** — resists puncture from sharp snack edges (e.g., nacho corners).
6. **Heat sealability** — hermetic sealing at **140–180 °C** for 1–3 seconds.
7. **Grease/oil resistance** — prevents oil stain (oily snacks like murukku, chakli).
8. **Printability & clarity** — for marketing graphics and consumer visibility.

### 2.3 Packaging Materials — The Engineer's Toolkit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for plastic polymers used in snacks — "PEPPP AL"**
> **P**E, **P**P, **P**ET, **P**VDC, **P**A (Nylon) — **A**luminium foil — **L**DPE/EVA copolymers.

| Material | Common Abbreviation | Key Property | Typical Snack Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Low-Density Polyethylene | **LDPE** | Excellent moisture barrier, heat-sealable | Inner sealant layer of chip bags |
| Linear Low-Density Polyethylene | **LLDPE** | High tear & puncture resistance | Heavy-duty snack sacks |
| Polypropylene | **PP** | Good clarity, high melting point (160 °C) | Twist wraps for toffees, candy |
| Polyethylene Terephthalate | **PET** | High tensile strength, glossy | Bottled snacks, rigid trays |
| Polyvinylidene Chloride | **PVDC** | Outstanding O₂ barrier | Coated layer in multi-layer films |
| Oriented Polypropylene | **OPP** | Excellent clarity, moderate barrier | Outer print layer of namkeen pouches |
| Metallized Polyester | **Met-PET** | Ultra-high O₂ + light barrier | Lays chips, alu-pouch for fried snacks |
| Aluminium Foil | **Al-foil** | Absolute (zero) permeability | Lamination in 3-ply snack pouches |
| Polyamide (Nylon 6) | **PA** | Puncture resistance, aroma barrier | Vacuum pouches for nuts, sev |
| Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate | **EVA** | Flexible, low-T sealability | Inner sealant for chocolate, soft snacks |
| Paper / Cardboard | — | Renewable, printable, breathable | Outer cartons, popcorn tubs |
| Glass | — | Inert, hermetic | Premium pickles, dry-fruit snacks |
| Metal (Tinplate, Al-can) | — | Total barrier, stackable | Canned nuts, baked snack tins |

### 2.4 Common Packaging Formats for Snacks

- **Single-layer Pouches:** LDPE/PP — for low-shelf-life traditional snacks.
- **Two-ply Laminated Pouches:** OPP/PE or PET/PE — namkeen, bhujia.
- **Three-ply Laminated Pouches:** PET/Al-foil/PE — chips, fried snacks with long shelf life.
- **Four-ply Vacuum Pouches:** PA/PE or PET/PVDC/PA/PE — nuts, roasted snacks.
- **Stand-up Pouches (SUP):** With bottom gusset — premium snack retail packs.
- **Flow-wrap:** OPP/PP overwrap — candy bars, energy bars.
- **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS):** Vertical or Horizontal — bulk chips in factories.
- **Aseptic Cartons:** Tetra-style — flavoured milk-based snacks.

### 2.5 Packaging Technologies Specific to Snack Preservation

#### A. Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Replacing the in-pack atmosphere with a controlled gas mixture (typically **N₂ + CO₂**, sometimes with O₂ exclusion) to suppress microbial and oxidative spoilage.

A typical snack MAP gas blend:

$$ \text{Blended Gas Composition (Potato Chips)} \;\Rightarrow\; \approx 70\% \, \text{N}_2 \; + \; 30\% \, \text{CO}_2 $$

- **N₂** is inert — displaces O₂, prevents oxidative rancidity, prevents pouch collapse.
- **CO₂** is bacteriostatic and fungistatic — extends microbial shelf life.
- **O₂** is held below **< 1–2 %** to suppress oxidation of fried snacks.

#### B. Vacuum Packaging

Air is mechanically evacuated to **O₂ residual ≤ 1 %**. Used for nuts, roasted chana, murukku, sev. Cannot be used for brittle snacks (chips) — they crush under atmospheric pressure differential.

#### C. Nitrogen Flushing

Soft variant of MAP where pure N₂ is flushed to displace air — same principle without the CO₂ bacteriostatic effect. Used for **potato wafers and namkeen** at industrial scale.

#### D. Active Packaging (Emerging)

- **Oxygen scavengers** (iron-based sachets) — chemically absorb residual O₂.
- **Moisture absorbers** (silica gel, calcium oxide) — keep *a*w low.
- **Antimicrobial films** (chitosan, Ag-zeolite, essential oils).

### 2.6 Shelf-Life Estimation — The Math Behind the Snacks

The shelf life of a moisture-sensitive snack is governed by moisture gain through the package barrier until a critical water activity (*a*w,crit) is reached at which the product becomes unacceptable.

$$ t_s \;=\; \frac{W_c \cdot m_s}{WVTR \cdot A} $$

Where:
- $t_s$ = shelf life in **days**
- $W_c$ = critical moisture content gain (fraction, decimal)
- $m_s$ = mass of snack in package (g)
- $WVTR$ = water vapour transmission rate of package (g/m²·day)
- $A$ = area of package (m²)
- $a_{w,crit}$ = critical water activity (typical snack limit ≈ **0.60–0.65**)

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Variables / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Shelf life from WVTR | $t_s = \dfrac{W_c \cdot m_s}{WVTR \cdot A}$ | $t_s$ in days; $WVTR$ in g/m²·day |
| 2 | Permeability (general) | $P = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{\Delta p \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $P$ in mol·m/m²·s·Pa |
| 3 | Henry's Law for gas in polymer | $C = S \cdot p$ | $S$ = solubility coeff. |
| 4 | Fick's 1st Law (steady state) | $J = -D \, \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $J$ = flux, $D$ = diffusivity |
| 5 | Ideal Gas Law (for MAP headspace) | $pV = nRT$ | $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| 6 | Arrhenius (oxidation rate) | $k = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$ in kJ/mol |
| 7 | Q₁₀ rule (temperature acceleration) | $Q_{10} = \dfrac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} \approx 2$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | MAP O₂ residual | $O_2^\% = \dfrac{V_{O_2}}{V_{headspace}} \times 100$ | Target ≤ 1–2 % |
| 9 | Package volume for gas mix | $V_i = \dfrac{n_i RT}{p}$ | $V_i$ = partial volume of gas *i* |
| 10 | Migration limit (overall) | $ML \leq 10\,\text{mg/dm}^2$ (EU 10/2011) | Standard regulatory ceiling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions commonly tested in KTU:**
> - For **chips/crackers**: $a_{w,crit} \approx 0.65$
> - For **fried namkeen**: OTR requirement ≤ **50 cm³/m²·day·atm**
> - For **roasted nuts**: WVTR requirement ≤ **5 g/m²·day**
> - Sealing temperature window for LDPE: **140 – 180 °C**, dwell **1 – 3 s**, pressure **2 – 4 bar**

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Indian snack industry** (estimated market > ₹ 40,000 crore in 2024), the **3-ply laminate PET/Al-foil/PE** is the workhorse for chips, namkeen, and mixture. The Al-foil middle layer gives near-zero O₂ and WVTR, the PET gives mechanical strength and printability, and the PE inner layer gives hermetic heat-sealability. Variations replace Al-foil with **Met-PET** for cost-effective barrier.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shelf-Life Equation from Fick's First Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** The shelf-life formula $t_s = (W_c \cdot m_s)/(WVTR \cdot A)$ is *the* most-asked derivation in snack packaging questions. Practice writing it from scratch.

**Starting Point — Fick's First Law (1-D, steady state):**

$$ J \;=\; -D \, \frac{dC}{dx} $$

**Step 1 —** Recognise that the *steady-state moisture flux* $J$ through the packaging film equals the moisture gain rate of the snack, divided by the package area:

$$ J \;=\; \frac{1}{A}\frac{dm}{dt} $$

Where:
- $J$ = moisture flux (g/m²·day)
- $dm/dt$ = rate of moisture entering the snack (g/day)
- $A$ = package area (m²)

**Step 2 —** By definition of WVTR under standard conditions (T = 38 °C, RH = 90 % outside; T = 25 °C, RH = 0 % inside — for a moisture-sensitive snack considered a *perfect moisture sink* until $a_w$ reaches the critical value):

$$ WVTR \;\equiv\; \frac{1}{A}\frac{dm}{dt} \bigg\vert_{\text{steady}} $$

**Step 3 —** Rearrange for total moisture gained over shelf life:

$$ dm \;=\; WVTR \cdot A \cdot dt $$

**Step 4 —** Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = t_s$, and moisture gain from $0$ to critical moisture gain $W_c \cdot m_s$:

$$ \int_{0}^{W_c m_s} dm \;=\; WVTR \cdot A \int_{0}^{t_s} dt $$

**Step 5 —** Evaluate the integrals:

$$ W_c \cdot m_s \;=\; WVTR \cdot A \cdot t_s $$

**Step 6 —** Solve for shelf life $t_s$:

$$ \boxed{\,t_s \;=\; \frac{W_c \, m_s}{WVTR \cdot A}\,} $$

The same derivation applied to **oxygen transmission** gives:

$$ t_{s,\,O_2} \;=\; \frac{[O_2]_{crit} \cdot V_{headspace}}{OTR \cdot A} $$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Solved Analytically

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark style):** A roasted chana snack of mass **200 g** is packed in a stand-up pouch of total area **0.04 m²**.
