The critical moisture content gain (beyond which the snack loses crispness) is **0.03 g water / g snack**. The laminate has a **WVTR of 2.5 g/m²·day** at 38 °C / 90 % RH. Estimate the shelf life.

**Step 1 —** Identify variables:
- $m_s = 200$ g
- $W_c = 0.03$ (dimensionless)
- $WVTR = 2.5$ g/m²·day
- $A = 0.04$ m²

**Step 2 —** Compute critical total moisture gain:

$$ \Delta m \;=\; W_c \cdot m_s \;=\; 0.03 \times 200 \;=\; 6\;\text{g} $$

**Step 3 —** Apply the shelf-life formula:

$$ t_s \;=\; \frac{W_c \cdot m_s}{WVTR \cdot A} $$

$$ t_s \;=\; \frac{0.03 \times 200}{2.5 \times 0.04} $$

$$ t_s \;=\; \frac{6.0}{0.10} $$

$$ \boxed{\,t_s \;=\; 60\;\text{days}\,} $$

**Step 4 —** Sanity check using Q₁₀ rule at 25 °C: If storage temperature drops from 38 °C to 25 °C, with $Q_{10} \approx 2$:

$$ t_s (25°C) \;\approx\; 60 \times 2^{\,(38-25)/10} \;\approx\; 60 \times 2^{1.3} \;\approx\; 60 \times 2.46 \;\approx\; 148\;\text{days} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Reducing storage temperature by just **13 °C** extends shelf life by ~2.5×. This is the *cold-chain rationale* behind refrigerated snack aisles.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Materials vs. Barrier Performance

| Material | WVTR (g/m²·day, 38 °C / 90 % RH) | OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹) | Relative Cost | Snacks Best Suited |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| LDPE (50 µm) | 6 – 12 | 1500 – 3000 | Low | Loose namkeen, short shelf-life |
| OPP (20 µm) | 4 – 8 | 800 – 1500 | Low | Outer layer; overwrap |
| PET (12 µm) | 18 – 25 | 90 – 120 | Medium | Print layer; structural |
| Met-PET (12 µm) | **< 1** | **< 1.5** | Medium-High | Lays chips, fried namkeen |
| Al-foil (9 µm) | **< 0.01** | **< 0.01** | High | Long-shelf-life snack pouches |
| PVDC-coated PET | 1 – 3 | 5 – 15 | High | Premium nuts, cookies |
| PA (Nylon 6, 15 µm) | 60 – 90 | 50 – 80 | Medium | Vacuum pouches |
| EVOH (5 µm) | 5 – 10 | 0.5 – 3 | High | Co-extruded barrier layer |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Migration Calculation (for FSSAI / EU 10/2011 Compliance)

**Step 1 —** Identify overall migration limit (OML) per EU 10/2011:
$$ OML_{\max} = 10\;\text{mg/dm}^2 $$

**Step 2 —** Identify specific migration limits (SML) for substances of concern (e.g., for vinyl chloride monomer):
$$ SML_{VC} = 0.01\;\text{mg/kg}\;\text{food} $$

**Step 3 —** Conduct migration test in food simulant (e.g., 10 % ethanol for aqueous snacks, 3 % acetic acid for acidic, olive oil for fatty snacks) at **10 days / 40 °C** or **10 days / 60 °C** depending on intended use.

**Step 4 —** Compare migrated quantity with OML and SML; reject material if either is breached.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Tier Packaging Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef tier1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b23a48,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tier2 fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#3a7d44,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tier3 fill:#bee1e6,stroke:#1d4e89,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef snack fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    snackA[SNACK PRODUCT]:::snack

    subgraph T1["TIER 1 PRIMARY DIRECT CONTACT"]
        direction LR
        p1a["Inner sealant LDPE 30 to 60 micrometre"]:::tier1
        p1b["Barrier layer Met PET or Al foil"]:::tier1
        p1c["Outer print layer OPP or PET"]:::tier1
    end

    subgraph T2["TIER 2 SECONDARY GROUPING"]
        direction LR
        p2a["Printed carton board"]:::tier2
        p2b["Shrink wrap collation"]:::tier2
    end

    subgraph T3["TIER 3 TERTIARY TRANSPORT"]
        direction LR
        p3a["Corrugated fibreboard box"]:::tier3
        p3b["Stretch wrap pallet"]:::tier3
    end

    snackA --> p1c
    p1c --> p1b
    p1b --> p1a
    p1a --> T2
    T2 --> T3
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram of Industrial Snack Packaging Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef qual fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#a0522d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef crit fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A[Raw snack from fryer or oven]:::step
    B[Cooling conveyor T below 30 degree C]:::step
    C[Flavour drum seasoning]:::step
    D[Quality check metal detector weight check]:::qual
    E[Multihead weigher 10 to 14 heads]:::step
    F[Form Fill Seal machine vertical]:::crit
    G{N2 flushing OR MAP gas injection}:::crit
    H[Heat seal bar 140 to 180 degree C]:::step
    I[Date coding and batch printing]:::step
    J[Cartoning secondary pack]:::step
    K[Case packing tertiary]:::step
    L[Palletizing and stretch wrap]:::step
    M[Warehouse dispatch]:::step

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K --> L --> M
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-Ply Laminated Pouch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef outer fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1a237e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef inner fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef env fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT"]
        O2[O2 in air 21 percent]:::env
        H2O[Moisture in air]:::env
        LIGHT[Light UV and visible]:::env
        ODOUR[External odours]:::env
    end

    subgraph POUCH["3 PLY LAMINATE SNACK POUCH"]
        direction LR
        L1["OUTER LAYER OPP or PET 12 to 20 micrometre PRINTABILITY AND MECHANICAL STRENGTH"]:::outer
        L2["MIDDLE LAYER Al FOIL or Met PET 9 to 12 micrometre BARRIER AGAINST O2 WVTR LIGHT"]:::mid
        L3["INNER LAYER LDPE or EVA 30 to 60 micrometre HEAT SEAL FOOD CONTACT"]:::inner
        L1 --> L2 --> L3
    end

    subgraph SNK["SNACK INTERIOR"]
        P[Snack product low aw protected]:::env
    end

    O2 --> L1
    H2O --> L1
    LIGHT --> L1
    ODOUR --> L1
    L3 --> P
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Snack Package

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef q fill:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef d fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    Q1{Q1 Shelf life requirement}:::q
    Q2{Q2 Snacks are fried oily or baked dry}:::q
    Q3{Q3 Cost ceiling in INR per pack}:::q

    D1[D1 LDPE or PP single layer short shelf life]:::d
    D2[D2 PET PE two ply namkeen mi]:::d
    D3[D3 PET Al foil PE three ply fried chips long shelf]:::d
    D4[D4 Met PET PE economic barrier medium shelf]:::d
    D5[D5 Vacuum PA PE bag nuts sev]:::d
    D6[D6 MAP N2 CO2 gas flushed chips]:::d

    Q1 -->|less than 1 month| Q3
    Q1 -->|1 to 6 months| Q2
    Q1 -->|more than 6 months| D3
    Q2 -->|fried oily| D2
    Q2 -->|baked dry crisp| Q3
    Q2 -->|need O2 control| D6
    Q2 -->|vacuum strong pouch| D5
    Q3 -->|economy| D1
    Q3 -->|mid range| D4
    Q3 -->|premium| D3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**. Mention the role of **N₂** and **CO₂** in snack packaging.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **MAP** is a preservation technique in which the air inside a snack package is replaced with a controlled gas mixture to extend shelf life.
> - **Nitrogen (N₂)** — inert filler gas; displaces O₂, prevents oxidative rancidity of fried snacks, prevents pouch collapse under vacuum.
> - **Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — bacteriostatic and fungistatic; suppresses microbial growth in moisture-containing snacks.
> - Typical snack blend: **~70 % N₂ + ~30 % CO₂**, with residual O₂ held **below 1–2 %**.
> - (3 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks roles of gases)

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` List any **six functions** of packaging in snack food technology.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Preservation** of sensory and nutritional quality.
2. **Containment** for handling and transport.
3. **Protection** against moisture, O₂, light, microbes.
4. **Information** — ingredients, nutrition, batch, FSSAI label.
5. **Convenience** — easy open, reseal, portion control.
6. **Marketing** — branding and consumer appeal.
*(Any six; ½ mark each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Each question is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`** Answer the following:

**(a)** Classify the **different packaging materials** used in snack food industry. Discuss the properties and applications of any **three multi-layer laminates** commonly used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **vacuum packaging** and **nitrogen flushing** with neat diagrams. Compare their suitability for chips vs roasted nuts. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Classification + 3 multi-layer laminates **(7 marks)**

**Classification** *(2 marks):*

- **Flexible packaging** — films, pouches, sachets (LDPE, PP, PET, Al-foil laminates).
- **Rigid packaging** — bottles, jars, cans, trays (PET, glass, tinplate, Al).
- **Semi-rigid packaging** — tubs, cups, trays (PP, PS, moulded pulp).

**Three multi-layer laminates** *(5 marks; ~1.5 marks each):*

1. **PET / Al-foil / PE (3-ply):** Outer PET gives printability + mechanical strength; middle Al-foil gives near-zero O₂/WVTR barrier; inner PE gives hermetic heat-seal. Used for potato chips, fried namkeen — shelf life 6–12 months.

2. **OPP / Met-PET / PE:** Cost-effective alternative to Al-foil; Met-PET provides barrier via vacuum-deposited aluminium (typically 30–50 nm). Used for mid-shelf-life snacks like bhujia, mixture — shelf life 3–6 months.

3. **PA / PE (vacuum pouch):** Outer PA (Nylon 6) provides puncture resistance and aroma barrier; inner PE provides sealability. Used for vacuum-packed nuts, roasted chana, sev — shelf life 4–8 months refrigerated.

**(b)** Vacuum vs Nitrogen flushing **(7 marks)**

**Vacuum Packaging** *(3 marks):*
- Air mechanically evacuated; pouch sealed hermetically.
- O₂ residual ≤ 1 %; inhibits aerobic microbes + oxidation.
- **Limitation:** Brittle snacks (chips, papad) **crush** under 1 atm external pressure → not suitable.

**Nitrogen Flushing (MAP variant)** *(3 marks):*
- Pure N₂ flushed to displace O₂; pouch sealed while N₂ fills.
- N₂ is inert; no oxidation; pouch **does not collapse** because internal pressure ~ equals external.
- **Suitability:** Ideal for **chips** — crispness retained; bulk volume preserved.

**Comparison table** *(1 mark):*

| Property | Vacuum | N₂ Flush |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Chip (brittle) | Not suitable (crushes) | Highly suitable |
| Roasted nuts | Highly suitable | Suitable |
| Pouch shape | Wrinkled, flat | Puffy, attractive |
| Equipment cost | Lower | Higher |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Answer the following:

**(a)** What are the **functional requirements** of a snack food package? Discuss the importance of **moisture barrier and oxygen barrier** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **200 g** snack is packed in a pouch of total surface area **0.05 m²**. The critical moisture gain is **0.04 g water per g snack** beyond which it loses crispness. The laminate has a **WVTR of 3 g/m²·day**. Calculate the **shelf life in days**. If the same snack is stored at a temperature **10 °C lower** than the test condition, estimate the new shelf life using **Q₁₀ = 2**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Functional requirements + barrier discussion **(7 marks)**

**Eight functional requirements** *(4 marks; ½ mark each):*

1. Containment, 2. Protection from physical damage, 3. Moisture barrier, 4. Oxygen barrier, 5. Light barrier, 6. Microbial protection, 7. Information/labeling, 8. Convenience & appeal.

**Moisture barrier** *(1.5 marks):*
- Critical because most snacks (chips, crackers, papad, namkeen) must remain **crisp**.
- Loss of crispness occurs when $a_w$ rises above **0.60–0.65**.
- Achieved using **Al-foil, Met-PET, PVDC-coated films, EVOH co-extrusions**.

**Oxygen barrier** *(1.5 marks):*
- Critical because fried snacks contain **PUFA-rich oils** that oxidize → rancidity, off-flavours.
- Oxidation kinetics: $k = k_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$ — accelerated at higher temperature.
- Achieved using **Al-foil (OTR ≈ 0)**, **Met-PET (OTR < 1.5)**, **N₂ flushing / MAP**.

**(b)** Shelf-life numerical **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 —** Identify variables *(1 mark):*
- $m_s = 200$ g, $A = 0.05$ m², $W_c = 0.04$, $WVTR = 3$ g/m²·day.

**Step 2 —** Apply shelf-life formula *(3 marks):*

$$ t_s = \frac{W_c \cdot m_s}{WVTR \cdot A} $$

$$ t_s = \frac{0.04 \times 200}{3 \times 0.05} $$

$$ t_s = \frac{8.0}{0.15} $$

$$ \boxed{t_s = 53.33\;\text{days} \approx 53\;\text{days}} $$

**Step 3 —** Apply Q₁₀ correction *(3 marks):*

$$ t_{s,new} = t_{s,old} \times Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10} $$

$$ t_{s,new} = 53.33 \times 2^{(10/10)} $$

$$ t_{s,new} = 53.33 \times 2 $$

$$ \boxed{t_{s,new} \approx 107\;\text{days}} $$

**Inference:** A drop of 10 °C doubles the shelf life of the moisture-sensitive snack — demonstrating the *Arrhenius / Q₁₀* effect.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up in $t_s$ formula:** Many students write $t_s = (WVTR \cdot A)/(W_c \cdot m_s)$ — *inverted*. **Memorize:** "moisture gain on top, barrier-area on bottom."
> 2. **Forgetting that Q₁₀ uses $\Delta T$ in °C, not Kelvin.** Both are numerically identical for differences — but explicitly state the unit to score the method mark.
> 3. **Confusing MAP with vacuum packaging.** MAP = gas-blend replaced; Vacuum = air evacuated. Examiners deduct 1 mark for the swap.
> 4. **Omitting FSSAI compliance** in Part A answers about labelling. Always mention the **14-digit FSSAI License number** for any branded snack.
> 5. **Confusing OTR with WVTR.** Oxygen barrier ≠ moisture barrier. A film good for one may be poor for the other.
> 6. **Numerical errors:** Forgetting to convert cm² to m² in area. Always cross-check: $1\;\text{m}^2 = 10{,}000\;\text{cm}^2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for the Packaging topic (Module 3):**

- ✅ Packaging is an **integrated unit operation**, not a passive step.
- ✅ Three functional tiers: **Primary → Secondary → Tertiary**.
- ✅ Eight functional requirements — **containment, protection, moisture, O₂, light, microbes, info, convenience.**
- ✅ **Mnemonic "PEPPP AL"** — polymers most used in snack packaging.
- ✅ **3-ply PET / Al-foil / PE** is the industry workhorse for fried snacks.
- ✅ **MAP** uses N₂ + CO₂; **Vacuum** removes air; **N₂ flush** uses pure N₂.
- ✅ **$a_{w,crit}$** for crisp snacks ≈ **0.60–0.65**.
- ✅ **$t_s = (W_c \cdot m_s)/(WVTR \cdot A)$** — the key shelf-life formula.
- ✅ **Q₁₀ ≈ 2** — every 10 °C drop roughly doubles shelf life.
- ✅ **WVTR** unit: **g/m²·day**; **OTR** unit: **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹**.
- ✅ Migration limits: **OML ≤ 10 mg/dm²** per EU 10/2011.
- ✅ **FSSAI 14-digit license number** mandatory on every branded snack package.
- ✅ **Brittle snacks (chips) → N₂ flush**, not vacuum (prevents crushing).
- ✅ **Oily snacks (murukku, sev) → PA/PE or Met-PET/PE** for oil + O₂ barrier.
- ✅ **Al-foil** gives **near-zero** permeability for both O₂ and water vapour.
- ✅ Always state **sealing conditions**: 140–180 °C, 1–3 s, 2–4 bar for LDPE inner seal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machinery, Equipment and Care
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Machinery, Equipment and Care # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Snack Food Technology, **machinery and equipment** refer to the engineered mechanical, thermal, and processing units used to convert raw food ingredients (cereals, tubers, pulses, oils, spices, dairy, and meat) into finished, shelf-stable, organoleptically acceptable snack products. The term *care* encompasses the routine **maintenance, sanitation (CIP – Clean-In-Place), calibration, lubrication, and operator-safety protocols** that preserve the equipment's hygienic, mechanical, and thermal efficiency throughout its service life. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** The syllabus defines *care* not merely as "repair," but as a **preventive, predictive, and total productive maintenance (TPM)** framework combined with **HACCP-aligned sanitation** that ensures food safety, product consistency, and regulatory compliance (FSSAI, USFDA, BIS). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a snack food production line as a **restaurant kitchen on an industrial scale**. Just like a chef needs a sharp knife (which must be honed daily), a calibrated stove (which must be cleaned after every dish), and a reliable oven (which needs its thermostat checked periodically), a snack plant needs: - The **extruder** = the chef's hands shaping the dough. - The **fryer/oven** = the chef's stove or oven. - The **seasoning drum** = the chef's mixing bowl. - The **care routine** = the chef's daily cleaning, sharpening, and maintenance habits. Without daily care, even the most expensive industrial fryer will produce rancid, contaminated chips — just as a dirty kitchen ruins the best recipe. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Benchmark Metric:** A well-maintained snack line aims for an **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) ≥ 85%**, comprising *Availability × Performance × Quality* metrics. World-class plants target **OEE > 90%**. ## 1.3 Core Categories of Snack Food Equipment | Category | Functional Role | Typical Equipment | |---|---|---| | **Size Reduction** | Particle size control | Hammer mills, Roller mills, Disc attrition mills | | **Mixing / Blending** | Homogenization of ingredients | Ribbon blenders, Paddle mixers, Planetary mixers | | **Forming / Shaping** | Product geometry | Extruders (single/twin screw), Rotary moulders, Slitters | | **Cooking** | Gelatinization & expansion | Continuous fryers, Tunnel ovens, Puffing guns, Microwave tunnels | | **Coating / Seasoning** | Flavor application | Tumble drums, Spray coaters, Enrobers | | **Packaging** | Shelf-life & convenience | VFFS, HFFS, Flow wrappers, Pouch sealers | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constant to Remember:** The **thermal death point** for *Salmonella* in moist snack matrices is **70 °C held for 2 minutes**, which dictates the minimum cooking temperature inside any snack cooking equipment (fryer/oven) — a non-negotiable benchmark for FSSAI compliance. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Core Equipment ### 2.1.1 Extruders (Single-Screw & Twin-Screw) **Working Principle:** The extruder is the heart of modern snack (especially puffed / expanded snack) production. Raw material — typically *degermed maize grits*, *wheat flour*, or *rice flour* conditioned to **14–18% moisture** — is fed through a hopper into a rotating screw inside a barrel. The screw performs four sequential functions: 1. **Conveying (Feed Zone):** Transports material from hopper to compression zone. 2. **Compression (Mixing Zone):** Compresses and shears the material against the barrel wall. 3. **Cooking (Kneading Zone):** Generates heat through mechanical shear (1/3 from external heaters, 2/3 from viscous dissipation). 4. **Forming/Expansion (Die Zone):** Forces the cooked melt through a die; sudden pressure drop causes **instantaneous flash-off** of superheated steam → *puffing*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mechanical Energy Conversion:** The Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) input is the single most critical control parameter. ### 2.1.2 Continuous Frying System Snack frying (for potato chips, banana chips, namkeen) is performed in a **continuous belt fryer** where product moves through hot oil (typically **palmolein or groundnut oil at 160–190 °C**). Key zonal divisions: - **Pre-heat zone** (entry) — partial surface drying. - **Frying zone** (middle) — moisture removal & crust formation. - **Vacuum / de-oiling zone** (exit) — steam-stripping residual oil. ### 2.1.3 Tunnel Oven (Baked Snacks) Baked snacks (biscuits, crackers, baked puffs) require a **multi-zone tunnel oven** with three controlled zones: - **Zone 1 (Bottom heat)** — sets the bottom structure. - **Zone 2 (Top + Bottom)** — bulk moisture removal. - **Zone 3 (Top heat / Browning)** — Maillard reaction and color development. ### 2.1.4 Seasoning Drum (Rotary Coater) A hexagonal/octagonal tumbler where the base snack is rotated and sprayed with a slurry of **oil + seasoning + anti-caking agent**. The drum angle (typically **15°–25°** from horizontal) and rotational speed (**8–25 RPM**) control coating uniformity. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** | $\text{SME} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot N}{m}$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Extruder energy input | | **Cook Yield** | $\text{Cook Yield} = \dfrac{W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Frying/oven mass balance | | **Oil Uptake (OU)** | $\text{OU} = \dfrac{O_{\text{final}} - O_{\text{initial}}}{O_{\text{final}}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Quality control on fried snacks | | **Heat Transfer Rate** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\text{W}$ or $\text{J/s}$ | Fryer/oven design | | **Frying Heat Flux** | $q = h \cdot (T_{\text{oil}} - T_{\text{product}})$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Critical frying parameter | | **Laminar Heat Transfer Coefficient (oil side)** | $h = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4} \cdot \dfrac{k}{D}$ | $\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ | Convective side of fryer | | **OEE** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Decimal (×100 for $\%$) | Equipment effectiveness | | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | Decimal | Maintenance KPI | | **Performance (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | Decimal | Speed KPI | | **Quality (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | Decimal | Rejection KPI | | **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | $\text{hours}$ | Reliability KPI | | **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | $\text{hours}$ | Maintainability KPI | | **Power Number (Newtonian)** | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5}$ | Dimensionless | Mixer impeller design | > [!NOTE] > **Where to use these:** *SME* and *Cook Yield* are the most likely KTU numericals; *OEE* and *MTBF* frequently appear as 3-mark definition questions. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SME optimization** (typically **150–400 kJ/kg** for direct-expanded snacks) dictates whether the product becomes *dense (low SME)* or *fluffy high expansion (high SME)* — critical for brand identity. - **Oil Uptake** in fried snacks is controlled by **surface moisture removal rate** — engineering the de-oiling hood reduces oil content from **~35% to <25%**, saving millions in oil cost annually for a mid-scale plant. - **OEE** is the industry gold-standard KPI — every percentage point lost represents **~2,800 hours of lost capacity per year** on a single line. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) in an Extruder We begin with the **fundamental definition** of energy input per unit mass of product processed. ### Step 1: Define Net Power Delivered to the Material The net power transferred to the product by the rotating screw is the **gross motor power minus the no-load (idling) power**, since the latter is dissipated as heat in bearings and screw-barrel friction without contributing to product work. $$ P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{gross}} - P_{\text{idle}} $$ ### Step 2: Convert Mass Flow Rate to Throughput If $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of product in **kg/s**, the energy delivered *per kilogram* of product is simply: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{P_{\text{net}}}{\dot{m}} $$ ### Step 3: Express in Standard Industry Form (Shaft Speed Form) The screw motor delivers torque $\tau$ (N·m) at rotational speed $N$ (rad/s). Power equals torque times angular velocity. $$ P_{\text{net}} = \tau \cdot \omega = \tau \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} $$ Substituting back: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot 2\pi N}{60 \,\dot{m}} $$ ### Step 4: Rearranging into the canonical KTU form $$ \boxed{\text{SME} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot N}{\dot{m}}} $$ where $\tau$ is in N·m, $N$ in rad/s, and $\dot{m}$ in kg/s gives **J/kg**; multiply by $10^{-3}$ to express as **kJ/kg**. ### Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU Typical) **Given:** A single-screw extruder operates at $N = 250$ rad/s with a net torque $\tau = 80$ N·m. The throughput $\dot{m} = 0.5$ kg/s. **Solution:** $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{80 \times 250}{0.5} $$ $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{0.5} = 40{,}000 \text{ J/kg} $$ Converting: $$ \text{SME} = 40 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ This is a *low-SME* condition typical of a **dense, less-expanded snack** (e.g., a third-generation pellet-based snack). To get a fully expanded puffed snack, we would need SME in the range **150–400 kJ/kg**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) Decomposition ### Step 1: Define Availability Availability measures *unplanned downtime* (equipment failures + changeovers) and is the simplest component: $$ A = \dfrac{T_{\text{running}}}{T_{\text{planned}}} $$ ### Step 2: Define Performance Performance captures *speed losses* (running slower than ideal cycle time): $$ P = \dfrac{(C_{\text{ideal}} \times N_{\text{units}})}{T_{\text{running}}} $$ where $C_{\text{ideal}}$ = ideal cycle time per unit, $N_{\text{units}}$ = total units produced (good + reject). ### Step 3: Define Quality Quality captures the *rejection rate*: $$ Q = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{units}}} $$ ### Step 4: Combine into OEE The three components are **multiplicatively independent** — losing 5% in each does not give a 15% total loss; it gives: $$ \text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q $$ ### Step 5: Numerical Example A line runs 480 min in an 8-hour shift, loses 30 min to changeovers, runs at 90% of ideal speed, and produces 9,500 good pieces out of 10,000. $$ A = \dfrac{450}{480} = 0.9375 $$ $$ P = 0.90 $$ $$ Q = \dfrac{9500}{10000} = 0.95 $$ $$ \text{OEE} = 0.9375 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.8016 = 80.16\% $$ **Interpretation:** This plant falls in the *"Typical"* category (60–85%). World-class snack plants target **>85%**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Selection for Snack Categories | Snack Type | Primary Equipment | Critical Process Parameter | Typical OEE Target | |---|---|---|---| | **Potato chips** | Continuous vacuum fryer | Oil temp **160–175 °C**, residence time **2–4 min** | 80–85% | | **Expanded puffs (curl, ball)** | Twin-screw extruder | SME **200–350 kJ/kg**, die pressure **30–50 bar** | 78–88% | | **Baked biscuits** | Tunnel oven | Zone 3 temp **200–230 °C**, band speed | 82–90% | | **Namkeen (Indian savouries)** | Ribbon mixer + batch fryer | Oil temp **170–185 °C** | 70–80% | | **Popcorn** | Popping kettle/continuous popper | Kernel moisture **13–14%** | 75–85% | | **Coated nuts** | Tumble drum coater | Drum speed **12 RPM**, spray rate | 85–92% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** For high-quality fried snacks, the **oil-quality** is as much a "process parameter" as temperature. Polar compound content must remain **< 24%** (FSSAI limit) — this requires **continuous oil filtration + scheduled oil turnover every 6–8 hours of frying**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Typical Extruded Snack Production Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material Silos
Maize Grits / Rice Flour] --> B[Pre-conditioner
Moisture 14-18%] B --> C[Feeder
Volumetric / Gravimetric] C --> D[Extruder
Single or Twin Screw] D --> E[Die & Cutter Assembly
Rotating knives] E --> F[Drying Tunnel
Multi-pass conveyor] F --> G[Flavor Seasoning Drum
Hexagonal tumbler] G --> H[Cooling Conveyor] H --> I[Metal Detector & Check Weigher] I --> J[Packaging Machine
VFFS / Pouch] J --> K[Cartoning & Coding] K --> L[Finished Goods Warehouse] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading this diagram:** Each block represents a *Critical Control Point (CCP)* under HACCP. The metal detector + check weigher (Block I) is the **final CCP** before packaging — a non-negotiable food-safety checkpoint per FSSAI Schedule 4. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Continuous Fryer Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FB[Continuous Frying System] A1[Feed Conveyor
Infeed belt] --> A2[Pre-heat Submerged Belt
Oil 165 C] A2 --> A3[Main Frying Zone
Oil 175 C] A3 --> A4[Post-fry Drain Section] A4 --> A5[Vacuum De-oiling Hood
Steam stripping] A5 --> A6[Take-away Conveyor] end subgraph OS[Oil Circulation Subsystem] B1[Oil Reservoir Tank] --> B2[Pump] B2 --> B3[Heat Exchanger
Indirect gas/steam] B3 --> B4[Filter
Strain + carbon polish] B4 --> A3 A4 -.returns oil.-> B1 end subgraph VC[Vapour & Stack Management] C1[Stack Hood] --> C2[ESP / Cyclone Separator] C2 --> C3[Plume Suppression] end A3 -.emits vapour.-> C1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Functional Insight:** The **Oil Circulation Subsystem** and the **Vapour Management** are *auxiliary* but absolutely critical — without them the main frying line cannot operate. This is why preventive maintenance budgets allocate **15–20% of cost to auxiliaries**, not the fryer body. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Equipment Care (Maintenance + Sanitation) Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TPM[TPM Framework] M1[Preventive Maintenance
Scheduled - daily/weekly] M2[Predictive Maintenance
Vibration + thermal scan] M3[Corrective Maintenance
Breakdown repair] M4[Autonomous Maintenance
Operator-level cleaning] end subgraph SAN[Sanitation CIP] S1[Pre-rinse
Ambient water] S2[Caustic Wash
2% NaOH 70 C] S3[Rinse] S4[Acid Wash
1% HNO3 60 C] S5[Final Rinse
Potable water] S6[Sanitization
200 ppm chlorine] end M4 --> SAN SAN --> Production TPM --> Production ``` --- ## 4.4 Material-of-Construction Selection Matrix | Equipment Part | Preferred Material | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Fryer tank** | **SS 304** | Corrosion resistance to hot oil + chloride from food salts | | **Extruder barrel** | **Hardened tool steel / nitrided SS** | Wear resistance at 250 °C + 50 bar | | **Dough contact rollers** | **SS 316** (electropolished) | Hygiene + acid/salt resistance | | **Seasoning drum interior** | **SS 304**, mirror-polished Ra ≤ 0.8 µm | Prevents flavor carry-over & microbial harborage | | **Seals & gaskets** | **Food-grade silicone / EPDM** | Temperature + oil resistance, FDA compliant | | **Cutting blades** | **Hardened SS / tungsten carbide coating** | Edge retention under abrasive grits | > [!WARNING] > **Never use mild steel, brass, or copper** for any product-contact surface. They corrode, contaminate the product with metallic off-flavors, and fail FSSAI/Codex hygiene standards. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). State the world-class benchmark value. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is a comprehensive manufacturing KPI that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, expressed as the **multiplicative product of three independent factors**: $$ \text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality} $$ - **Availability** captures downtime losses (equipment failure, changeovers). - **Performance** captures speed losses (running below ideal cycle time). - **Quality** captures rejection losses (defective products). **World-class benchmark:** **OEE ≥ 85%** is universally accepted as the world-class benchmark in the food processing industry, including snack food manufacturing. *(Examiner's note: A bare definition without the three-component breakdown will lose 1 mark.)* --- ### Question 2. List any three food-grade materials of construction used for snack processing equipment and state one reason for each selection. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Stainless Steel 304 (SS 304):** Used for fryer tanks and seasoning drums because of its excellent corrosion resistance to hot edible oils and food-grade salts. *(1 mark)* 2. **Stainless Steel 316 (SS 316):** Used for product-contact rollers and dough-handling surfaces because of its superior resistance to chloride attack and acidic flavor systems. *(1 mark)* 3. **Food-grade silicone rubber:** Used for seals and gaskets because it withstands high temperatures (up to 230 °C) and remains compliant with FDA and FSSAI food-contact regulations. *(1 mark)* *(Alternative correct material: Nitrided tool steel for extruder barrels — high wear resistance under abrasive grits.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a **single-screw extruder** for the manufacture of expanded snack foods. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **CIP (Clean-In-Place) procedure** for a continuous snack fryer. List the chemicals, temperatures, and microbiological validation criteria. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a): Single-Screw Extruder **1. Introduction (1 mark):** A single-screw extruder is a continuous thermo-mechanical cooker that converts conditioned flour-based raw materials into expanded snack products via simultaneous *shear cooking, pressure build-up, and flash-off expansion* at the die. **2. Block Diagram / Functional Zones (3 marks):** A single-screw extruder consists of four sequential functional zones within a barrel: - **Feed Zone:** Coarse-pitched deep flights; transports and pre-heats material from the hopper. Approximately **30–40% of barrel length.** - **Transition (Compression) Zone:** Shallow flight depth (high compression ratio, typically **3:1 to 5:1**); compacts material and begins shear heating. - **Metering (Cooking) Zone:** Shallow uniform flights; generates mechanical energy dissipation — produces ~**80% of total SME**. - **Die Zone:** Final restriction plate with shaped orifices; product exits at high pressure. **3. Working Principle (2 marks):** Material entering at **~14–18% moisture** is sheared, compressed, and heated to **150–200 °C** under pressures of **30–80 bar**. On exiting through the die, the sudden pressure drop causes **superheated moisture to flash instantaneously**, expanding the molten starch matrix into a porous structure — the *puffing* phenomenon. **4. Key Process Variables (1 mark):** *Barrel temperature profile, screw speed (200–400 RPM), feed rate, moisture content, and die geometry.* --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b): CIP Procedure for a Continuous Fryer **1. What is CIP? (1 mark):** CIP is an automated, closed-loop cleaning system that circulates cleaning solutions through the equipment without dismantling — it is mandatory for snack fryers due to polymerized oil and Maillard deposits. **2. CIP Sequence (3 marks):** | Step | Solution | Concentration | Temperature | Duration | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1. Pre-rinse | Potable water | — | 40–50 °C | 10 min | | 2. Caustic wash | Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) | **1.5–3%** | **70–80 °C** | 30 min | | 3. Intermediate rinse | Potable water | — | Ambient | 10 min | | 4. Acid wash | Nitric acid (HNO₃) | **0.5–1.5%** | **60–70 °C** | 20 min | | 5. Final rinse | Potable water | — | Ambient | 10 min | | 6. Sanitization | Sodium hypochlorite | **100–200 ppm** | Ambient | 15 min | **3. Microbiological Validation (2 marks):** - **Total Plate Count (TPC):** ≤ **10 CFU/cm²** on product-contact surface (swab test). - **Coliform:** Absent in 1 mL rinse water. - **Pathogens:** *Salmonella* and *Listeria* absent in 25 g sample. - **ATP swab test:** ≤ **100 RLU** (relative light units) for verification. **4. Frequency and Documentation (1 mark):** CIP is performed **at the end of every production shift**; records maintained per FSSAI Schedule 4 audit requirements. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** Describe the **equipment care and maintenance regime** for a snack food processing plant. Differentiate between *preventive* and *predictive* maintenance with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A twin-screw extruder runs at a screw speed of **300 rad/s**, with a net torque of **120 N·m**, processing **0.8 kg/s** of conditioned corn grits. Calculate the **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** and comment on the product type expected. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a): Equipment Care Regime **1. Definition of Equipment Care (1 mark):** Equipment care is an integrated framework of *Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)* and *food-safety sanitation* designed to maximize uptime, ensure hygiene, and extend service life of snack processing machinery. **2. Four Pillars of Maintenance (4 marks):** | Type | Definition | Example in Snack Plant | |---|---|---| | **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | Scheduled routine servicing based on time or cycles, *regardless* of equipment condition. | Daily lubrication of extruder gearbox bearings; weekly belt tension check on fryer conveyor. | | **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** | Condition-based servicing using sensor data, performed *only when a deviation* signals impending failure. | Vibration analysis on fryer motor; thermography of oven heating coils; oil-quality sensors trigger filter change. | | **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | Repair performed *after* a failure has occurred (run-to-failure approach). | Replacing a snapped seasoning drum drive chain. | | **Autonomous Maintenance (AM)** | Routine cleaning, inspection, and minor servicing done by the *equipment operator* under TPM. | Operator wiping down the fryer exterior and checking oil level at shift start. | **3. Key Differences — Preventive vs Predictive (2 marks):** - **Preventive** is *calendar-based* and *avoidable-cost driven*; it sometimes replaces parts still in good condition (over-maintenance). - **Predictive** is *sensor-driven* and *condition-based*; parts are replaced exactly when needed, reducing inventory and downtime. **4. Key KPI (1 mark):** World-class plants maintain **MTBF > 1000 hours** and **MTTR < 30 minutes**. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(b): SME Numerical **Given:** - Screw speed $N = 300$ rad/s - Net torque $\tau = 120$ N·m - Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 0.8$ kg/s **Step 1: Recall the SME formula.** $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot N}{\dot{m}} $$ **Step 2: Substitute the values.** $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{120 \times 300}{0.8} $$ **Step 3: Numerator computation.** $$ 120 \times 300 = 36{,}000 $$ **Step 4: Divide by mass flow rate.** $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{36{,}000}{0.8} = 45{,}000 \text{ J/kg} $$ **Step 5: Convert to kJ/kg.** $$ \text{SME} = 45 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Comment on Product Type (2 marks):** A SME of **45 kJ/kg** is in the *low* range. This is characteristic of a **dense, less-expanded product** such as a **third-generation snack pellet** (a dense, glassy intermediate that requires secondary expansion by frying or hot-air puffing at a later stage). For direct-expanded "second-generation" puffed snacks, SME in the range **150–400 kJ/kg** is required — so this machine would need to either *reduce throughput to ~0.1 kg/s* or *increase torque and speed* to reach direct-expansion conditions. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Machinery & Equipment Topic:** > > 1. **Writing OEE in parts and not multiplying** — students often describe the three components but forget to write the formula $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$. *You lose 1 mark immediately.* > 2. **Confusing MTBF and MTTR** — these are frequently interchanged in answers. MTBF is *between failures* (reliability); MTTR is *to repair* (maintainability). > 3. **SME unit confusion** — many students write SME in J/s instead of J/kg, or forget to convert to **kJ/kg**, which is the industry-conventional unit. > 4. **CIP step omission** — listing only 3 of the 6 CIP steps costs a full 2 marks. *Always remember: Pre-rinse → Caustic → Rinse → Acid → Final Rinse → Sanitize.* > 5. **Writing "stainless steel" without specifying grade** — SS 304 vs SS 316 vs SS 316L are not interchangeable; examiners expect **grade-level precision**. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q** is the single most-tested KPI in equipment topics. World-class benchmark: **≥ 85%**. - ✅ **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** for direct-expanded snacks: **150–400 kJ/kg**; for dense pellets: **30–80 kJ/kg**. - ✅ **CIP for fryers** uses a 6-step sequence; the **caustic wash at 70–80 °C with 2% NaOH** is the most critical. - ✅ **Material of construction** for food contact: **SS 304** (general), **SS 316** (chloride/salt exposure), **silicone** (seals). - ✅ **Frying oil temperature:** **160–190 °C** for snacks; **FSSAI polar compound limit: 24%**. - ✅ **Maintenance types:** Preventive (calendar) vs Predictive (sensor-based) — both required; **MTBF > 1000 h** is world-class. - ✅ **Microbiological validation post-CIP:** TPC ≤ **10 CFU/cm²**; *Salmonella* absent in **25 g**. - ✅ **Fryer design:** Three zones — pre-heat, main frying, de-oiling. Use of vacuum de-oiling reduces oil content from **~35% → <25%**. - ✅ **Twin-screw vs Single-screw:** Twin-screw offers better mixing and cooking control but higher capital cost (~1.8–2.5×). - ✅ **Safety standards:** All rotating parts must have **mechanical interlocks**; all electrical panels must be **IP 55 or higher** due to wash-down environments. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Revision Strategy:** For the 14-mark question, always draw the **block diagram first** (3 marks guaranteed), then label the *principle* (2 marks), then enumerate *process variables* (2 marks). The diagram earns marks even if the written portion is incomplete.

topics.title: Pulse Based Snacks - Technology and Processing Formulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pulse Based Snacks - Technology and Processing Formulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pulse-Based Snacks** are ready-to-eat (RTE) or ready-to-cook (RTC) convenience food products manufactured primarily from the dried edible seeds of leguminous plants belonging to the family **Fabaceae (Leguminosae)**. These include whole pulses (chickpea, green gram, black gram, pigeon pea, lentil), split pulses (dals), pulse flours (besan, soy flour, moong flour), and pulse proteins (isolates and concentrates). Pulse-based snacks combine the **high protein content (20–30 %)**, dietary fibre, and micronutrient density of legumes with the **sensory appeal, shelf-stability, and convenience** characteristics demanded by modern snack consumers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *Pulse-based snacks* encompass traditional Indian fried/roasted snacks (sev, bhujia, papad, namakpara, murukku, pakora) as well as modern extruded and baked snacks where pulse flour or pulse protein replaces or complements cereal flour.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of pulses as **"nature's protein capsules"** — tiny seeds that store nitrogen-rich proteins, complex carbohydrates, and minerals inside a tough outer coat (husk). When you make a snack from pulses, you are essentially **unlocking a nutrient-dense package** and reshaping it into something crunchy, flavorful, and shelf-stable.

Imagine three approaches to this unlocking:

1. **The "Whole Grain" approach** — Like roasting whole moong or chana, where the seed is cooked in its near-natural form. Minimal processing, maximum fibre.
2. **The "Flour Transformation" approach** — Like grinding chana dal into besan and then pressing it through a *sev-maker* (jhara) into hot oil. The seed is crushed into powder, then re-engineered into noodle-like strands.
3. **The "High-Tech" approach** — Like extruding soy flour through a twin-screw extruder at **140–180 °C** and high pressure, where the proteins denature and starch gelatinize instantly, puffing into a porous crunchy matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Pulses Used in Snack Industry (Recall for Exams):**
> - **Bengal gram (Chana / Cicer arietinum)** → besan, chana dal snacks
> - **Black gram (Urad / Vigna mungo)** → papad, vada, murukku
> - **Green gram (Moong / Vigna radiata)** → moong dal snacks, namakpara
> - **Pigeon pea (Arhar / Cajanus cajan)** → toor dal snacks
> - **Lentil (Masoor / Lens culinaris)** → lentil chips, extruded snacks
> - **Soybean (Glycine max)** → soy chunks, soy fortified snacks

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Recall Anchors)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Protein content (raw pulse) | **20–30 %** |
| Carbohydrate content | **50–65 %** |
| Dietary fibre | **10–25 %** |
| Moisture (dried pulse) | **< 12 %** |
| Moisture (finished snack) | **3–6 %** |
| Water activity ($a_w$) of finished snack | **< 0.6** |
| Frying temperature | **160–190 °C** |
| Extrusion barrel temperature | **120–180 °C** |
| Screw speed (extrusion) | **200–400 rpm** |

> [!NOTE]
> Pulse proteins are classified as **globulins (salt-soluble)** and **albumins (water-soluble)** — legumin (11S) and vicilin (7S) being the dominant fractions. This biochemical fact governs the *functional behaviour* of pulse flour during frying, baking, and extrusion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Pulses? The Nutritional & Functional Logic

Pulses are nutritionally superior to cereals in three measurable dimensions:

1. **Higher protein density with balanced amino acid profile** — pulses complement cereals by supplying **lysine** (limiting in cereals), while cereals supply **methionine** (limiting in pulses). This is the foundation of the **"Protein Complementarity"** principle used in snack formulation.
2. **Low glycemic index** — pulse starch has a higher proportion of **amylose (30–40 %)** and resistant starch, leading to slower glucose release.
3. **Functional properties** — pulse proteins exhibit *foaming, emulsification, gelation, and water-holding capacity* essential for snack texturization.

## 2.2 The Six Pillars of Pulse Snack Processing

### Pillar 1: Pretreatment — Cleaning, Soaking, Dehulling
- **Cleaning**: Removal of stones, chaff, foreign seeds using *gravity separators* and *air classifiers*.
- **Soaking**: Hydration of seeds (typically **8–12 h**, 25–30 °C) to facilitate dehulling and reduce anti-nutritional factors (phytic acid, tannins, oligosaccharides).
- **Dehulling**: Mechanical removal of seed coat using *abrasive rollers* or *emery-coated discs*. Improves digestibility, reduces fibre, and enhances snack colour/texture.

### Pillar 2: Milling — From Pulse to Flour
- **Dry milling** → produces *besan* (chickpea flour), *moong flour*, *urad flour*.
- **Wet milling** → produces batter (used for vada, pakora, dhokla).
- Particle size reduction to **< 250 µm** is critical for uniform hydration during extrusion/frying.

### Pillar 3: Mixing & Dough Formation
The formation of a viscoelastic dough is the *gateway* to all pulse snacks. The dough must have:
- Optimal **moisture content (20–35 % wet basis)** depending on process
- Proper **protein hydration** for elasticity
- Proper **starch hydration** for binding

### Pillar 4: Shaping — The "Architecture" Step
- **Extrusion** (single/twin screw) → curls, puffs, third-generation snacks
- **Sev-maker (jhara) press** → sev, bhujia, gathiya
- **Sheeting & cutting** → namakpara, mathri, ribbon pakoda
- **Hand-rolling / moulding** → papad, chakli

### Pillar 5: Cooking — Gelatinization, Dextrinization, Puffing
- **Frying** (160–190 °C): rapid moisture evaporation → porous crispy structure
- **Hot-air roasting/puffing** (200–260 °C): gun-puffing principle (sudden vapor pressure release)
- **Extrusion cooking**: simultaneous HTST (high-temperature short-time) treatment → gelatinization, protein denaturation, expansion

### Pillar 6: Post-Cooking — Seasoning, Cooling, Packaging
- Oil-coating to carry seasoning
- Tumble-coating with spice blends (masala)
- Moisture equilibration to $a_w < 0.6$
- Packaging in **metallized polyester / LDPE laminates**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Protein Complementarity** (Combined BV) | $\text{BV}_{\text{mix}} = f_A \cdot \text{BV}_A + f_B \cdot \text{BV}_B$ where $f_A + f_B = 1$ | Pulse + cereal blending |
| 2 | **Water Absorption Index (WAI)** | $\text{WAI} = \dfrac{W_{\text{gel}}}{W_{\text{dry sample}}}$ | Flour hydration capacity |
| 3 | **Water Solubility Index (WSI)** | $\text{WSI} = \dfrac{W_{\text{dissolved solids}}}{W_{\text{dry sample}} } \times 100 \ (\%)$ | Starch degradation indicator |
| 4 | **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{D_{\text{extrudate}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$ | Extrudate puffiness |
| 5 | **Bulk Density (BD)** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{\text{bulk}}} \quad (\text{g/cm}^3)$ | Crispness correlate |
| 6 | **Moisture content (wet basis)** | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100 \ (\%)$ | Standard reporting |
| 7 | **Moisture content (dry basis)** | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100 \ (\%)$ | Engineering calculations |
| 8 | **Oil absorption** | $\text{OA} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil absorbed}}}{m_{\text{dry snack}}} \times 100$ | Quality of fried snacks |
| 9 | **Fried snack oil uptake (kinetic model)** | $\Delta m_{\text{oil}} \propto \sqrt{D_s \cdot t}$ where $D_s$ = surface diffusivity | Frying time optimization |
| 10 | **$a_w$ & microbial stability** | $a_w < 0.6 \Rightarrow$ no microbial growth | Shelf-life rule |
| 11 | **Hardness (Texture Profile Analysis)** | $F_{\max} = k \cdot \rho_b^{\,n}$ (empirical) | Crispness measurement |
| 12 | **Shelf-life (Q$_{10}$ rule)** | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | Accelerated storage studies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In markdown tables, vertical bars are escaped as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table syntax integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pulse snack processing is a major economic driver in India's **₹40,000+ crore snack industry**. Companies like **Haldiram's, Bikaji, Balaji, ITC (Bingo!)** employ pulse-flour extrusion, frying, and seasoning lines running at **1–10 tonnes/hour**. Understanding *why* a besan sev expands differently from a corn-based puff is what enables a food technologist to engineer **better crispness, lower oil uptake, higher protein claim**, and **cleaner labels** (e.g., "baked not fried", "high protein", "gluten-free").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Protein Complementarity Ratio

**Problem:** A snack formulation mixes chickpea flour (BV = 62) and rice flour (BV = 54). Find the proportion of chickpea flour needed to achieve a *combined BV of 60* — the minimum considered "high quality" by FAO standards.

**Step 1 — Define the mixing fractions.**
Let $f_C$ = fraction of chickpea flour and $f_R$ = fraction of rice flour.

$$f_C + f_R = 1$$

**Step 2 — Express the weighted average of Biological Value.**

$$\text{BV}_{\text{mix}} = f_C \cdot \text{BV}_C + f_R \cdot \text{BV}_R$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values.**

$$60 = f_C \cdot 62 + (1 - f_C) \cdot 54$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
60 &= 62 f_C + 54 - 54 f_C \\
60 - 54 &= (62 - 54) f_C \\
6 &= 8 f_C \\
f_C &= \dfrac{6}{8} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the rice fraction.**

$$f_R = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$

> **Conclusion:** A blend of **75 % chickpea flour + 25 % rice flour** yields the target BV of 60. This is the "by-eye" derivation of why most Indian pulse snacks already use this 3:1 pulse-to-cereal ratio — it has emerged from centuries of empirical optimization.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Expansion Ratio of an Extruded Pulse Snack

**Problem:** A twin-screw extruder has a die diameter of $D_{\text{die}} = 4.0 \ \text{mm}$. After extrusion of a soy–rice blend at barrel temperature **160 °C**, screw speed **300 rpm**, and feed moisture **18 % wb**, the resulting extrudate cross-section diameter is $D_{\text{ext}} = 11.2 \ \text{mm}$. Determine the expansion ratio.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition.**

$$\text{ER} = \dfrac{D_{\text{ext}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\text{ER} = \dfrac{11.2}{4.0} = 2.80$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**
- ER = 1.0 → no expansion (over-cooked, dense)
- ER = 2.0–3.0 → ideal for crispy RTE puffs
- ER > 4.0 → overly expanded, fragile structure

> **Conclusion:** An ER of **2.80** places this extrudate in the *desirable range* for an RTE pulse-based puff snack.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Oil Uptake in Fried Pulse Snacks

The oil absorbed by a fried snack during cooling follows a diffusion-controlled model:

$$\Delta M_{\text{oil}}(t) = M_{\infty} \left[ 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right) \right]$$

where $\tau$ is a characteristic diffusion time constant. For an empirical linearized form (valid for short cooling times):

$$\Delta M_{\text{oil}} \approx k_{\text{oil}} \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

**Worked numerical:** For sev fried at **180 °C** and removed to drain for **t = 60 s**:
- $k_{\text{oil}} = 0.85 \ \text{g oil} \cdot \text{s}^{-1/2}$ per 100 g sev (typical)
- Therefore:

$$
\Delta M_{\text{oil}} \approx 0.85 \times \sqrt{60} = 0.85 \times 7.746 \approx 6.58 \ \text{g oil per 100 g sev}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** This is why food technologists recommend *shallow frying* with **rapid post-fry draining and forced-air cooling** — reducing effective contact time $t$ is the most direct lever to cut oil content (and calories).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Process Routes

| Process Route | Temperature (°C) | Time | Oil Uptake | Expansion | Protein Retention | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Deep-fat frying** | 160–190 | 30–90 s | High (20–35 %) | Low | 85–90 % | Low | Sev, bhujia, namakpara |
| **Hot-air roasting** | 180–220 | 5–15 min | Very low (1–3 %) | Low | 90–95 % | Medium | Puffed chana, soy nuts |
| **Gun puffing** | 250–280 (sudden) | 5–15 s | Low (2–5 %) | High (ER 3–6) | 88–92 % | High | Puffed snacks (murmura-type) |
| **Single-screw extrusion** | 130–170 | 20–40 s | Low–Medium | Medium | 85–90 % | Medium | Direct-expanded snacks |
| **Twin-screw extrusion** | 140–180 | 15–30 s | Low | High (ER 2.5–4) | 88–93 % | High | Protein-rich RTE puffs |
| **Baking** | 160–200 | 10–25 min | Very low | Low | 92–96 % | Medium | Baked mathri, crackers |

## 3.5 Derivation IV — Shelf-Life Estimation Using Q₁₀

**Problem:** A pulse-based snack stored at **30 °C** has a shelf-life of **180 days** (defined as the time for peroxide value to reach 20 meq/kg). Find the shelf-life at **40 °C** if $Q_{10} = 2.0$ for lipid oxidation in this product.

**Step 1 — Recall the Q₁₀ relationship.**

$$\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = Q_{10}^{\,(T_2 - T_1)/10}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\dfrac{k_{40}}{k_{30}} = 2.0^{(40 - 30)/10} = 2.0^{1} = 2.0$$

**Step 3 — Since shelf-life is inversely proportional to rate constant:**

$$\text{SL}_{40} = \dfrac{\text{SL}_{30}}{2.0} = \dfrac{180}{2.0} = 90 \ \text{days}$$

> **Conclusion:** A 10 °C rise in storage temperature halves the shelf-life. This is the empirical basis for the industry-wide recommendation to store pulse-based fried snacks **below 25 °C** for maximum shelf stability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Pulse Snack Process Flow — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire block-level functional architecture of pulse snack manufacturing, from raw pulse to finished packaged product.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Pulse Intake] --> B[Pre-Cleaning: Destoner, Air Classifier]
    B --> C[Soaking Tank: 8-12 h at 25-30 C]
    C --> D[Dehulling: Abrasive Roller]
    D --> E[Drying: 12-14 percent Moisture]
    E --> F{Milling Route}
    F --> F1[Dry Milling to Besan / Pulse Flour]
    F --> F2[Wet Milling to Batter]
    F1 --> G[Formulation & Dough Mixing]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H{Shaping Route}
    H --> H1[Extruder: 140-180 C]
    H --> H2[Sev Maker / Sheeter]
    H --> H3[Moulding: Papad Chakli]
    H1 --> I1[Extrusion Cooking]
    H2 --> I2[Deep Fat Frying: 160-190 C]
    H3 --> I3[Sun Drying / Baking]
    I1 --> J[Seasoning Tumbler]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Cooling: a w less than 0.6]
    K --> L[Metal Detector and Sieving]
    L --> M[Packaging: Metallized Laminate]
    M --> N[Finished Pulse Snack]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram represents the **functional flow**, not a physical drawing. Each block is a unit operation; each decision diamond (e.g., *Milling Route*, *Shaping Route*) represents a process-choice fork.

## 4.2 Twin-Screw Extruder — Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Feed Hopper] --> R2[Feed Screw: 18 percent MC wb]
    R2 --> R3[Mixing Zone: 80-100 C]
    R3 --> R4[Cooking Zone: 140-160 C]
    R4 --> R5[Kneading Block: High Shear]
    R5 --> R6[Cooking Zone 2: 160-180 C]
    R6 --> R7[Die: 3-5 mm diameter]
    R7 --> R8[Sudden Pressure Drop]
    R8 --> R9[Expanded Extrudate: ER 2.5-4]
    R9 --> R10[Cutting Blade]
    R10 --> R11[Conveyor to Seasoner]
```

## 4.3 Pulse Snack Formulation Triangle — Ingredient Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pulse Flour: 60-80 percent] --> MIX[Snack Dough Matrix]
    P2[Cereal Flour: 10-30 percent] --> MIX
    P3[Spices Seasoning: 2-8 percent] --> MIX
    P4[Salt: 1-2 percent] --> MIX
    P5[Oil Shortening: 2-10 percent] --> MIX
    P6[Water: 15-30 percent wb] --> MIX
    MIX --> PROD{Pulse Snack Type}
    PROD --> Q1[Bhujia Sev]
    PROD --> Q2[Papad]
    PROD --> Q3[Extruded Puff]
    PROD --> Q4[Namakpara]
```

## 4.4 Quality Control Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QC[Finished Snack] --> T1[Moisture Test: less than 6 percent]
    QC --> T2[Water Activity: less than 0.6]
    QC --> T3[Peroxide Value: less than 10 meq per kg]
    QC --> T4[Texture: Hardness and Crispness]
    QC --> T5[Color: L a b Values]
    QC --> T6[Microbial: TPC less than 10^4 cfu per g]
    T1 --> DEC{Acceptance Decision}
    T2 --> DEC
    T3 --> DEC
    T4 --> DEC
    T5 --> DEC
    T6 --> DEC
    DEC --> PASS[Release for Packaging]
    DEC --> REJ[Reject: Reprocess or Dispose]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define pulse-based snacks and list any four commonly used pulses in snack formulation. Mention their protein range.

> **Model Answer:**
> *Pulse-based snacks* are ready-to-eat or ready-to-cook convenience food products manufactured primarily from dried seeds of leguminous crops (Family: Fabaceae). They provide high-quality protein, dietary fibre, and micronutrients in a shelf-stable, sensorially appealing form.
>
> Four commonly used pulses:
> 1. **Bengal gram (Chana)** — Protein ~20–22 %
> 2. **Black gram (Urad dal)** — Protein ~24–26 %
> 3. **Green gram (Moong)** — Protein ~24 %
> 4. **Soybean** — Protein ~36–40 %
>
> *Reference range: 20–30 % protein for most pulses, except soy which can reach 40 %.*
> **[3 Marks]**

### Q2. What is protein complementarity? Give one example relevant to pulse snack formulation.

> **Model Answer:**
> **Protein complementarity** is the principle by which two or more protein sources with different limiting amino acids are combined to yield a combined protein quality (BV/PER) superior to either source alone.
>
> **Example:** Chickpea (limiting in methionine) + Rice (limiting in lysine) → The blend supplies both amino acids in adequate proportions, raising the combined Biological Value above that of either flour alone.
> **[3 Marks]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe in detail the **unit operations involved in the processing of pulse-based snacks**, with emphasis on soaking, dehulling, milling, and extrusion cooking. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **principle of twin-screw extrusion** for pulse-based RTE snacks. Discuss the role of barrel temperature, screw speed, feed moisture, and die geometry on expansion ratio and crispness. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

The processing chain for pulse-based snacks comprises six sequential unit operations:

**1. Cleaning and Grading**
Removal of foreign matter (stones, chaff, weed seeds, dust) by *air-screen cleaners*, *gravity separators*, and *magnetic separators*. Grading by size ensures uniform subsequent processing.

**2. Soaking**
Whole pulses are hydrated in water at **25–30 °C for 8–12 h** (sometimes up to 18 h for hard-seeded varieties). Soaking achieves:
- Moisture uptake to **50–60 % wb**
- Reduction of anti-nutritional factors (phytic acid, tannins, oligosaccharides like raffinose, stachyose)
- Easier dehulling through seed-coat loosening

**3. Dehulling**
Mechanical removal of seed coat using **abrasive-type dehullers** (emery-coated discs or rollers). For dals (split pulses), an additional **splitting operation** produces *cotyledons*. Dehulling improves:
- Digestibility (removes insoluble fibre)
- Cooking quality
- Snack colour and texture

**4. Milling**
Pulses are converted to flour using:
- **Dry milling** (roller mill, hammer mill, pin mill) → *besan* (chickpea flour), particle size < 250 µm
- **Wet milling** (stone grinder or burr mill with water) → smooth batter for vada, dhokla
- Quality is monitored by **WAI** and **WSI** as functional indicators.

**5. Mixing / Dough Formation**
Pulse flour is mixed with water (**15–30 % wb**), salt, spices, and sometimes cereal flour. The dough must develop viscoelasticity via **protein hydration and starch swelling**.

**6. Shaping and Cooking**
Three principal routes:
- **Frying** (sev, bhujia, namakpara) — 160–190 °C oil
- **Extrusion cooking** (RTE puffs) — 140–180 °C barrel
- **Hot-air roasting / gun puffing** — 200–280 °C

**7. Post-Cooking Operations**
Seasoning coating, cooling to $a_w < 0.6$, metal detection, and packaging in moisture- and oxygen-barrier laminates.

*[Mark Allocation: Cleaning 1, Soaking 1, Dehulling 1, Milling 1, Mixing 1, Cooking 2, Post-cooking 1 = 7 marks total]*

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Principle of Twin-Screw Extrusion:**
Twin-screw extrusion is a **HTST (High-Temperature Short-Time)** continuous cooking process in which a moistened, formulated feed is conveyed, compressed, sheared, and heated by *two intermeshing screws* rotating inside a *barrel*. The material experiences temperatures of **120–180 °C** for **15–30 seconds** under high pressure, then is forced through a *die* where sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation of superheated steam**, leading to puffing and expansion.

**Key Process Variables and Their Effects:**

| Variable | Range | Effect on Expansion Ratio (ER) | Effect on Crispness / Texture |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Barrel Temperature** | 120–180 °C | Higher T → more starch gelatinization → higher ER up to optimum | Over-temperature causes collapse |
| **Screw Speed** | 200–400 rpm | Higher speed → higher SME → higher ER | Excess speed → mechanical degradation |
| **Feed Moisture** | 14–22 % wb | Lower moisture → higher ER | Higher moisture → denser, chewy texture |
| **Die Diameter** | 2–6 mm | Smaller die → higher pressure drop → higher ER | Affects crispness bite |
| **Die L/D Ratio** | 2:1 to 4:1 | Higher L/D → more shear → higher ER | Influences cell structure |

**Mechanism of Expansion:**
Inside the barrel, the dough becomes a *plasticized melt* of denatured proteins and gelatinized starch. At the die exit, superheated water flashes into steam, leaving a porous, aerated structure that solidifies as it cools. The **Expansion Ratio (ER)** is defined as:

$$\text{ER} = \dfrac{D_{\text{ext}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$$

**Role of Pulse Protein:**
Pulse proteins (legumin and vicilin) undergo **denaturation and cross-linking** during extrusion, providing a structural network that traps air cells. This is why high-protein formulations (soy-based) can produce **crispy, well-expanded extrudates** with low bulk density.

**Typical Values for Pulse Extrudates:**
- Bulk density: **100–300 kg/m³**
- Expansion ratio: **2.5–4.0**
- WAI: **4–7 g gel/g dry**
- WSI: **20–35 %**

*[Mark Allocation: Principle 2, Variable effects 3, Mechanism 1, Typical values 1 = 7 marks total]*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **traditional pulse-based snacks of India** — sev, bhujia, papad, namakpara, and murukku — with respect to formulation, process parameters, and quality attributes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **functional role of pulse proteins** in snack texture. How does protein denaturation and starch gelatinization during frying or extrusion govern the final product's crispness, oil uptake, and shelf-life? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**1. Sev**
- *Formulation:* Besan (60–80 %), cereal flour (10–25 %), spices, salt, oil, water.
- *Process:* Dough pressed through a *sev-maker (jhara)* with perforated disc into hot oil at **170–185 °C** for 20–40 s.
- *Quality attributes:* Uniform strand thickness (1.5–2 mm diameter), golden yellow colour, crispness (low bulk density), moisture < 4 %.

**2. Bhujia**
- *Formulation:* Besan + moth bean / urad dal flour + spice blend (clove, cinnamon, cardamom), salt.
- *Process:* Similar to sev but with finer die and longer strands; fried at **165–180 °C**.
- *Quality attributes:* Hard, brittle texture; deep spice penetration; long shelf-life (4–6 months).

**3. Papad**
- *Formulation:* Urad dal flour (primary), salt, soda bicarb, optional spices; lentil + black gram blends are common.
- *Process:* Dough sheeted, sun-dried (moisture < 12 %), then roasted/fried/microwaved by consumer at point-of-use.
- *Quality attributes:* Uniform thickness (1–2 mm), puffability upon roasting, characteristic speckled appearance.

**4. Namakpara (Salty Sticks)**
- *Formulation:* Whole wheat + besan + salt + ghee/oil; sometimes moong flour inclusion.
- *Process:* Sheeted (2–3 mm), cut into strips, deep-fried at **170–180 °C** for 30–60 s.
- *Quality attributes:* Crisp, salty, golden brown; moisture < 3 %; $a_w < 0.55$.

**5. Murukku (Chakli)**
- *Formulation:* Rice flour + urad dal flour (3:1 typical), sesame, cumin, salt.
- *Process:* Dough extruded through a *chakli mould* (star die) into hot oil.
- *Quality attributes:* Spiral/twisted shape, crisp bite, characteristic roasted aroma.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Snack | Main Pulse | Cooking | Distinctive Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sev | Besan | Fry | Thin strands |
| Bhujia | Besan + moth/urad | Fry | Spicy, long strands |
| Papad | Urad flour | Sun-dry + roast | Puffable sheet |
| Namakpara | Besan (blended) | Fry | Salty sticks |
| Murukku | Urad + rice | Fry | Spiral extruded |

*[Mark Allocation: 5 snacks × ~1.4 marks each = 7 marks]*

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Functional Role of Pulse Proteins in Snack Texture:**

Pulse proteins — predominantly **legumin (11S)** and **vicilin (7S)** globulins — play four critical roles:

**(i) Protein Denaturation and Gelation:**
During frying (> 100 °C) or extrusion (> 120 °C), the native globular proteins **unfold** (denature), exposing hydrophobic and sulfhydryl groups. These groups form **disulfide bonds (S–S)** and **hydrophobic interactions**, creating a continuous protein matrix that traps air cells, stabilizes the porous snack structure, and provides mechanical strength (crispness).

**(ii) Starch–Protein Interaction:**
Simultaneously, pulse starch undergoes **gelatinization** (loss of crystalline order, swelling of granules, amylose leaching). The gelatinized starch fills the protein matrix, producing a **composite solid foam**. The ratio of protein to gelatinized starch controls *crispness-to-hardness* balance.

**(iii) Crispness and Oil Uptake:**
A well-set protein network produces a **rigid, porous matrix**. Crispness is empirically correlated with bulk density:

$$F_{\max} \propto \rho_b^{\,n}, \quad n \approx 1.5{-}2$$

A denser structure (higher $\rho_b$) → harder, less crisp. Lower $\rho_b$ → crispier but more fragile.

**Oil uptake** is governed by **capillary suction into pores** during cooling. Lower $\rho_b$ can paradoxically increase oil uptake if pore size exceeds the critical radius. Hence, optimal pulse snacks balance **expansion (high ER)** with **fine cell structure (small pores)**.

**(iv) Shelf-Life Implications:**
Protein–lipid interactions can promote oxidation. Pulse proteins contain **polyunsaturated fatty acids** (in residual oil) susceptible to oxidative rancidity. Proper **moisture control ($a_w < 0.6$)** and **oxygen-barrier packaging** are essential. The **Q₁₀ rule** applies: storage at **25 °C** extends shelf-life dramatically compared to 35 °C.

**Cumulative Quality Equation (Engineering Synthesis):**

$$\text{Quality} = f(\text{ER}, \rho_b, M, a_w, \text{PV})$$

where each parameter must lie within its acceptance window.

*[Mark Allocation: Protein function 2, Denaturation 2, Crispness/oil 2, Shelf-life 1 = 7 marks total]*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** *expansion ratio* (geometric) with *bulk density* (mass/volume); they are inversely correlated but **NOT the same parameter**.
> - When defining pulses, **always mention "Family Fabaceae / Leguminosae"** for full marks.
> - In extrusion questions, **mention HTST principle** and **flash evaporation at die** to score the process-clarity marks.
> - For fried snacks, students often forget to **state oil temperature (160–190 °C)** and **frying time** — both are mandatory.
> - In shelf-life derivations, **state the Q₁₀ assumption explicitly** before substituting values.
> - **Do not write "soy is a pulse"** in absolute terms — soybean is a *oilseed legume*; refer to it as a *pulse-like legume* or *grain legume*.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Pulses = Family Fabaceae**, dried seeds, **20–30 % protein** (soy up to 40 %).
- **Protein complementarity**: pulse + cereal gives BV higher than either alone (lysine ↔ methionine).
- **Six process pillars**: cleaning → soaking → dehulling → milling → shaping → cooking → post-cook (packaging).
- **Soaking**: 8–12 h at 25–30 °C; reduces anti-nutritional factors (phytic acid, oligosaccharides).
- **Dehulling** improves digestibility and snack colour; milling produces besan (< 250 µm).
- **Twin-screw extrusion** operates at 120–180 °C, 200–400 rpm, 14–22 % feed moisture; HTST principle; flash evaporation at die.
- **Expansion Ratio (ER) = $D_{\text{ext}} / D_{\text{die}}$**; desirable range for pulse puffs is **2.5–4.0**.
- **WAI** and **WSI** are critical functional indices for pulse flour; WAI ↑ with starch damage.
- **Frying**: 160–190 °C oil; oil uptake ∝ √t (diffusion-controlled).
- **Papad, sev, bhujia, namakpara, murukku** are the five *must-know* traditional Indian pulse snacks.
- **Papad uses urad dal flour**; **sev/bhujia use besan**; **murukku uses urad + rice (3:1)**.
- **Finished snack moisture < 6 %**, **$a_w < 0.6$**, **PV < 10 meq/kg oil** for adequate shelf stability.
- **Q₁₀ rule**: a 10 °C rise halves shelf-life; store below 25 °C.
- **Pulse proteins** (legumin 11S, vicilin 7S) denature during frying/extrusion → form structural matrix → govern crispness, oil uptake, and texture.
- **Gluten-free advantage** of pulse flours is a major modern marketing claim (e.g., besan-based, moong-based extruded snacks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instant Food Pre-Mixes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instant Food Pre-Mixes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Instant Food Pre-Mixes** are scientifically formulated, dry powdered or granular food products in which all the required ingredients (in proper proportions) are pre-blended, pre-processed, and packed in a convenient form so that the consumer can prepare the finished food item at home or in a food-service establishment merely by the addition of water (or milk) and minimal cooking or processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> Instant food pre-mixes are dehydrated, ready-to-cook formulations based on convenience and shelf-stability. The product is reconstituted by the end-user through the addition of a liquid (water/milk) followed by controlled heating, mixing, fermentation (optional), or resting — yielding the traditional food in a fraction of the conventional preparation time.

The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) classifies such products under **FSSAI's "Food Product Standards and Food Additives"** regulations, recognizing pre-mixes as **Ready-to-Cook (RTC)** convenience foods.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Think of an instant food pre-mix like a **"Lego Kit of Cooking"**:

> 🧩 Imagine you want to build a Lego house. Instead of buying 5,000 individual bricks, sorting them, and assembling them yourself, you buy a **pre-sorted box** containing *exactly the right bricks in the right proportions*, with an instruction manual. You just snap them together.

That's exactly what an instant pre-mix is to traditional cooking:

| Traditional Cooking | Instant Food Pre-Mix |
|---------------------|----------------------|
| Buy rice, urad dal, fenugreek, salt separately | Buy a single packet of **Idli Mix** |
| Soak 4–6 hours, grind, ferment 8–12 hours | Just add water → steam for 10 min |
| **Total time: ~24 hours** | **Total time: ~15 minutes** |
| Multiple utensils, expertise required | One bowl, one spoon, one steamer |

The *value addition* has already been done by the **food technologist** (soaking, grinding, drying, blending, ingredient balancing). The consumer only performs the **final reconstitution step**.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Quantitative Benchmarks

The following industry-standard benchmarks are critical for KTU exam answers:

- **Moisture Content**: typically **≤ 6–10 % (w/w)** — critical for shelf-life (low water activity)
- **Water Activity (aₐ)**: typically **≤ 0.6** — inhibits microbial growth
- **Particle Size**: usually **60–100 mesh (BSS)** for most cereal-legume pre-mixes
- **Reconstitution Time**: typically **2–15 minutes** depending on product
- **Bulk Density**: **0.4–0.8 g/cm³** for most flour-based pre-mixes
- **Shelf Life**: **6–18 months** in moisture-proof packaging

> [!NOTE]
> **Why low moisture?**
> Microbial activity (bacteria, yeast, mould) requires water in the **free** state. By reducing moisture to **<10%**, food technologists push the product into a state where bacteria simply cannot multiply — a principle known as **hurdle technology** (Leistner, 2000).

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Pre-Mixes

Based on the food category and processing requirement, KTU recognizes the following major families:

1. **Batters & Dosa/Idli-Type Pre-Mixes** (fermented cereal-legume foods)
2. **Beverage Mixes** (tea, coffee, soup, health drink mixes)
3. **Dessert Mixes** (kheer, halwa, gulab jamun, custard)
4. **Bakery Pre-Mixes** (cake, pancake, muffin, cookie)
5. **Upma/Pongal/Khichdi Pre-Mixes** (single-pot meal pre-mixes)
6. **Sauce/Gravy Pre-Mixes** (instant curry bases, rasam, sambar)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Manufacturing Flow (Why & How)

The complete industrial process for instant food pre-mix manufacturing follows a deliberate sequence of **value-additive unit operations**:

### Step 1 — Raw Material Procurement & Cleaning
- **Why?** Cereals (rice, wheat, ragi) and pulses (urad dal, moong dal) are purchased in bulk. They may carry stones, dust, husks, and foreign matter.
- **How?** Pass through **destoner, magnetic separator, air classifier, and de-stoner cum aspirator**.

### Step 2 — Pre-Conditioning (Soaking / Roasting / Parching)
- **Why?** Soaking softens the grain for grinding; roasting/parching develops flavour, reduces anti-nutrients (phytates), and decreases microbial load.
- **How?** Controlled soaking at **30–60 °C for 2–6 hours**, followed by roasting at **150–180 °C** in a drum roaster or fluidized bed roaster.

### Step 3 — Primary Processing (Grinding / Milling)
- **Why?** Particle size reduction increases surface area → improves hydration kinetics during reconstitution.
- **How?** Hammer mill, pin mill, or attrition mill to a target fineness of **80–100 mesh (BSS)**.

### Step 4 — Wet Processing (Optional — for batter-based mixes)
- **Why?** For idli/dosa pre-mixes requiring pre-fermented character, the slurry is fermented before drying to develop lactic acid and CO₂.
- **How?** Wet grinding → natural fermentation (12–18 h at 30 °C) → spray drying or roller drying.

### Step 5 — Drying (Critical for Shelf Stability)
- **Why?** Water removal to <10% ensures microbial safety and long shelf life.
- **How?**
  - **Spray drying** — for fine powders; inlet **150–200 °C**, outlet **80–100 °C**
  - **Roller drying (Drum drying)** — for pre-gelatinized starch-based mixes
  - **Fluidized bed drying** — uniform, fast
  - **Extrusion cooking + drying** — for RTE snack pre-mixes

### Step 6 — Blending & Homogenization
- **Why?** Each ingredient must be uniformly distributed so every spoonful contains the right ratio.
- **How?** Ribbon blender, V-blender, or double-cone blender for **15–30 minutes**.

### Step 7 — Functional Additive Incorporation
- **Why?** Salt, sugar, leavening agents (baking powder), emulsifiers (lecithin), flavors (vanillin), antioxidants (BHA/BHT), and stabilizers (Xanthan gum, Guar gum) are added.
- **How?** Added as fine powders at the blending stage, often pre-sifted through a **60-mesh sieve**.

### Step 8 — Packaging
- **Why?** Protect from moisture, oxygen, light, and pests.
- **How?** Multi-layer packaging — typically **LDPE / Aluminum foil / PET** laminate, often nitrogen-flushed to displace oxygen.

---

## 2.2 Functional Role of Each Ingredient (The "Why" Behind Each Component)

| Ingredient Category | Functional Role | Common Examples |
|---------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Cereal Flour** (Base) | Bulk, texture, starch source | Rice, wheat, ragi, oats |
| **Legume Flour** | Protein fortification, viscoelasticity | Urad dal, moong dal |
| **Leavening Agent** | CO₂ generation → porous texture | Baking soda, baking powder (NaHCO₃ + acidulant) |
| **Salt (NaCl)** | Flavor enhancer, gluten modifier | Refined iodized salt |
| **Sugar** | Sweetness, browning (Maillard), texture | Sucrose, dextrose |
| **Emulsifier** | Uniform fat dispersion | Lecithin, Mono- & diglycerides |
| **Stabilizer / Gum** | Viscosity control, mouthfeel | Xanthan gum, CMC, Guar gum |
| **Acidulant** | pH control, flavor, leavening activation | Citric acid, malic acid |
