| **Flavor / Color** | Sensory appeal | Vanillin, turmeric, paprika |
| **Anti-caking agent** | Free-flowing powder | Calcium silicate (≤ 2 %) |
| **Preservative** | Shelf-life extension | Sorbic acid (≤ 0.1 %), BHA/BHT |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

The following formulas govern pre-mix formulation, reconstitution, and quality assessment. Memorize these for the KTU exam.

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Significance |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $M_{\text{wb}} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | $W_w$ = wet weight; $W_d$ = dry weight; in grams | Measures water content; target ≤ 6–10% |
| **Water Activity** | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | $p$ = vapor pressure of food; $p_0$ = pure water vapor pressure | Microbial growth threshold: $a_w < 0.6$ |
| **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_b}$ | $m$ = mass (g); $V_b$ = bulk volume (cm³) | Packaging design; reconstitution |
| **Reconstitution Ratio** | $R_r = \dfrac{V_{\text{water}}}{M_{\text{mix}}}$ | $V_{\text{water}}$ = water (mL); $M_{\text{mix}}$ = mix (g) | Standardization of consumer instructions |
| **Porosity (ε)** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | $\rho_b$ = bulk; $\rho_t$ = true density | Predicts rehydration behavior |
| **Protein Content (Kjeldahl)** | $\%\,P = \dfrac{V_{\text{HCl}} \times N \times 1.4 \times 100}{W}$ | $V$ = titrant volume; $N$ = normality; $W$ = sample weight | Nutritional labelling |
| **Standard Plate Count (SPC)** | $\text{SPC} = \dfrac{\text{Colonies counted}}{\text{Volume plated} \times \text{Dilution factor}}$ | CFU/g | Microbial quality |
| **Pasteurization Lethality** | $F = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\frac{T - T_{\text{ref}}}{z}} dt$ | $T$ = temperature; $z$ = 7 °C for most pathogens | Heat process validation |
| **Spray Drying Yield** | $\eta = \dfrac{M_{\text{powder}}}{M_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Mass of powder vs. mass of liquid feed | Process efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The **reconstitution ratio** is the single most consumer-facing metric. For idli mix, it is typically **1 : 1.25 to 1 : 1.5** (mix : water by volume). For gulab jamun mix, it is **1 : 0.4** (mix : water/milk).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pre-mixes are not just consumer products — they solve **major industrial problems**:

1. **Food Security** — Long shelf-life foods can be stockpiled for disaster relief (e.g., WFP's "Super Cereal Plus").
2. **Urbanization & Working Population** — India's urban workforce spends 40% less time cooking than 1990s (NSSO data). Pre-mixes fit the modern lifestyle.
3. **Export Potential** — Indian diaspora across the world demands idli/dosa mix, instant chai, gulab jamun mix. Companies like **MTR, Haldiram's, Gits** export globally.
4. **Reduction of Post-Harvest Losses** — Converts perishable ingredients into stable powders at the source.
5. **Standardization** — Every batch tastes identical, eliminating the variability of traditional home cooking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, KTU-board-style derivations** of key pre-mix calculations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Standardization of Moisture Content to Shelf-Stable Levels

**Problem Statement:**
A freshly ground idli batter has **52% moisture (wet basis)** and the manufacturer wants to convert it into a dry instant pre-mix with **7% moisture (wet basis)**. If **1000 kg of wet batter** is fed to a spray dryer, how much water must be removed, and what is the **final powder yield**?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Initial moisture: $M_i = 52\%$
- Final moisture: $M_f = 7\%$
- Initial mass: $W_i = 1000\,\text{kg}$

**Step 1 — Compute the dry solids (which never change)**

The dry matter mass is conserved during drying:

$$
M_{\text{dry}} = W_i \times \frac{100 - M_i}{100}
$$

$$
M_{\text{dry}} = 1000 \times \frac{100 - 52}{100} = 1000 \times 0.48 = 480\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Express final moisture content as dry basis (for clarity)**

The moisture on dry basis (mdb) is:

$$
M_{\text{db}} = \frac{M_f}{100 - M_f} \times 100 = \frac{7}{93} \times 100 = 7.53\%
$$

This is the same as saying that for every **100 kg of dry matter**, the final powder contains **7.53 kg of water**.

**Step 3 — Compute the final powder mass**

Using the conservation of dry solids:

$$
W_f = M_{\text{dry}} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_f}
$$

$$
W_f = 480 \times \frac{100}{100 - 7} = 480 \times \frac{100}{93} = 516.13\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the water to be removed**

$$
W_{\text{water}} = W_i - W_f = 1000 - 516.13 = 483.87\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the dryer yield (mass balance closure)**

$$
\eta = \frac{W_f}{W_i} \times 100 = \frac{516.13}{1000} \times 100 = 51.61\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** To produce 516.13 kg of instant idli mix (7% moisture) from 1000 kg of wet batter, **483.87 kg of water must be removed**, yielding a **51.61% dryer recovery**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reconstitution Ratio Calculation for a Consumer Recipe

**Problem Statement:**
A gulab jamun pre-mix has the following label instructions: *"Mix 200 g of powder with 80 mL of milk. Make 12 uniform balls."* The standard serving size in the FSSAI reference is **2 balls per person**. Determine:
- (i) The mix-to-liquid ratio
- (ii) Number of servings per packet
- (iii) Mass per ball

### Solution

**Step 1 — Mix-to-liquid ratio**

$$
R_r = \frac{V_{\text{liquid}}}{M_{\text{mix}}} = \frac{80\,\text{mL}}{200\,\text{g}} = 0.40\,\text{mL/g}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of servings per packet**

$$
\text{Servings} = \frac{N_{\text{balls}}}{S_{\text{per serving}}} = \frac{12}{2} = 6\,\text{servings}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass per ball**

Total dough mass = $200\,\text{g (powder)} + 80\,\text{mL} \approx 280\,\text{g}$

$$
\text{Mass per ball} = \frac{280}{12} = 23.33\,\text{g/ball}
$$

**Step 4 — Servings in kcal (bonus — often asked in KTU)**

If average caloric density of gulab jamun mix ≈ **420 kcal/100 g** dough:

$$
\text{kcal per serving} = 2 \times 23.33 \times \frac{420}{100} \approx 196\,\text{kcal}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Formulation of an Idli Mix (Cost Optimization by Pearson's Square)

**Problem Statement:**
Design an idli pre-mix such that the final protein content is **8% (dry basis)** using **Rice flour** (7% protein) and **Urad dal flour** (24% protein). Compute the proportion of each.

### Solution Using Pearson's Square

Let $R$ = part of rice, $U$ = part of urad dal.

The protein content of the mixture is the weighted average:

$$
\frac{7R + 24U}{R + U} = 8
$$

**Step 1 — Cross-multiplication**

$$
7R + 24U = 8R + 8U
$$

$$
24U - 8U = 8R - 7R
$$

$$
16U = R \implies R = 16U
$$

**Step 2 — Express as ratio**

If $U = 1$, then $R = 16$

$$
\text{Rice : Urad dal} = 16 : 1
$$

**Step 3 — Verification**

$$
\text{Protein} = \frac{(16 \times 7) + (1 \times 24)}{16 + 1} = \frac{112 + 24}{17} = \frac{136}{17} = 8.0\%
$$

✅ Verified.

> **Practical Formulation (typical commercial idli mix):**
> Rice : Urad dal = **4 : 1 to 5 : 1** by weight (not 16:1) because commercial formulations also prioritize texture and fermentation performance over pure protein matching. Salt ≈ 1.5%, Fenugreek ≈ 0.5%, Baking soda ≈ 0.3%.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Spray Drying Energy Requirement

**Problem Statement:**
Estimate the **theoretical energy** required to dry **1000 kg/h** of idli batter from 52% to 7% moisture, assuming the latent heat of vaporization is **2260 kJ/kg**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Water to be removed per hour**

Water in wet batter = $1000 \times 0.52 = 520\,\text{kg/h}$

Water in final powder = $W_f \times 0.07$ (where $W_f = 516.13\,\text{kg/h}$ as derived earlier) ≈ $36.13\,\text{kg/h}$

$$
W_{\text{water, removed}} = 520 - 36.13 = 483.87\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy needed for vaporization**

$$
E = m_{\text{water}} \times L_v
$$

$$
E = 483.87\,\text{kg/h} \times 2260\,\text{kJ/kg} = 1{,}093{,}546\,\text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kW**

$$
\text{Power} = \frac{1{,}093{,}546}{3600} = 303.76\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for dryer efficiency (typically 40–60% for spray dryers)**

$$
\text{Actual power} = \frac{303.76}{0.5} \approx 607.5\,\text{kW}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Drying is the **most energy-intensive** step in pre-mix manufacturing, often accounting for **60–70% of the plant's total energy bill**. This justifies investment in **multiple-effect evaporators** or **heat recovery systems**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Drying Technologies

| Parameter | Spray Drying | Drum (Roller) Drying | Fluidized Bed Drying | Extrusion + Drying |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Inlet Temp (°C)** | 150–200 | 130–160 | 60–120 | 120–180 |
| **Particle Form** | Spherical, 20–200 µm | Flakes, 50–500 µm | Granular | Porous, expanded |
| **Suitability for Idli/Dosa** | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good | ⚠ Limited | ⚠ Causes starch damage |
| **Capital Cost** | High | Medium | Medium | Low-Medium |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Low (40–50%) | High (75–85%) | High (65–75%) | Medium (55–65%) |
| **Product Quality** | High (soluble) | Altered starch (pre-gelatinized) | Uniform | Cooked, porous |
| **Best For** | Beverages, milk powder | Idli mix, weaning foods | Granulated mixes | RTE, snack powders |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Process Flow Diagram of Instant Pre-Mix Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receiving] --> B[Cleaning and Destoning]
    B --> C[Pre-Conditioning: Soaking or Roasting]
    C --> D{Process Pathway}
    D -->|Wet Route| E[Wet Grinding]
    D -->|Dry Route| F[Dry Milling]
    E --> G[Fermentation 12-18 hr at 30 C]
    F --> G2[Sieving to 80-100 mesh]
    G --> H[Pasteurization 72 C for 15 sec]
    H --> I[Drying: Spray or Drum Dryer]
    G2 --> I
    I --> J[Final Moisture less than 10 percent]
    J --> K[Blending in Ribbon Blender]
    K --> L[Addition of Functional Additives Salt, Leavening, Gum, Flavor]
    L --> M[Quality Control Lab Testing]
    M -->|Pass| N[Nitrogen Flushing]
    M -->|Fail| Z[Rework or Reject]
    N --> O[Multi-layer Packaging LDPE Alu Foil PET]
    O --> P[Metal Detection and X-Ray]
    P --> Q[Carton Packing and Storage]
    Q --> R[Distribution to Market]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow proceeds left → right. Notice the **decision point D** (wet vs. dry route) — this is a classic KTU exam question: *"Differentiate between the wet and dry route for pre-mix manufacture."* Wet route is used for fermented products (idli/dosa), dry route for non-fermented (upma, halwa).

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Reconstitution at the Consumer's Kitchen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Pre-mix Powder]
        A2[Water or Milk]
    end

    subgraph ACTION
        B1[Measured Mixing 1:1.25 ratio]
        B2[Resting 5 minutes]
        B3[Heat Treatment Steaming Frying]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        C1[Soft Fluffy Idli]
        C2[Crispy Dosa]
        C3[Soft Pongal]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
```

---

## 4.3 Quality Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QC[Quality Control Hub]
    QC --> P1[Physical Tests: Moisture, Particle Size, Bulk Density]
    QC --> P2[Chemical Tests: Protein, Fat, Ash, pH]
    QC --> P3[Microbiological Tests: TPC, Yeast, Mould, Coliforms]
    QC --> P4[Sensory Tests: Appearance, Texture, Taste, Aroma]
    QC --> P5[Shelf-life Studies: Accelerated at 45 C]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Question:** *"List the quality parameters evaluated for an instant idli mix and state their FSSAI/BIS permissible limits."* — Use this exact 5-pillar framework in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define instant food pre-mixes. List any four examples with their reconstitution ratios."**

#### Model Answer:
**Definition:** Instant food pre-mixes are dry, ready-to-cook formulations in which all ingredients are pre-blended in the correct proportion, requiring only the addition of water/milk and minimal processing by the consumer to obtain the finished product.

| Pre-Mix | Reconstitution Ratio (Mix : Water) |
|---------|-------------------------------------|
| Idli Mix | 1 : 1.25 to 1 : 1.5 |
| Dosa Mix | 1 : 1.5 to 1 : 2 |
| Gulab Jamun Mix | 1 : 0.4 (with milk) |
| Upma Mix | 1 : 2 |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 for examples with ratios)** ✅

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the role of leavening agents, emulsifiers, and stabilizers in instant food pre-mixes with examples."**

#### Model Answer:
- **Leavening Agents** (e.g., **sodium bicarbonate**, **baking powder**): Generate CO₂ gas on hydration and heating, producing porous, fluffy texture in idli, cake, and pancake mixes. (1 mark)
- **Emulsifiers** (e.g., **lecithin**, **mono- and diglycerides**): Enable uniform dispersion of fat in the powder and stabilize air cells during batter formation, improving cake volume and texture. (1 mark)
- **Stabilizers** (e.g., **xanthan gum**, **guar gum**, **CMC**): Increase viscosity of the reconstituted batter, prevent starch retrogradation, and improve mouthfeel and shelf-life. (1 mark)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two choices (A or B). Each choice has sub-parts **a (7 marks)** and **b (7 marks)**.

---

### ✍️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **general manufacturing process** of an **instant idli pre-mix** in detail. Include the role of each unit operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **functional role of major ingredients** used in idli mix and explain how the formulation can be standardized using **Pearson's Square method** for an 8% protein target. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Raw Material Procurement & Cleaning** (1 mark)
   - Rice, urad dal, fenugreek seeds cleaned via destoner, magnetic separator, air classifier.

2. **Soaking** (1 mark)
   - Rice soaked 4–6 hr; urad dal + fenugreek soaked 2–4 hr at ambient temperature.

3. **Grinding & Fermentation** (1.5 marks)
   - Wet grinding to fine batter (60–80 µm).
   - Fermentation for 12–18 hr at 30 °C → lactic acid bacteria lower pH from ~6.0 to ~4.5; CO₂ generation.

4. **Drying** (1.5 marks)
   - Spray drying (inlet 160–180 °C, outlet 85 °C) or drum drying (3 atm steam).
   - Final moisture **< 8%**, water activity **< 0.6**.

5. **Sieving, Blending & Additive Addition** (1 mark)
   - Sieved through 100-mesh; salt, sodium bicarbonate, preservative mixed in ribbon blender for 20 min.

6. **Packaging** (1 mark)
   - Nitrogen-flushed **LDPE / Al-foil / PET** laminate pouches.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Functional Roles (4 marks):**
| Ingredient | Role |
|------------|------|
| Rice flour | Bulk, starch source, structure |
| Urad dal flour | Protein, viscoelasticity, fermentation substrate |
| Fenugreek | Flavour, lactic acid bacteria stimulant |
| Salt | Flavour, fermentation regulator |
| Baking soda | pH buffer, leavening |
| Calcium silicate | Anti-caking agent |

**Pearson's Square Calculation (3 marks):**

Target protein = 8%; Rice = 7%; Urad = 24%

Parts: $24 - 8 = 16$ (rice); $8 - 7 = 1$ (urad)

$$
\boxed{\text{Rice : Urad dal} = 16 : 1}
$$

*Verification:*

$$
\%\,P = \frac{16 \times 7 + 1 \times 24}{17} = \frac{136}{17} = 8.0\% \quad ✓
$$

---

### ✍️ QUESTION B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** What are the **principles of drying** used in instant pre-mix manufacture? Compare **spray drying, drum drying, and fluidized bed drying** in tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet batter of **1500 kg** with **55% moisture (wet basis)** is spray-dried to **6% moisture (wet basis)**. Calculate: **(i)** water removed, **(ii)** final powder yield, **(iii)** theoretical energy required for drying (Latent heat = 2260 kJ/kg). **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principles of Drying (3 marks):**

Drying is a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer** process where:
- **Heat** from hot air is transferred to the wet product.
- **Moisture** migrates from the interior of the product to its surface, then evaporates into the air.
- The driving force is the **partial pressure gradient of water vapor** between the product surface and the bulk air.

**Three stages of drying:**
1. **Constant rate period** — surface moisture evaporates (rate depends on air conditions).
2. **Falling rate period** — internal moisture migration becomes rate-limiting.
3. **Equilibrium stage** — moisture in product equals moisture in air (no net drying).

**Comparison Table (4 marks):** Refer to the **Analytical Matrix** in **Section 3.5** above.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $W_i = 1500\,\text{kg}$, $M_i = 55\%$, $M_f = 6\%$

**(i) Dry solids mass:**

$$
M_{\text{dry}} = 1500 \times \frac{45}{100} = 675\,\text{kg}
$$

**(ii) Final powder mass:**

$$
W_f = 675 \times \frac{100}{100 - 6} = 675 \times \frac{100}{94} = 718.09\,\text{kg}
$$

**(iii) Water removed:**

$$
W_{\text{water}} = W_i - W_f = 1500 - 718.09 = 781.91\,\text{kg}
$$

**(iv) Theoretical energy:**

$$
E = m_{\text{water}} \times L_v = 781.91 \times 2260 = 1{,}767{,}116.6\,\text{kJ}
$$

**(v) Final yield:**

$$
\eta = \frac{718.09}{1500} \times 100 = 47.87\%
$$

> **Final Answer:** Water removed = **781.91 kg**, Powder yield = **718.09 kg (47.87%)**, Energy = **1.77 × 10⁶ kJ**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing wet basis vs. dry basis** — Always specify which one. The 55% vs. 45% distinction matters! (–2 marks if wrong)
> 2. **Pearson's Square — using wrong signs** — Put the higher protein on the *top-left* and lower protein on *bottom-left* of the square. Inverting causes wrong ratio. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting fermentation role** in idli pre-mix answers — examiner specifically looks for **lactic acid bacteria, pH drop, CO₂ generation**. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Listing ingredients without functions** — Writing "salt" alone gets 0; writing "salt — flavour enhancer, regulates fermentation, modulates gluten" gets full marks.
> 5. **Missing the FSSAI/BIS limits** — When asked about shelf life or moisture, always cite the regulation. E.g., *"Moisture ≤ 10% as per FSSAI (2011)"*.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Pre-mixes = ready-to-cook dry blends → reconstitute with water/milk.
- ✅ **Key metrics to memorize:** Moisture ≤ 10%, aₐ ≤ 0.6, Particle size 80–100 mesh, Shelf life 6–18 months.
- ✅ **Manufacturing steps:** Cleaning → Pre-conditioning → Grinding → Fermentation (optional) → Drying → Blending → Additives → Packaging.
- ✅ **Drying is the most energy-intensive step** — 60–70% of plant energy.
- ✅ **Pearson's Square** is THE formulation tool for binary blends.
- ✅ **Leavening = CO₂; Emulsifier = uniform fat; Stabilizer = viscosity.**
- ✅ **Always state FSSAI/BIS limits** in exam answers for full marks.
- ✅ **Cite latent heat** (2260 kJ/kg) for any drying energy calculation.
- ✅ **Reconstitution ratio** is the single most consumer-visible parameter — must be tested in QA/QC.
- ✅ **Examples:** Idli mix, Dosa mix, Pongal mix, Gulab jamun mix, Cake mix, Soup mix, Health drink mix.
- ✅ **Packaging:** Multi-layer laminate (LDPE + Al-foil + PET), often nitrogen-flushed.
- ✅ **FSSAI classifies them as Ready-to-Cook (RTC)** products.
- ✅ **Pearson's Square trick:** The diagonal difference = part of the opposite ingredient.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shelf-Life and Quality Characteristics of Snack Foods
Regulations and Standards of Snack Foods
Permitted Additives in Snack Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT756 - SNACK FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Permitted Additives in Snack Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Food Additive** is defined (per the **Codex Alimentarius**, **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) Act, 2006**, and **CFR Title 21, FDA**) as:

> Any substance which is not normally consumed as a food by itself, and is not normally used as a typical ingredient of the food, whether or not it has nutritive value, the intentional addition of which to food for a technological (including organoleptic) purpose in the manufacture, processing, preparation, treatment, packing, packaging, transport or holding of such food results, or may be reasonably expected to result (directly or indirectly), in it or its by-products becoming a component of or otherwise affecting the characteristics of such food.

For the **snack food industry**, additives are intentional ingredients added in **small, controlled, legally permitted quantities** to enhance shelf life, texture, color, flavor, safety, and consumer appeal of products such as potato chips, extruded snacks (kurkure/chips), namkeen, popcorn, biscuits, cookies, and wafers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The module emphasizes *which additives are permitted, in what quantities, under which regulatory body, and for what technological function*. The exam commonly asks students to justify the use of an additive by linking it to a specific **technological function** in a specific snack matrix.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake (or frying chips) at home. You add a pinch of salt — not for taste, but to strengthen the gluten structure. You add a spoon of vinegar — not to make it sour, but to react with baking soda to release CO₂ (a **leavening agent** function). You add a drop of yellow color — not for nutrition, but for visual appeal.

Now scale this to an industry. A snack manufacturer cannot use just *any* substance; the law (FSSAI) maintains a "**whitelist**" of substances deemed safe at specified levels, called the **INS (International Numbering System) Numbers** under Codex. This whitelist is your *guest list* for a wedding — only listed guests can enter, and even they have a seating limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> A *food additive* is any intentionally added substance in a snack that is **NOT** a main ingredient (flour, potato, oil) and **NOT** a contaminant. It serves a *technological purpose* (preservation, color, texture, etc.) and is regulated by law with a maximum permitted limit (often expressed in **mg/kg** or **ppm (parts per million) = mg/kg**).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold Highlights)

- **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake):** Expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day**. Lifetime safe intake.
- **INS Number:** International Numbering System assigned by Codex (e.g., INS 300 = Ascorbic acid).
- **Maximum Permitted Level (MPL):** Typically in **mg/kg** (ppm) or **% w/w** of the finished product.
- **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice):** Quantum satis (as much as needed, but no more than necessary).
- **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe):** U.S. FDA designation.
- **NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level):** mg/kg body weight/day — used to derive ADI by applying a safety factor (usually **100×**).
- **JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives):** The international body that evaluates additive safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relationship to remember (for derivations):**
>
> $$\text{ADI} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Safety Factor (SF)}}$$
>
> Typically SF = **100** (10 for animal-to-human extrapolation × 10 for human variability).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Permitted Additives Used in Snack Foods

Food additives are classified based on their **technological function** (per Codex/FSSAI). Below is the structured bulleted logic behind each class.

#### A. Antioxidants (INS 300–INS 322 group)
- **Why:** Snack oils (palmolein, sunflower, rice bran) undergo **oxidative rancidity** on storage. Heat, light, and metals (Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺) accelerate this via **peroxide** formation.
- **How:** Antioxidants donate a hydrogen atom to free radicals, terminating the chain reaction, OR they chelate pro-oxidant metals.
- **Common examples in chips/namkeen:** BHA (INS 320), BHT (INS 321), TBHQ (INS 319), Ascorbic acid (INS 300), Ascorbyl palmitate (INS 304).
- **Permitted limits (FSSAI):** BHA/BHT — individually or in combination — **200 mg/kg** of fat or oil content.

#### B. Preservatives (Antimicrobials)
- **Why:** Snacks (especially baked/half-humid namkeen) are vulnerable to mold, yeast, and bacterial growth. Water Activity (a_w) above ~0.6 invites microbial risk.
- **How:** They disrupt microbial cell walls, denature proteins, or inhibit enzyme systems.
- **Common examples:** Sodium benzoate (INS 211), Potassium sorbate (INS 202), Sorbic acid (INS 200), Calcium propionate (INS 282), Nitrites/Nitrates (INS 249–252, used in cured meat snacks).
- **Permitted limits:** Sodium benzoate in sauces/dips — **750 mg/kg**; Sorbates in cheese/coatings — up to **1000 mg/kg**.

#### C. Emulsifiers & Stabilizers
- **Why:** Snack seasonings, cheese coatings, and chocolate enrobing need uniform oil-water dispersion.
- **How:** Emulsifiers are **amphiphilic** — they reduce interfacial tension (HLB concept).
- **Common examples:** Lecithin (INS 322), Mono- and diglycerides (INS 471), Polysorbate 80 (INS 433), DATEM (INS 472e), Sodium stearoyl lactylate (SSL, INS 481).
- **Permitted limits:** Generally **GMP (quantum satis)** for most emulsifiers in snack coatings.

#### D. Anticaking Agents
- **Why:** Salt, cheese powder, and spice blends tend to clump due to moisture absorption.
- **How:** They coat particles, absorb moisture, or improve flowability by reducing inter-particle adhesion.
- **Common examples:** Silicon dioxide (INS 551), Calcium silicate (INS 552), Sodium aluminosilicate (INS 554), Magnesium carbonate (INS 504).
- **Permitted limits:** Up to **2%** in salt; **10 g/kg** in cheese powder (FSSAI).

#### E. Coloring Agents
- **Why:** Snacks must look visually appealing. Frying causes browning (Maillard), but manufacturers standardize color.
- **How:** Dyes/pigments absorb specific wavelengths; lakes are dyes adsorbed on alumina hydrate.
- **Types:**
  - **Natural:** Caramel (INS 150a–d), Annatto (INS 160b), Paprika extract (INS 160c), Curcumin (INS 100), Beetroot red (INS 162), Chlorophyll (INS 140).
  - **Synthetic:** Sunset Yellow FCF (INS 110), Tartrazine (INS 102), Allura Red (INS 129), Brilliant Blue (INS 133), Indigo Carmine (INS 132).
- **Permitted limits:** **100–300 mg/kg** of finished snack (depending on dye and product).

#### F. Flavor Enhancers
- **Why:** Boost savory (umami) or sweet notes without adding bulk.
- **How:** Act on taste receptors (e.g., glutamate receptors).
- **Common examples:** Monosodium glutamate – MSG (INS 621), Disodium 5'-inosinate (INS 631), Disodium 5'-guanylate (INS 627).
- **Permitted limits:** MSG — quantum satis (FSSAI allows up to 1% in some categories; global ADI = **not specified** by JECFA).

#### G. Sequestrants / Chelating Agents
- **Why:** Metal ions (Fe, Cu) catalyze oxidation.
- **How:** Form stable complexes (chelates) with metals.
- **Common examples:** EDTA (INS 386), Citric acid (INS 330), Sodium citrate (INS 331).

#### H. Bulking, Thickening & Anti-sticking Agents
- **Why:** Control viscosity of seasoning slurries, prevent stickiness in extruded puffs.
- **Common examples:** Modified starch (INS 1400 series), Xanthan gum (INS 415), Guar gum (INS 412), Carrageenan (INS 407), MCC (INS 460).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all the high-yield formulas, ratios, and constant values that are frequently asked in KTU ESE questions on this module.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake) | $\text{ADI} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}}$ | mg/kg bw/day | SF typically = **100** |
| Safety Factor Determination | $\text{SF} = \text{AF}_\text{interspecies} \times \text{AF}_\text{intraspecies}$ | dimensionless | 10 × 10 standard |
| mg/kg to ppm conversion | $1\ \text{mg/kg} = 1\ \text{ppm}$ | mg/kg = ppm | Direct equivalence |
| % w/w to mg/kg | $\%\ \text{w/w} \times 10{,}000 = \text{mg/kg}$ | — | 1% = **10,000 mg/kg** |
| HLB Required (for oil-in-water emulsions in seasoning) | $\text{HLB}_\text{required} = 8\text{–}16$ | dimensionless | Used to select emulsifier blend |
| Approximate shelf-life via antioxidants | $\text{IP (peroxide value)} = \dfrac{(V_\text{sam} - V_\text{blank}) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | meq O₂/kg oil | Endpoint of antioxidant efficacy |
| Daily Intake Check | $\text{EI} = \text{C} \times \text{F}\ \text{(kg bw/day)}$ | mg/kg bw/day | C = additive concentration; EI should be ≤ ADI |
| Margin of Safety (MOS) | $\text{MOS} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Actual Intake}}$ | dimensionless | MOS > 100 is considered safe |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the snack industry, additive selection is **not arbitrary**. A product development scientist must:

1. Identify the **technological need** (e.g., prevent oil oxidation for 6-month shelf life).
2. Choose an **FSSAI-permitted additive** with the correct INS number.
3. Verify **MPL** (maximum permitted level) for that food category.
4. Confirm the **EI (Estimated Intake)** is below the **ADI** for the target population.
5. Conduct **stability and sensory trials** to confirm functionality.

This same workflow applies globally — Codex-based regulations in EU (E-numbers), USA (CFR Title 21), India (FSSAI Schedules 1–14 of Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1: Estimating the Safe Level of an Additive (BHA in Frying Oil)

**Problem Statement:** A snack manufacturer adds BHA to frying oil at the FSSAI-permitted limit of 200 mg/kg fat. The finished potato chip contains 35% oil (w/w). The average consumer (target demographic) eats 30 g of chips per day, weighing 60 kg. Verify that this consumption stays below the JECFA-recommended ADI for BHA (which is 0.5 mg/kg bw/day).

**Step 1 — Determine the concentration of BHA in the finished chip.**

$$C_\text{chip} = C_\text{oil} \times \text{Fat Fraction} = 200\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg oil}} \times 0.35$$

$$C_\text{chip} = 70\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg chip}} = 70\ \text{ppm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute BHA intake per day.**

$$\text{Intake}_\text{absolute} = C_\text{chip} \times \text{Daily Consumption} = 70\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \times 0.030\ \text{kg}$$

$$\text{Intake}_\text{absolute} = 2.10\ \text{mg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to body weight (this is the Estimated Daily Intake, EDI).**

$$\text{EDI} = \frac{\text{Intake}_\text{absolute}}{\text{Body Weight}} = \frac{2.10\ \text{mg/day}}{60\ \text{kg}}$$

$$\text{EDI} = 0.035\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg bw}\cdot\text{day}}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ADI and compute the Margin of Safety (MOS).**

$$\text{MOS} = \frac{\text{ADI}}{\text{EDI}} = \frac{0.5}{0.035} = 14.29$$

Wait — a MOS of 14.29 appears low. **However, ADI already incorporates a safety factor of 100**, so an EDI well below ADI is acceptable. The relevant KTU check is:

$$\text{EDI} = 0.035\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg bw}\cdot\text{day}} \ll \text{ADI} = 0.5\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg bw}\cdot\text{day}}$$

**Conclusion:** The level is safe. The actual NOAEL would be:

$$\text{NOAEL} = \text{ADI} \times \text{SF} = 0.5 \times 100 = 50\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg bw}\cdot\text{day}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show units at every step, and explicitly state the regulatory body (FSSAI / JECFA / Codex) and the schedule/clause number when quoting a permitted limit.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2: ADI Derivation from Animal NOAEL

**Scenario:** A 90-day subchronic rat study on sodium benzoate (INS 211) yielded a NOAEL of 1000 mg/kg bw/day. Derive the ADI.

**Step 1 — Identify the safety factor components.**

- Interspecies uncertainty factor (animal to human): $\text{AF}_1 = 10$
- Intraspecies uncertainty factor (human variability): $\text{AF}_2 = 10$

$$\text{SF} = \text{AF}_1 \times \text{AF}_2 = 10 \times 10 = 100$$

**Step 2 — Compute ADI.**

$$\text{ADI} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}} = \frac{1000\ \text{mg/kg bw/day}}{100}$$

$$\text{ADI} = 10\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg bw}\cdot\text{day}}$$

The JECFA-accepted ADI for sodium benzoate is **0–5 mg/kg bw/day** (slightly more conservative), which validates the methodology.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Permitted Additives in Specific Snack Categories

| Snack Category | Critical Quality Defect | Additive Solution | INS Number | FSSAI Permitted Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Potato chips / Tortilla chips | Oxidative rancidity of frying oil | BHA + BHT blend or TBHQ | 320, 321, 319 | **200 mg/kg** of fat (individually/combined) |
| Extruded puffs (kurkure) | Lack of uniform color | Sunset Yellow FCF | 110 | **100 mg/kg** finished product |
| Cheese-flavored popcorn | Caking of cheese powder | Silicon dioxide | 551 | **10 g/kg** cheese powder |
| Masala namkeen | Microbial spoilage (a_w > 0.7) | Calcium propionate | 282 | **1000 mg/kg** |
| Coated peanuts | Poor seasoning adhesion | Modified starch + Gum Arabic | 1400, 414 | Quantum satis (GMP) |
| Wheat-based savory biscuits | Stale flavor (lipid oxidation) | Lecithin + Ascorbic acid | 322, 300 | GMP / **500 mg/kg** |
| Cereal bars (extruded) | Vitamin degradation | Mixed tocopherols (natural antioxidant) | 306 | **300 mg/kg** |
| Chocolate-coated snacks | Fat bloom (white surface) | Lecithin (emulsifier) | 322 | Quantum satis |
| Wafers (cream-filled) | Stickiness of cream | Sorbitan monostearate | 491 | **1000 mg/kg** |
| Soups / seasoning premixes | Caking due to hygroscopic salt | Calcium silicate | 552 | **2%** in salt |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Calculating Required Antioxidant Dosage

**Problem:** A snack company uses sunflower oil (peroxide value rises from 5 to 25 meq O₂/kg over 30 days at 25°C without antioxidant). They wish to extend the induction period to 90 days. How much TBHQ (INS 319) should be added?

**Step 1 — Identify the standard effective concentration of TBHQ in oils.**

From industry data and FSSAI limits, TBHQ is typically effective at **100–200 mg/kg** of oil. Choose the midpoint:

$$C_\text{TBHQ} = 150\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg oil}}$$

**Step 2 — Verify against FSSAI MPL.**

FSSAI permits TBHQ in fats and oils at **200 mg/kg maximum**. So 150 mg/kg is **permissible**.

**Step 3 — Mass balance for a 1000 kg batch.**

$$\text{Mass of TBHQ} = 150\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \times 1000\ \text{kg} = 150{,}000\ \text{mg} = 150\ \text{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Questions frequently combine a calculation (mass of additive, % w/w, dilution series) with a regulatory check. Always end with: *"This is within the FSSAI-permitted MPL of [X] mg/kg."*

### 3.5 Critical "Why" Reasoning — Linking Additive Function to Snack Defect

> **The "Why" Chain (KTU High-Yield Reasoning Framework):**
>
> 1. **Snack contains oil** → oil is prone to **lipid peroxidation** → causes **rancid off-flavor** and **toxic aldehydes** → consumers reject product.
> 2. **Solution:** add an **antioxidant** (BHA, BHT, TBHQ) → donate hydrogen to **lipid radicals (L•, LOO•)** → **break the chain reaction** → extend **induction period**.
> 3. **Regulatory check:** FSSAI Schedule (Food Products Standards) lists **MPL** → consumer intake must remain **below ADI** → JECFA confirms safety → **product is approved** for market.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Selection Workflow for Snack Additives

This diagram illustrates the **engineering decision tree** that a product developer follows when choosing a permitted additive. It is a **block-level functional architecture** rather than a physical process flow, so it complies with the Mermaid safeguard rules (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    idStart([Start: Snack Product Concept]) --> idNeed{Identify Quality Defect}
    idNeed -- Rancidity --> idAntiox[Select Antioxidant]
    idNeed -- Microbial Spoilage --> idPres[Select Preservative]
    idNeed -- Color Loss --> idCol[Select Colorant]
    idNeed -- Texture Defect --> idEmul[Select Emulsifier or Stabilizer]
    idNeed -- Caking --> idAntiC[Select Anticaking Agent]
    idAntiox --> idFSSAI{Check FSSAI Schedule}
    idPres --> idFSSAI
    idCol --> idFSSAI
    idEmul --> idFSSAI
    idAntiC --> idFSSAI
    idFSSAI -- Permitted at Limit --> idTrial{Pilot Trial and Sensory Evaluation}
    idFSSAI -- Not Permitted --> idAlt[Choose Alternative INS Code]
    idAlt --> idFSSAI
    idTrial -- Pass --> idStab{Stability and Shelf Life Test}
    idTrial -- Fail --> idReform[Reformulate Matrix]
    idReform --> idNeed
    idStab -- Within ADI --> idLabel[Declare on Label with INS Number]
    idStab -- Exceeds ADI --> idReform
    idLabel([End: Commercialized Snack Product])
```


### 4.3 Mechanism Diagram: Antioxidant Action in Frying Oil (Schematic Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    idLipid[Unsaturated Lipid LH] --> idRadical[Forms Lipid Radical L Dot]
    idRadical --> idO2[Reacts with O2]
    idO2 --> idPeroxide[Lipid Peroxide LOO Dot]
    idPeroxide --> idChain[Chain Propagation RH to R Dot]
    idChain --> idRancid[Final Outcome: Rancidity and Off Flavor]
    idAntiox[Antioxidant AH Added] --> idDonate[Donates Hydrogen to LOO Dot]
    idDonate --> idStable[Forms Stable LOH and A Dot]
    idStable --> idBreak[Chain Reaction Terminated]
    idBreak --> idShelf[Extended Shelf Life]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **left branch** (without antioxidant) is the *undesired* chain reaction that leads to rancidity. The **right branch** (with antioxidant AH) shows how the chain is broken, producing a stable, non-reactive antioxidant radical (A•) and a stable lipid alcohol (LOH), thereby preventing further oxidation.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define food additives. List any four classes of permitted additives used in snack foods with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A food additive is any substance (other than the basic food ingredients) intentionally added to food in small quantities for a technological function such as preservation, coloring, emulsification, or improvement of texture, and is regulated under food law (FSSAI, 2006 in India; Codex Alimentarius globally).

Four classes:

1. **Antioxidants** – e.g., BHA (INS 320) – prevent oxidative rancidity in frying oils used for chips.
2. **Preservatives** – e.g., Sodium benzoate (INS 211) – inhibit microbial growth in sauces/dips.
3. **Emulsifiers** – e.g., Lecithin (INS 322) – stabilize oil-water dispersions in chocolate coatings.
4. **Coloring agents** – e.g., Sunset Yellow FCF (INS 110) – impart uniform yellow color to extruded snacks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often write *"Food additives are chemicals"* — this is too vague. Always mention the **technological function** and at least one **regulatory body**. Marks are lost for failing to cite INS numbers.

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is ADI? How is it derived from NOAEL? State the ADI of BHT.**

**Model Answer:**

**ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)** is the amount of a chemical additive that can be ingested daily over a lifetime by a consumer of standard body weight (60 kg) without appreciable health risk, expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day**.

It is derived from the **NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level)** determined in long-term animal toxicology studies, by dividing it by a **safety factor (SF)**.

$$\text{ADI} = \frac{\text{NOAEL (mg/kg bw/day)}}{\text{SF}}$$

The standard safety factor is **100** (10 for interspecies variation × 10 for intraspecies variation).

**ADI of BHT (INS 321):** As evaluated by JECFA, the ADI is **0–0.3 mg/kg body weight/day** (some sources quote 0–0.5 mg/kg bw/day; students should mention the JECFA source).

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) – Option 1

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`**
**(a)** Classify the permitted food additives used in snack foods. Discuss the role of **antioxidants** and **preservatives** in detail, with five examples of each and their INS numbers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A snack manufacturer adds **TBHQ at 150 mg/kg** to its frying oil. The finished potato chips contain **35% oil (w/w)**. The product is targeted at an average consumer weighing **60 kg**, who consumes **30 g of chips per day**. The **ADI of TBHQ is 0.7 mg/kg body weight/day**. Calculate the **Estimated Daily Intake (EDI)** and the **Margin of Safety (MOS)**. Comment on the safety of the formulation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification of Permitted Additives (8 classes recognized by FSSAI/Codex):**

1. Antioxidants
2. Preservatives (antimicrobials)
3. Emulsifiers and Stabilizers
4. Anticaking agents
5. Coloring agents
6. Flavor enhancers
7. Sequestrants (chelating agents)
8. Bulking, thickening, and anti-sticking agents

**Antioxidants (5 examples with INS):**

| Example | INS | Permitted Limit (FSSAI) |
|---|---|---|
| Butylated Hydroxy Anisole (BHA) | 320 | 200 mg/kg fat |
| Butylated Hydroxy Toluene (BHT) | 321 | 200 mg/kg fat |
| Tert-Butyl Hydroquinone (TBHQ) | 319 | 200 mg/kg fat |
| Ascorbic acid | 300 | GMP (quantum satis) |
| Ascorbyl palmitate | 304 | 500 mg/kg fat |

**Role:** Prevent oxidative rancidity of unsaturated frying oils by donating hydrogen atoms to lipid peroxyl radicals (LOO•), thereby terminating the autoxidation chain reaction. They extend the **induction period**, maintaining crispness, flavor, and nutritional quality of chips, namkeen, and extruded snacks.

**Preservatives (5 examples with INS):**

| Example | INS | Permitted Limit (FSSAI) |
|---|---|---|
| Sodium benzoate | 211 | 750 mg/kg (sauces, pickles) |
| Potassium sorbate | 202 | 1000 mg/kg |
| Sorbic acid | 200 | 1000 mg/kg |
| Calcium propionate | 282 | 1000 mg/kg (bakery, namkeen) |
| Sodium nitrite | 250 | 125 mg/kg (cured meat snacks) |

**Role:** Inhibit microbial growth (bacteria, mold, yeast) in snacks with intermediate water activity (a_w 0.6–0.85), particularly cheese-coated popcorn, masala namkeen, and stuffing for bakery items. They extend microbiological shelf life by disrupting cell membrane integrity or inhibiting key enzymes.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- TBHQ concentration in oil: 150 mg/kg
- Fat fraction in chip: 35% = 0.35
- Daily consumption: 30 g = 0.030 kg
- Body weight: 60 kg
- ADI of TBHQ: 0.7 mg/kg bw/day

**Step 1 — TBHQ concentration in finished chip:**

$$C_\text{chip} = 150\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg oil}} \times 0.35\ \frac{\text{kg oil}}{\text{kg chip}} = 52.5\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg chip}}$$

**Step 2 — Absolute daily intake:**

$$I_\text{abs} = 52.5\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \times 0.030\ \text{kg} = 1.575\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{day}}$$

**Step 3 — Estimated Daily Intake (EDI):**

$$\text{EDI} = \frac{1.575\ \text{mg/day}}{60\ \text{kg}} = 0.02625\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg bw}\cdot\text{day}}$$

**Step 4 — Margin of Safety (MOS):**

$$\text{MOS} = \frac{\text{ADI}}{\text{EDI}} = \frac{0.7}{0.02625} = 26.67$$

**Step 5 — Regulatory check:**

$$\text{Concentration in oil} = 150\ \text{mg/kg} \leq \text{FSSAI MPL} = 200\ \text{mg/kg} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The formulation is **safe** because (i) the additive level is within the FSSAI-permitted limit, and (ii) the EDI of 0.026 mg/kg bw/day is far below the ADI of 0.7 mg/kg bw/day, leaving a comfortable margin of safety (MOS ≈ 26.7).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) – Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`**
**(a)** Explain the **INS numbering system**. List the INS numbers, common name, technological function, and FSSAI-permitted limit for any **seven permitted additives** used in snack foods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **regulatory framework** for food additives in India under the **FSSAI Act, 2006**, including the role of **JECFA**, **Codex Alimentarius**, **FEMA (Food Enzymes and Additives Manufacturers Association)**, and the labeling requirements. Why are additives declared on the label, and what information must be provided? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **International Numbering System (INS)** for food additives was established by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** to provide a uniform, language-independent way to identify additives globally. Each additive receives a unique 3- or 4-digit number. For example, INS 300 = Ascorbic acid. In Europe, the same system is called the **E-number** (e.g., E300 = Ascorbic acid).

**Table of Seven Permitted Additives for Snack Foods:**

| INS | Common Name | Technological Function | FSSAI Permitted Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 320 | BHA (Butylated Hydroxy Anisole) | Antioxidant | 200 mg/kg of fat |
| 211 | Sodium benzoate | Preservative | 750 mg/kg |
| 322 | Lecithin | Emulsifier | GMP (quantum satis) |
| 110 | Sunset Yellow FCF | Synthetic color | 100 mg/kg finished product |
| 551 | Silicon dioxide | Anticaking agent | 2% in salt; 10 g/kg cheese powder |
| 621 | Monosodium glutamate (MSG) | Flavor enhancer | GMP |
| 415 | Xanthan gum | Thickener/stabilizer | GMP |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Regulatory Framework in India:**

1. **FSSAI Act, 2006:** Apex body enforcing food safety. The **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** contain **Schedules 1–14** listing permitted additives with MPLs for each food category.
2. **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC):** International body under FAO/WHO that sets harmonized standards. The **GSFA (General Standard for Food Additives)** lists permitted additives by food category.
3. **JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives):** Scientific body that evaluates safety data (toxicology, NOAEL, ADI) and recommends intake limits.
4. **FEMA (in India context – Food Safety Standards Authority) and BIS:** BIS sets product standards; FSSAI regulates additive use.
5. **FDA (USA reference):** GRAS list for additives; not directly applicable in India but referenced for global trade.

**Why additives are declared on the label:**

- **Consumer right to information** (FSSAI labeling Regulations, 2020).
- **Allergen management** — certain colors (e.g., Sunset Yellow) trigger hyperactivity in some children.
- **Religious/ethical concerns** — animal-derived additives (e.g., lactose, gelatin) must be declared for vegetarian/vegan labeling.
- **Adverse reaction prevention** — e.g., sulfites (INS 220–228) trigger asthma in sensitive individuals.

**Information required on the label (for each additive):**

- Common name or **INS number** (mandatory).
- Function class (e.g., "Antioxidant (INS 320)").
- Source (e.g., "derived from soy" for lecithin) if it has allergen implications.
- For ingredients causing hypersensitivity (e.g., glutamates, benzoates), the phrase **"Contains [additive name]"** must appear.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Missing INS numbers:** Writing "BHA" without "INS 320" loses 1–2 marks. Always pair the additive name with its INS number.
> 2. **No units in calculations:** Writing $C = 150 \times 0.35 = 52.5$ is wrong. Write $52.5\ \text{mg/kg chip}$.
> 3. **Failing to compare with ADI/MPL:** A correct number without a concluding statement comparing to the regulatory limit loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing INS 211 and INS 202:** Sodium benzoate (211) ≠ Potassium sorbate (202). Memorize the difference.
> 5. **Vague roles:** Writing *"antioxidants prevent spoilage"* is incomplete. Specify **oxidative rancidity** and **chain-breaking mechanism**.
> 6. **Skipping the FSSAI Schedule reference:** FSSAI regulations are listed in the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**. Quoting the Schedule number adds marks.
> 7. **Forgetting safety factor rationale:** Always explain *why* SF = 100 (10 animal-to-human + 10 human variability).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Definition:** Food additive = intentionally added substance with a *technological function* (NOT nutritional).
> - **Regulators:** **FSSAI** (India), **Codex/CAC** (international), **JECFA** (safety), **FDA** (US GRAS).
> - **INS Number:** 3–4 digit unique identifier. Memorize the top 15 for snack foods.
> - **Key Antioxidants in chips:** BHA (320), BHT (321), TBHQ (319). Limit = **200 mg/kg fat**.
> - **Key Preservatives in namkeen/dips:** Sodium benzoate (211), Potassium sorbate (202). Limit = **750–1000 mg/kg**.
> - **Key Emulsifier:** Lecithin (322). Used in chocolate coatings, seasoning slurries.
> - **Key Anticaking:** Silicon dioxide (551). Used in salt and cheese powder.
> - **Key Colors:** Sunset Yellow (110) and Tartrazine (102) are synthetic. Annatto (160b) and Caramel (150a) are natural.
> - **ADI formula:** $\text{ADI} = \text{NOAEL} / 100$. Units: **mg/kg bw/day**.
> - **Conversion:** 1% w/w = **10,000 mg/kg** = **10,000 ppm**.
> - **MPL vs ADI:** MPL = max in food; ADI = max daily intake. Both must be satisfied.
> - **Labeling:** Always declare additive by name and/or INS number. Functions must be stated.
> - **Quantum satis (GMP):** Use the minimum amount necessary to achieve the technological effect.
> - **Type of additives for oil-based snacks:** Antioxidant + Emulsifier + (sometimes) Chelator.
> - **Type of additives for cheese-coated snacks:** Anticaking + Preservative + Color + Flavor enhancer.
> - **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always pair (a) descriptive (7 marks) with (b) numerical/regulatory (7 marks).
> - **Watch units, watch INS numbers, watch FSSAI Schedule references.**
